An interactive course · 5 parts · 20 sections

Finance & Economics: Zero to Master

A complete, practical curriculum that takes you from zero knowledge to genuine fluency in how money, markets, companies, and economies actually work — built for someone with a computer-science and data background who wants to work in the financial sector.

No finance background assumed. Full math comfort assumed. Every concept is explained in plain English first, then made precise, then made interactive.

Read

Twenty sections build on each other — start at Section 1 and go in order, or jump to what you need. Hover any dotted term for an instant definition.

Play

Every section has live calculators and simulators. Drag the sliders — intuition comes from seeing numbers move, not from memorizing formulas.

Test

Each section ends with a short quiz that targets the classic misconceptions. Get one wrong and you'll see exactly why.

Track

Hit "Mark complete" at the end of each section — your progress is saved in this browser and shown in the sidebar.

Everything here is for education, not investment advice. Figures use simplified, illustrative numbers unless a real historical event is named.

Part I · Economic Foundations

1. Money and the Time Value of Money

Every price, every loan, every stock valuation, and every retirement plan rests on one idea: a dollar today and a dollar next year are different things with different values. Master that idea here and you will recognize it, wearing different costumes, in every section that follows.

What money actually is

Welcome to the start of the course. Over five parts you will build from raw fundamentals (Part I: money, micro, macro, central banks, governments) through markets and instruments (Part II), company analysis and valuation (Part III), portfolios and risk (Part IV), and finally quant tooling, fintech, and your career map (Part V). Everything compounds — fittingly, that is also the subject of this section.

Before money can have a time value, we should pin down what money is. Imagine an economy with no money: you write code, your neighbor grows wheat. To eat, you need a farmer who happens to want software — economists call this the double coincidence of wants, and it makes barter hopeless at scale. Money solves it by acting as a universal intermediary. Anything that society accepts in that role is money — and it must do three jobs:

  • Medium of exchange — everyone accepts it as payment, so you can sell code for money and buy wheat with money. Two searches instead of one impossible one.
  • Store of value — it holds its worth over time, so you can earn today and spend next month. Fish would fail this test; gold and dollars (mostly) pass.
  • Unit of account — it is the common denominator for prices. Instead of quoting wheat in code-hours and shoes in wheat, everything is quoted in one unit. Think of it as the schema that makes every value in the economy comparable.

Historically, money began as commodity money — cowrie shells, salt, and above all precious metals. The kingdom of Lydia struck standardized coins around 600 BCE; Song-dynasty China circulated paper notes a thousand years ago; and by the 19th century most major currencies were paper claims redeemable for gold under the gold standard. That link was cut in stages and ended for good in 1971, when the US stopped converting dollars to gold.

Since then, all major currencies are fiat money — from the Latin for "let it be done." A dollar is not a claim on anything physical; it is valuable because everyone agrees it is, because the government demands taxes in it (guaranteeing baseline demand), because it is legal tender, and because a central bank manages its scarcity. Fiat is a shared protocol: it works as long as the network of users keeps trusting it, and its integrity is maintained not by cryptography but by institutions — how, exactly, is the story of Section 4.

Common pitfall

"Fiat money is backed by nothing, so it's worthless" is a popular take and a wrong one. Fiat is backed by enforced tax demand, legal status, and managed scarcity. When that management fails — Weimar Germany in 1923, Zimbabwe in 2008 — the store-of-value function collapses first, and prices explode. Scarcity, not substance, is what money requires.

Why money has a time price: interest

Here is the pivotal question of this section: if I borrow $1,000 from you today and repay $1,000 in a year, have you broken even? No — you have lost, three times over. Interest is the price that compensates the lender, and it bundles three distinct charges:

  • Opportunity cost — the term for the best alternative you gave up. Your $1,000 could have been earning a return elsewhere; lending it to me means forgoing that.
  • Inflation — the general rise in prices over time. If prices rise 3%, the $1,000 I return buys about 3% less than the $1,000 you gave me.
  • Default risk — I might not pay you back at all. Lenders charge a risk premium on top of everything else to be paid for that possibility.

So an interest rate is never arbitrary: it is a data-rich price that encodes time preference, expected inflation, and perceived risk. That is why the US government borrows more cheaply than a startup, and why rates jump when inflation expectations do.

There are two ways interest can accumulate on the principal. With simple interest, you earn on the original amount only: $1,000 at 10% simple pays $100 every year — $1,300 after three years. With compound interest, each period's interest joins the principal and starts earning itself: $1,000 becomes $1,100, then $1,210, then $1,331. The gap looks trivial at year three. It is not trivial at year thirty.

Real world: the $24 island

In 1626 the Dutch reportedly bought Manhattan from the Lenape for goods worth about $24. Terrible deal? Compounded at 7% per year for the 400 years since, $24 grows to roughly $13.6 trillion — several times the market value of all Manhattan real estate today. At 5% it grows to only about $7.2 billion. Two lessons: compounding over long horizons produces absurd numbers, and the rate matters enormously.

The compounding machine: FV, the rule of 72, and APR vs APY

Time to formalize. If you invest a future value problem, one line does it all:

FV = PV × (1 + r)n
FV = future value · PV = present value (amount today) · r = rate per period · n = number of periods

Each year multiplies your balance by (1 + r), and exponents stack the multiplications. To a CS graduate this should feel familiar: compounding is exponential growth, the same recurrence as b = b * (1 + r) run in a loop. And like all exponentials, it is deceptively slow early and violent late — the last decade of a 30-year investment typically creates more wealth than the first two combined. Check Figure 1.1 again: the curve earns most of its money at the right edge.

Worked example

You invest $10,000 at 7% per year for 30 years. FV = 10,000 × (1.07)30 = 10,000 × 7.612 = $76,123. Total interest: $66,123 — of which only $21,000 (30 × $700) is interest on the original principal. The other $45,123 is interest earned by interest.

A handy mental shortcut: the rule of 72. Money doubles in approximately 72 ÷ r years. At 8%, doubling takes about 72 ÷ 8 = 9 years (the exact answer is 9.006 — eerily good). At 10%, about 7.2 years. It also runs in reverse: at 6% growth, 12 years per doubling, so a 36-year career gives you three doublings, or 8×. The rule is a first-order approximation that is tightest for rates between about 4% and 12% — fine for napkins, not for contracts.

Compounding frequency. Rates are quoted per year, but interest is often credited monthly or daily. If a 6% annual rate compounds monthly, each month applies 0.5%, and the twelve multiplications overshoot 6% slightly, because each month's interest starts earning immediately:

Compounding of a 6% quoted rate $10,000 after one year Effective yield
Annual $10,600.00 6.000%
Semi-annual $10,609.00 6.090%
Quarterly $10,613.64 6.136%
Monthly $10,616.78 6.168%
Daily $10,618.31 6.183%

This is why the same loan can wear two different numbers. The APR is the quoted rate — the periodic rate times the number of periods. The APY (also called the effective annual rate, or EAR) is what your balance actually does in a year:

EAR = (1 + APR ÷ m)m − 1
m = compounding periods per year (12 for monthly, 365 for daily)

Common pitfall

A credit card advertising "24% APR" compounds monthly: 2% per month, so EAR = (1.02)12 − 1 = 26.8%. Lenders quote loans in APR (looks smaller) and savings in APY (looks bigger) — always convert to EAR before comparing anything. A 12% APR compounded monthly is really 12.68%; the chart below shows how the gap grows with frequency, with diminishing returns as you approach continuous compounding.

Now stop reading and drive the machine yourself. The lab below opens with the worked example above ($10,000, 7%, 30 years) so you can confirm the $76,123 — then add a monthly contribution and watch what steady saving plus compounding does. The rate is treated as an effective annual rate, and contributions are added at the end of each month.

Compound interest lab

Drag the sliders; try 15 vs 30 years at the same rate, then add a $500 monthly contribution.

Final value—
Total contributed—
Interest earned—
Baseline ending value—
Deep dive: continuous compounding and e

What happens as compounding frequency goes to infinity — every second, every nanosecond? The one-year growth factor (1 + r/m)m converges to er as m → ∞. This is the very limit that defines Euler's number, discovered by Jacob Bernoulli while studying exactly this compound-interest question in 1683. So continuously compounded money grows as FV = PV × ert.

The practical gap is tiny — at 6%, daily compounding gives 6.1831% effective and continuous gives 6.1837% — but the form is beloved by quants because log-returns add across time instead of multiplying, which makes the math (and the code) dramatically cleaner. You will meet ert again in Section 9 (Derivatives), where option-pricing models live entirely in continuous time.

Discounting: the most important idea in finance

Now run the machine backwards. If money grows going forward, it must shrink going backward: a dollar promised in the future is worth less than a dollar today. Computing exactly how much less is called discounting, and the result is the present value. Just invert the compounding formula:

PV = FV(1 + r)n
r here is called the discount rate — the return you could earn elsewhere on money of similar risk

Why is this "the most important idea in finance"? Because nearly every financial object is a bundle of future cash payments, and discounting is the only honest way to compare bundles with different timings. A bond is a list of promised coupons — Section 8 is, at its core, one long exercise in discounting them. A stock is a claim on future profits — the valuation models of Section 13 discount those. A pension, a lawsuit settlement, a startup's business plan: all just cash-flow lists waiting to be discounted. Learn this one operation and you have the skeleton key.

Key idea

The time value of money: amounts at different dates are different currencies, and (1 + r)n is the exchange rate between them. Never add or compare dollar amounts from different dates without converting them to the same date first.

The discount rate you choose is doing a lot of quiet work. It encodes pure time preference (even a risk-free promise must beat sitting on cash), expected inflation, and — crucially — risk: the shakier the promise, the higher the rate, and the less the promise is worth today. Discount $1,000,000 arriving in 40 years at 7% and it is worth 1,000,000 ÷ 1.0740 = $66,780 today; at 10% it collapses to about $22,000. Small changes in r produce huge changes in PV at long horizons — which is why analysts argue endlessly about discount rates, and why long-dated assets swing so hard when interest rates move.

Your edge

You already know this function. Discounting is exponential decay — PV = FV × (1 + r)−n is the same shape as radioactive half-life or a decaying cache score. The rule of 72 works in reverse too: at a 7.2% discount rate, a future payment loses half its present value every 10 years. And a discount rate plays the same role as a decay constant in your models: one parameter that quietly controls everything downstream.

Present value flipper

Type any future amount and see what it is worth today — then raise the rate and watch it melt.

Present value—

—

Streams of cash flows: annuities and perpetuities

Real financial life rarely involves a single future payment. Mortgages, salaries, pensions, bond coupons, and subscription revenues are streams — sequences of cash flows. The universal recipe: discount each payment individually and sum. Present values add — that linearity is what makes finance computable.

Two special streams are so common they earn their own formulas. An annuity pays a fixed amount C each period for n periods. Summing the geometric series of discounted payments (derivation in the deep dive) collapses to:

PVannuity = C × 1 − (1 + r)−nr
C = payment per period · r = rate per period · n = number of payments

Worked example: the lottery's fine print

A "$1,000,000 jackpot" paid as $50,000 a year for 20 years is not worth $1,000,000. At a 5% discount rate: PV = 50,000 × (1 − 1.05−20) ÷ 0.05 = 50,000 × 12.462 = $623,111. The headline number quietly ignores the time value of money — which is exactly why lump-sum options are always advertised smaller.

Run the formula in reverse and you get every mortgage payment ever quoted: the bank sets PV equal to the loan amount and solves for C. Borrow $400,000 for 30 years at 6% (0.5% monthly, 360 payments) and C = 400,000 × 0.005 ÷ (1 − 1.005−360) = $2,398.20 per month. Over the full term you pay about $863,353 — more interest than principal. Each payment first covers the month's interest and puts the remainder toward the balance, a schedule called amortization: early payments are mostly interest, late ones mostly principal.

Let n → ∞ and the annuity becomes a perpetuity — payments forever. Infinite payments, finite value, because distant payments discount toward zero. The formula is almost embarrassingly simple:

PVperpetuity = C ÷ r
C = payment per period, forever · r = discount rate per period

This is the intuition behind endowments and pensions: a $100 million university endowment at a sustainable 4% supports $4 million of spending per year, forever. It is not hypothetical, either — the British government issued actual perpetual bonds ("consols") from 1751, redeeming the last ones only in 2015. And the workhorse valuation model of Section 13 is a perpetuity whose payment grows at rate g each year: PV = C ÷ (r − g), the growing perpetuity. Three formulas — single payment, annuity, perpetuity — and you can price a startling fraction of the financial world.

Deep dive: deriving the annuity formula in four lines

The PV of an annuity is a geometric series — the same one you summed in Algorithms when analyzing divide-and-conquer costs. Write x = 1 ÷ (1 + r). Then:

PV = Cx + Cx2 + … + Cxn = Cx × (1 − xn) ÷ (1 − x).

Substitute x = 1 ÷ (1 + r), multiply numerator and denominator by (1 + r), and note 1 − x = r ÷ (1 + r). The (1 + r) factors cancel, leaving PV = C × (1 − (1 + r)−n) ÷ r.

For the perpetuity, let n → ∞: since x < 1, xn → 0 and PV → C ÷ r. Sanity check via the lottery example: as n grows from 20 to ∞ at r = 5%, the multiplier rises from 12.46 to 20 — even an infinite stream of $50,000 payments is worth only $1,000,000 today. Distant money is cheap money.

Nominal vs real: the inflation adjustment

One correction remains, and skipping it invalidates everything above. All our numbers so far are nominal — raw dollar counts. But dollars are a measuring stick that shrinks: inflation means each year's dollar buys a little less. What you actually care about is the real quantity: purchasing power, not dollar count.

Notice that Figure 1.4 is just discounting in disguise: purchasing power decays by the inflation rate exactly as present value decays by the discount rate. To convert between nominal and real returns, use the Fisher equation, named for economist Irving Fisher:

(1 + rnominal) = (1 + rreal) × (1 + i)
i = inflation rate · quick approximation: rreal ≈ rnominal − i (good when rates are small)

Worked example

Your portfolio returns 8% in a year with 3% inflation. Real return = 1.08 ÷ 1.03 − 1 = 4.85% — close to the 5% the subtraction shortcut gives, but not equal, and the gap widens as rates rise. Nastier case: in 1979 US inflation hit about 13%, while regulated passbook savings accounts paid 5.5%. Savers' nominal balances grew every month while their real wealth shrank about 7% a year. Positive nominal, negative real: the books looked fine and the purchasing power bled out.

The discipline this imposes is simple: state every return, discount rate, and projection as nominal or real, and never mix the two in one calculation. Discount nominal cash flows at nominal rates and real cash flows at real rates — mixing them double-counts inflation. Markets even let you observe the split directly: the US Treasury issues inflation-protected bonds (TIPS) whose yields are real, so the gap between regular Treasury yields and TIPS yields reveals the market's inflation expectation — a trick we will use in Section 8. Where inflation itself comes from, and how the Fed fights it, is the business of Sections 3 and 4.

Key idea

Compounding grows your money; inflation compounds against it. Only the real rate — roughly nominal minus inflation — builds actual purchasing power. An investment "up 20%" during 20% inflation made you nothing.

Check your understanding

1. Which of these is NOT one of the three classic functions of money?

Money's three functions are medium of exchange, store of value, and unit of account. Managing investment risk is the job of diversification and hedging instruments (Sections 15 and 16), not of money itself — holding cash actually exposes you to inflation risk.

2. You invest $1,000 at 10% per year, compounded annually. What is it worth after 2 years?

1,000 × 1.10² = $1,210. The extra $10 over the simple-interest answer ($1,200) is year two's interest earned on year one's $100 of interest. ($1,221 would be roughly three years' compounding.)

3. Using the rule of 72, how long does money take to double at 6% per year?

72 ÷ 6 = 12 years (the exact answer is 11.9). The rule of 72 divides 72 by the percentage rate to approximate doubling time.

4. The discount rate used to value a future payment rises from 5% to 8%. The payment's present value…

PV = FV ÷ (1 + r)ⁿ, so a higher discount rate shrinks present value. This inverse relationship between rates and the value of future cash flows drives bond prices (Section 8) and stock valuations (Section 13) alike.

5. Your savings earn 6% nominal in a year with 4% inflation. Your real return is approximately…

The Fisher approximation gives 6% − 4% = 2% (exactly: 1.06 ÷ 1.04 − 1 = 1.92%). Only that slice grew your purchasing power; the other 4 percentage points merely kept pace with rising prices.

Key takeaways

  • Money is whatever reliably serves three functions — medium of exchange, store of value, unit of account. Modern fiat money is backed by trust, tax demand, and managed scarcity, not by gold.
  • Interest is the price of time: it compensates for opportunity cost, expected inflation, and default risk. Every rate you see encodes all three.
  • Compound interest earns interest on interest: FV = PV × (1 + r)ⁿ. It is exponential — slow early, explosive late — and money doubles roughly every 72 ÷ r years.
  • Quoted APRs understate true cost; convert to the effective annual rate, (1 + APR ÷ m)ᵐ − 1, before comparing anything.
  • Discounting is compounding in reverse: PV = FV ÷ (1 + r)ⁿ. Nearly all of finance — bonds, stocks, pensions, projects — is discounting streams of future cash flows.
  • Annuities (fixed payments for n periods) and perpetuities (payments forever, PV = C ÷ r) price mortgages, lotteries, pensions, and endowments with two compact formulas.
  • Always separate nominal from real: (1 + nominal) = (1 + real)(1 + inflation). Only real returns build purchasing power.

Part I · Economic Foundations

2. Microeconomics: How Markets Work

Strip away the tickers and the jargon, and every market — the corner coffee shop, the used-car lot, the New York Stock Exchange — runs on the same two forces: buyers who want things and sellers who provide them. This section hands you the supply-and-demand machinery that prices everything, including the stocks and bonds you'll meet later, and shows exactly what happens when that machinery breaks.

Listen to Section 2:

Scarcity, opportunity cost, and thinking at the margin

Economics starts from one blunt fact: scarcity. Wants are unlimited; time, money, land, and engineers are not. So every choice forces you to give something up, and economists insist you count that sacrifice as a real cost. The opportunity cost of anything is the value of the best alternative you didn't pick.

Worked example

You earn $120,000 a year and consider a two-year, full-time master's costing $80,000 in tuition. The sticker price is $80,000 — but the economic cost is $80,000 + (2 × $120,000) = $320,000, because you also give up two years of salary. Whether the degree is worth it depends on whether it delivers more than $320,000 of extra lifetime value, not $80,000. Most bad decisions come from ignoring the invisible $240,000.

Opportunity cost is the beating heart of finance. The discount rate you met in Section 1 (Money & the Time Value of Money) is nothing more than the opportunity cost of capital: the return your money could earn in its best alternative use. Every valuation you will ever build is an opportunity-cost calculation in a suit.

The second habit of mind is marginal thinking: rational decisions compare the benefit of one more unit against the cost of one more unit — never totals, never averages. An airline flight costs $90,000 to run whether it carries 150 or 151 passengers, so the average cost per seat is $600. But the marginal cost of passenger 151 — a snack and a little fuel — is maybe $20. Selling that last empty seat for $59 an hour before departure is pure profit, even though $59 looks absurd against a $600 average. Airlines, cloud providers, and market makers all price this way.

Marginal thinking also tells you what to ignore: sunk costs. The $2 million already spent on a failing project is gone whether you continue or quit, so it belongs in zero future decisions — a rule humans violate constantly, as you'll see in Section 17 (Behavioral Finance).

Your edge

Optimizing at the margin is gradient ascent. You don't ask "is this whole position good?" — you ask "does one more step improve the objective?" and keep stepping until marginal benefit equals marginal cost. Firms maximize profit exactly where marginal revenue = marginal cost, for the same reason your optimizer stops where the gradient hits zero.

Demand: what buyers will pay

Start on the buyer side. Each potential buyer has a willingness to pay — the maximum they'd hand over for one unit. Line every buyer up from the most eager to the least, and you get the demand curve: at any price P, it tells you how many units get bought. To a data person, a demand curve is literally every bid in the market, sorted descending — ORDER BY willingness_to_pay DESC. Stock exchanges make this visible: the bid side of an order book is a demand curve you can watch update in real time.

Demand curves slope down — cut the price and more units sell — for three reinforcing reasons. First, substitution: as coffee gets cheaper relative to tea, some tea drinkers defect. Second, the income effect: a lower price stretches every budget, so people can afford more of everything. Third, diminishing marginal utility: your fifth slice of pizza is worth less to you than your first, so you'll only buy it if it's cheap.

Common pitfall: movement vs. shift

When the good's own price changes, you slide along a fixed demand curve — economists call that a change in quantity demanded. When anything else changes — income, tastes, other prices — the entire curve moves. Mixing these up is the classic Econ 101 exam trap and, worse, a real analytical error: "price fell and people bought more" is a movement; "the product went viral and people buy more at every price" is a shift.

The curve shifts when any non-price driver changes. Income matters two ways: for a normal good, richer buyers demand more; for an inferior good, they demand less. Prices of related goods matter too: substitutes (Uber vs. Lyft) pull demand toward whichever got cheaper, while complements (printers and ink) rise and fall together. Add tastes, expectations about future prices, and the sheer number of buyers, and you have the full shifter list.

Here's the finance payoff: the demand curve for a stock shifts on expectations. A single earnings headline changes what buyers believe the future cash flows are worth, so the whole curve jumps in milliseconds — which is why prices gap instead of glide. When you hear "buyers stepped in" on financial TV, translate it as "the demand curve shifted right."

Supply: what sellers must get

Mirror image, seller side. The supply curve tells you how many units producers will offer at each price, and its backbone is marginal cost. A rational seller produces another unit only if the price covers the cost of making that unit — so the supply curve essentially is the industry's marginal-cost curve, sorted ascending.

That sorting explains why supply slopes up. In oil, the cheap wells pump first: Saudi fields might lift a barrel for $10, US shale needs $45, deep-water Arctic projects $70+. At $30 a barrel only the cheap wells run; at $80, everyone drills. Higher prices coax higher-cost producers off the bench — that's the upward slope, and it's why commodity analysts obsess over "cost curves."

Supply shifts when production conditions change: input costs (wages, energy, chips), technology (fracking shifted oil supply right for a decade), taxes and subsidies, sellers' expectations, and the number of sellers. In markets for securities, supply has its own vocabulary: a company's IPO or secondary offering adds shares to the market, an insider lockup expiry releases a wave of potential sellers, and the US Treasury shifts the bond supply curve every time it ramps up issuance to fund a deficit (see Section 5 and Section 8).

Event Curve affected Direction Why
Incomes rise (normal good) Demand Right Buyers can afford more at every price
A substitute gets cheaper Demand Left Buyers defect to the alternative
A complement gets cheaper Demand Right The bundle as a whole costs less
Buyers expect prices to rise soon Demand Right Purchases get pulled forward to today
Input costs jump Supply Left Each unit costs more to make
Technology improves Supply Right Each unit costs less to make
Per-unit tax on sellers Supply Left Acts exactly like a cost increase

Equilibrium: where price gets discovered

Put the two curves in one diagram and they cross exactly once. That crossing is equilibrium: the one price where the quantity buyers want equals the quantity sellers offer. Economists call it the market-clearing price, and traders call finding it price discovery — it is, quite literally, the product an exchange sells.

The magic is that nobody computes it. If the price sits too high, sellers offer more than buyers take — a surplus — and inventories pile up until someone cuts price. Too low, and eager buyers outnumber goods — a shortage — and bidding pushes price up. The system self-corrects like a thermostat, with price as the feedback signal. Play with it below.

Supply & demand simulator

Drag the shifters or hit a preset scenario — the dashed lines are the original curves, and the dot is the market-clearing point.

Market price P*$50.00
Quantity traded Q*100 units
Price vs. baseline±$0.00

Back to baseline: demand P = 100 − 0.5Q meets supply P = 20 + 0.3Q at Q* = 100 and P* = $50.

Now watch what happens when governments override the thermostat. A price ceiling set below equilibrium creates a permanent shortage: buyers want more than sellers will provide, and since price can't ration the good, something uglier does — queues, waiting lists, lotteries, favoritism, and side payments. Rent control is the textbook case: cap rents 30% below market and you get decade-long waiting lists, "key money" bribes, and landlords who stop maintaining buildings because tenants will queue up anyway. A price floor set above equilibrium does the reverse, producing surpluses — the EU's old butter mountains, or unsold labor when a wage floor binds.

Where legal trade is throttled, a black market forms to finish the job at the true equilibrium price — scalped tickets, smuggled gasoline, off-books apartments. The lesson isn't that all controls are always wrong; it's that price controls never repeal scarcity, they just change who queues and who profits.

Real world: ceilings in finance

Interest rates are prices too — the price of borrowing money. From the 1930s to the 1980s, the US capped the interest banks could pay depositors (Regulation Q). When market rates soared past the cap in the 1970s, savers did exactly what the model predicts: they exited, funneling cash into newly invented money market funds — a legal "black market" for yield that permanently reshaped American finance. Usury caps on loan rates show the flip side: cap the price of credit below what risky borrowers cost to serve, and lenders simply stop lending to them — a shortage called credit rationing.

Deep dive: surplus, deadweight loss, and the price of meddling

Economists measure a market's total win-win using two areas on the diagram. Consumer surplus is the triangle between the demand curve and the price: everyone who'd have paid more than P* pockets the difference. Producer surplus is the triangle between the price and the supply curve. With the simulator's baseline curves (D: P = 100 − 0.5Q, S: P = 20 + 0.3Q, equilibrium at Q* = 100, P* = $50), consumer surplus = ½ × (100 − 50) × 100 = $2,500 and producer surplus = ½ × (50 − 20) × 100 = $1,500. Total surplus: $4,000.

Now impose a $40 price ceiling. Sellers supply only (40 − 20) ÷ 0.3 ≈ 66.7 units, while buyers want (100 − 40) ÷ 0.5 = 120 — a shortage of about 53 units. The trades between Q = 66.7 and Q = 100 would have made both sides better off, but they never happen. Their lost value — ½ × (66.67 − 40) × (100 − 66.67) ≈ $444 — is called deadweight loss: wealth that doesn't go to buyers, sellers, or the government. It simply evaporates. Taxes create deadweight loss the same way, which is why economists judge policies by how much surplus they burn per dollar raised or redistributed.

Elasticity: how hard the market pushes back

Slope direction tells you which way quantity moves when price changes; elasticity tells you how much. The price elasticity of demand (PED) is the ratio of percentage changes:

PED = %ΔQdemanded ÷ %ΔP
|PED| > 1 → elastic (buyers flee) · |PED| < 1 → inelastic (buyers absorb) · PED is negative for normal demand; we compare absolute values

Demand is elastic when close substitutes exist, the good is a luxury, it eats a big share of the budget, or buyers have time to adapt. It's inelastic when the good is a necessity with no substitutes — insulin, a tank of gas this week, electricity tonight. Time matters enormously: gasoline demand barely budges in a month (you still have the same commute) but responds over five years (you buy a smaller car, move closer to work). Supply has an elasticity too, with time as the star: the number of apartments in a city is nearly fixed this year (inelastic) but very responsive over a decade of construction.

Elasticity has a cash consequence — the revenue test. Revenue = price × quantity, and a price hike raises one factor while shrinking the other. Which force wins depends entirely on elasticity: raise price on inelastic demand and revenue climbs; raise it on elastic demand and revenue bleeds.

Worked example: the revenue test

A café raises its latte from $5.00 to $5.50 (+10%). Daily sales drop from 200 to 170 cups (−15%). PED = −15% ÷ 10% = −1.5 → elastic. Revenue goes from 200 × $5.00 = $1,000 to 170 × $5.50 = $935. The price hike cost the café $65 a day.

Now suppose it's the only café inside a secure office campus and sales drop just 2% (200 → 196 cups). PED = −0.2 → inelastic. New revenue: 196 × $5.50 = $1,078. Same 10% hike, opposite result. (Purists compute the percentages against the midpoint of old and new values so the answer doesn't depend on direction; the verdict here is the same.)

Analogy: liquidity is elasticity in disguise

Liquidity — a word you'll hear daily in finance — is essentially the elasticity of an asset's market. In a deep, liquid market like Apple stock, a $10 million buy order barely moves the price: the order book is thick with sellers at every price, so supply to you is highly elastic. In a thin small-cap stock, the same order rips through the book and spikes the price — inelastic supply. When people say a market "dried up" in a crisis, they mean its elasticity collapsed: every trade started moving prices violently. More in Section 6 (The Financial System).

Market structures: from price takers to price makers

So far we've assumed sellers simply accept the market price. Whether they actually can depends on market power — and market power lives on a spectrum defined by how many sellers there are and how substitutable their products look.

At the far left sits perfect competition: many sellers, identical product, zero pricing power. A Kansas wheat farmer can sell unlimited bushels at the market price and exactly zero bushels a cent above it. Competition grinds price down to marginal cost and profits toward zero. The major currency pairs in FX trading come close: your euros are identical to everyone else's.

One notch right, monopolistic competition describes most of the economy you see: many sellers with slightly differentiated products — restaurants, apps, ETF providers. Branding buys a small markup, but substitutes cap it. Then comes oligopoly — a few big players whose moves visibly affect each other — and finally monopoly, one seller with no close substitute, free to restrict output and price far above cost. Regulators tolerate a natural monopoly like a water utility (duplicate pipe networks would be wasteful) but cap its prices in exchange.

Oligopoly is where strategy gets interesting, because each firm's best move depends on rivals' moves — the home turf of game theory. The canonical example is a pricing standoff:

Weekly profit ($m) Airline B: price High Airline B: price Low
Airline A: price High A: 8 · B: 8 A: 2 · B: 12
Airline A: price Low A: 12 · B: 2 A: 4 · B: 4

Check Airline A's logic. If B prices High, A earns 8 by matching or 12 by undercutting — undercut. If B prices Low, A earns 2 holding High or 4 matching Low — match. Cutting price is best no matter what B does, and B reasons identically. Both land on Low/Low earning 4 each, when High/High would have paid 8 each. That trap — nobody can improve by changing strategy alone — is a Nash equilibrium, and it's the prisoner's dilemma wearing a price tag. It explains why price wars erupt, and why a cartel — sellers colluding to hold prices High/High — is both illegal in most markets and chronically unstable: every member is tempted to secretly defect, which is why OPEC members so often bust their own quotas.

Real world: market power in finance

Finance has textbook oligopolies. Three credit rating agencies — S&P, Moody's, and Fitch — control roughly 95% of the global ratings market, a structure that mattered enormously in 2008 (see Section 17). A handful of exchanges and index providers (S&P Dow Jones, MSCI, FTSE Russell) collect tolls on trillions in assets. And when analysts praise a company's "moat," they mean durable market power — the ability to price above cost for years. That pricing power flows straight into the profit margins you'll dissect in Section 12 (Ratio Analysis).

When markets fail — and why financial regulation exists

Supply and demand is a marvel, but it earns its Nobel Prizes only under conditions: many participants, full information, and prices that capture all costs and benefits. Break those conditions and markets misfire in predictable ways. Three failures matter most — and all three run straight through finance.

Externalities. An externality is a cost or benefit that lands on bystanders. A factory that pollutes a river pays for labor and steel but not for the dead fish downstream, so pollution is overproduced — the price is a lie that omits part of the cost. Vaccines work in reverse: benefits spill onto others, so left alone we underproduce them. Finance's great negative externality is systemic risk: an over-leveraged bank that fails doesn't just hurt itself, it can topple counterparties and freeze credit for everyone — pollution, but the smoke is panic. That's the economic logic behind capital requirements, explored in Section 6 and Section 16 (Risk Management).

Public goods. A public good — national defense, clean air, financial stability itself — is one nobody can be excluded from and one person's use doesn't deplete. Since you get it whether you pay or not, everyone is tempted to be a free rider, and private markets underproduce the good. That's why stability is provisioned publicly, by central banks and regulators (Section 4).

Information asymmetry. The failure that defines finance. Information asymmetry means one side of a deal knows more than the other — and economist George Akerlof showed, with used cars, how that can destroy a market entirely. Suppose 100 cars are for sale: 50 "peaches" worth $12,000 to buyers ($10,000 to their sellers) and 50 lemons worth $4,000 to buyers ($2,000 to sellers). Buyers can't tell which is which, so they'll offer around the expected value: 0.5 × $12,000 + 0.5 × $4,000 = $8,000. But peach owners won't take $8,000 for a $10,000 car — they withdraw. Now the market is all lemons, buyers know it, and the price collapses toward $4,000. Good cars never trade at all. That death spiral is adverse selection.

Now reread that paragraph replacing "used car" with "share of stock." A company's insiders know far more about its true condition than outside investors. Without a fix, rational investors would price every stock like a possible lemon, good companies couldn't raise capital fairly, and markets would shrivel. This is precisely why the SEC exists: mandatory disclosure — audited financial statements (Section 11), quarterly filings, insider-trading bans — is anti-lemon technology. So are credit ratings, warranties, and bank due diligence. Regulation here isn't the opposite of free markets; it's the plumbing that keeps them from unraveling.

One sibling deserves its own name: moral hazard is hidden action rather than hidden quality — behaving more recklessly because someone else bears the downside. Deposit insurance stops bank runs (good) but can tempt banks to gamble with insured money (the hazard), which is why insurance comes bundled with supervision. Adverse selection is about who shows up to the deal; moral hazard is about what they do after signing. Keep the pair handy — they explain half of financial regulation, and a good share of the crises in Section 17.

Key idea

Markets fail exactly where prices stop telling the truth — about costs (externalities), about who pays (public goods), or about quality (asymmetric information). Financial regulation, at its best, is truth-maintenance for prices.

Check your understanding

1. You leave a $120,000 job for a two-year, full-time master's with $80,000 total tuition. Ignoring living costs, the economic cost of the degree is…

Opportunity cost includes the best alternative you give up — here, $240,000 of salary on top of $80,000 tuition. Future raises determine whether the degree is worth $320,000; they don't reduce what it costs.

2. Laptop prices fall and shoppers buy more laptops. On a supply-and-demand diagram, this is…

The good's own price changed, so you slide along a fixed curve — quantity demanded rises. The curve itself only shifts when a non-price factor (income, tastes, other goods' prices, expectations) changes.

3. A utility estimates the price elasticity of demand for electricity at −0.4. If it raises prices 10%, its revenue will…

With inelastic demand (|PED| < 1), the percentage drop in quantity is smaller than the percentage rise in price, so revenue = P × Q increases. That's the revenue test.

4. A city caps rents 30% below the market-clearing level. Standard supply-and-demand analysis predicts…

Below the equilibrium price, quantity demanded exceeds quantity supplied — a shortage. Since price can't ration the good, queues, key money, and black-market workarounds do, and landlords have little incentive to maintain units.

5. In Akerlof's used-car market, buyers can't observe quality, so they offer only an average price. The predictable result is…

Owners of above-average cars refuse the average price and withdraw, which lowers average quality, which lowers the price buyers will offer — adverse selection. Disclosure rules, audits, and warranties exist to break this loop.

Key takeaways

  • Scarcity makes every choice a trade-off; the true cost of anything is its opportunity cost — the best alternative foregone. The discount rate is finance's version of this idea.
  • Decide at the margin: compare the benefit and cost of one more unit, ignore sunk costs, and stop where marginal benefit equals marginal cost.
  • Demand slopes down (substitution, income effects, diminishing marginal utility); supply slopes up (rising marginal cost). Own-price changes move you along a curve; everything else shifts it.
  • Equilibrium is a feedback loop: surpluses push prices down, shortages push them up. Price controls don't repeal scarcity — they create queues, quality decay, and black markets.
  • Elasticity measures how hard quantity responds to price. Raise prices on inelastic demand and revenue rises; on elastic demand it falls. Market liquidity is elasticity in disguise.
  • Market power runs on a spectrum from perfect competition (price = marginal cost) to monopoly (price far above it); oligopolists play games, and the prisoner's dilemma explains price wars and cheating cartels.
  • Markets fail on externalities, public goods, and information asymmetry. Adverse selection and moral hazard are the two demons of finance — and disclosure rules, ratings, audits, and capital requirements are the exorcists.

Part I · Economic Foundations

3. Macroeconomics: The Big Picture

Microeconomics zoomed in on single markets; macroeconomics zooms out to the whole machine — total output, the price level, jobs, and the boom-bust rhythm underneath them. By the end of this section you'll be able to read a GDP release, a CPI print, or a jobs report the way an analyst does: knowing exactly what each number measures, and where it can mislead you.

GDP: the economy's top line

Macroeconomics asks three big questions: how much are we producing, what do things cost, and who has a job? Where Section 2 (Microeconomics) studied one market's supply and demand, macro adds everything up. The master number is GDP — the market value of everything a country produces in a quarter or a year.

Two words in that definition do heavy lifting. Final means we count the loaf of bread but not the flour that went into it — otherwise we'd double-count the wheat, the flour, and the bread. Final goods are the end products. And inside a country means geography, not nationality: a German-owned BMW plant in South Carolina counts in US GDP.

Statisticians measure GDP by adding up who spends the money — the expenditure approach:

GDP = C + I + G + NX
C = consumption · I = investment · G = government purchases · NX = net exports (exports − imports)

For the US (roughly $29 trillion in 2024) the proportions are surprisingly stable. Consumption is about 68% — the American economy is mostly households buying things. Investment (~18%) means new factories, equipment, software, and homes — not buying stocks; in GDP-speak, trading existing assets creates nothing new. Government purchases (~17%) cover roads, teachers, and defense — but not Social Security checks, which merely transfer money to households (it gets counted when they spend it, in C). Net exports run about −3% for the US: a persistent trade deficit, which we'll unpack in Section 5 (Fiscal Policy, Debt & Trade).

One trap will follow you everywhere in macro data: prices change. If GDP rises 6% but everything just got 6% more expensive, nothing real happened. Nominal GDP uses current prices; real GDP strips inflation out using a price index called the GDP deflator. Analysts almost always mean real growth when they say "the economy grew."

Worked example

Nominal GDP rises from $25.0T to $26.5T: +6.0%. The deflator says prices rose 4.0%. Real growth = 1.06 ÷ 1.04 − 1 ≈ 1.9% — close to the quick shortcut of 6% − 4% = 2%. A headline that looked like a boom was actually a sluggish year plus inflation.

Size also isn't standard of living. China's GDP (~$18T) dwarfs Switzerland's (~$0.9T), but divide by population and Switzerland produces about $100,000 per person versus China's ~$13,000. GDP per capita is the number to watch for living standards; total GDP is the number to watch for geopolitical and market weight.

Analogy

GDP is a speedometer, not a health checkup. It tells you how fast the machine is running — nothing about who's in the car or what's happening to the engine. It ignores unpaid work (a parent's childcare counts for zero; the same care purchased from a daycare counts fully), leisure, environmental damage, the informal cash economy, and distribution — GDP per capita can rise while the median household stagnates. Wikipedia adds enormous value and roughly $0 to GDP. Useful gauge; incomplete one.

Growth: why 1% is a big deal

Growth rates look boring — 1.5% versus 3% barely moves a chart for a year or two. But you already know from Section 1 what happens when a rate compounds: the gap goes from rounding error to canyon. Growth is the FV = PV × (1 + r)n machine applied to an entire country.

Run the numbers. Two countries both start at $10,000 of GDP per person. Country A grows 1.5% a year, Country B grows 3%. After 60 years — one working lifetime — A reaches about $24,400 and B about $58,900. Same starting point, and B's citizens end up 2.4 times richer, from a difference you'd barely notice in any single year. The Rule of 72 from Section 1 says it directly: at 1.5%, living standards double every ~48 years; at 3%, every ~24.

Real world: Korea vs. Ghana

In 1960, South Korea and Ghana had roughly similar income per person — both poor, both recently emerged from colonization or war. Korea then compounded at some of the fastest rates ever recorded for three decades. Today Korean income per person is roughly seven times Ghana's. No single year did that; the exponent did.

So what sets the growth rate? In the short run, lots of things — we'll get to cycles below. But in the long run the answer is almost embarrassingly simple: productivity — output per hour worked. A country gets richer per person only if each hour of work produces more stuff. As economist Paul Krugman put it, "productivity isn't everything, but in the long run it is almost everything."

Productivity grows through better machines and infrastructure (capital), better recipes (technology — from crop rotation to spreadsheets to LLMs), better-trained people (human capital), and institutions that make investing worthwhile — courts, property rights, competition. This is why economists get worked up about policies with "small" effects on growth: a policy that permanently adds 0.2 percentage points to productivity growth is worth more, compounded over 50 years, than almost any one-off program you could name.

Inflation: how the CPI is built, and why 2% is the target

Inflation is a sustained rise in the overall price level — which is the same thing as a fall in what each dollar buys. It's measured as the rate of change of a price index, and the most famous index is the CPI (Consumer Price Index).

The construction is beautifully simple in concept. The Bureau of Labor Statistics defines a basket of what a typical urban household buys, sends people out to collect roughly 80,000 prices every month, and averages the price changes using weights from surveys of actual household budgets. Shelter gets about a third of the weight; food about 13%; energy about 7%. Change in the weighted average = inflation.

Your edge

CPI is literally a weighted average — a dot product of budget-share weights and price changes. Two consequences fall out of the math. First, your personal inflation rate differs from the headline whenever your budget weights differ (renters in hot cities live a higher inflation rate than the index). Second, big-weight items dominate: a 5% move in shelter moves the index more than a 20% move in gasoline.

Worked example: a mini-CPI

Say the basket is 35% rent, 15% food, 5% gasoline, 45% everything else. This year rent rises 5%, food 2%, gas 20%, everything else 1%. Inflation = 0.35×5 + 0.15×2 + 0.05×20 + 0.45×1 = 1.75 + 0.30 + 1.00 + 0.45 = 3.5%. Note the 20% gas spike — the thing everyone sees on street signs — contributes only 1 point.

Because food and energy prices whipsaw with weather and geopolitics, analysts also watch core inflation (everything except food and energy) to see the underlying trend. Headline is what households feel; core is what forecasts better.

Where does inflation come from? Three classic stories, and real episodes usually mix them. Demand-pull: total spending outruns what the economy can produce — too much money chasing too few goods (think stimulus checks meeting a reopening economy in 2021). Cost-push: input costs jump and producers pass them on — the 1973 oil embargo quadrupled crude prices and inflation followed. And the monetary story: if the money supply grows persistently faster than output, prices eventually follow — Milton Friedman's famous line that inflation is "always and everywhere a monetary phenomenon" is the extreme version, and it becomes exactly true in every hyperinflation.

So why do the Fed, the ECB, and most modern central banks target roughly 2% inflation rather than zero? Four reasons. Measured inflation overstates true inflation slightly (indexes struggle with quality improvements and substitution), so 2% measured is closer to 0–1% real. A buffer above zero keeps the economy away from deflation. Mild inflation greases labor markets: firms can cut real wages by freezing nominal pay, which is easier than cutting paychecks outright. And it leaves the central bank room to push real interest rates below zero in a crisis — more in Section 4 (Central Banks).

Deflation sounds pleasant — falling prices! — and is anything but. If prices fall steadily, waiting becomes profitable: why buy today what's cheaper next month? Spending stalls, so firms cut prices and jobs, so spending stalls further. Worse, debts are fixed in dollars, so falling prices and wages make every mortgage and business loan heavier in real terms. That self-reinforcing spiral is the deflation trap; Japan spent much of the 1990s and 2000s stuck in a mild version of it, which is why central banks fear 0% far more than 3%.

Inflation time machine

Set an average inflation rate and watch what it quietly does to cash. Try the presets — especially the third one.

Your cash will buy what this buys today$41.20
Needed then to match your amount today$242.73
Purchasing power halves every23 yrs

At 3% average inflation, in 30 years your $100.00 buys what $41.20 buys today. To match it, you would need $242.73.

Play with the presets and you'll feel the compounding viscerally: at the 2% target, $100 still buys $55 worth of today's goods after 30 years — a slow, planned-for leak. At the 7% America actually averaged across the 1970s, $100 shrinks to about $13 of buying power over 30 years. That's why "cash is safe" is only true in the short run — a theme we'll return to in Section 7 (Equities) and Section 8 (Fixed Income).

And then there's hyperinflation — conventionally, inflation above 50% per month. In Weimar Germany in 1923, prices doubled roughly every three to four days at the peak; workers were paid twice daily and sprinted to spend wages before evening, and banknotes were cheaper than firewood. Zimbabwe in November 2008 hit an estimated 79 billion percent per month — prices doubling roughly every day — until the country simply abandoned its own currency. The mechanism is always the same: a government that can't tax or borrow prints money to pay its bills, everyone races to swap cash for anything real, and money stops doing its job (recall the three functions of money from Section 1 — hyperinflation kills all three). The "Argentina-style 50%" preset above is annual, not monthly — and even that halves your purchasing power every 1.7 years.

Unemployment: one number, many stories

The unemployment rate is the share of the labor force — not the population — that is jobless and actively looking. Both italicized conditions hide bodies. Retirees, full-time students, stay-at-home parents, and people who've given up searching aren't unemployed; they're simply not in the labor force at all.

Concrete version: imagine 100 working-age adults. 60 have jobs, 4 are searching, 36 are out of the labor force. The labor force is 64, so unemployment is 4 ÷ 64 = 6.25%. Now suppose 2 of the searchers get demoralized and stop looking. Nobody found a job — but the labor force shrinks to 62, unemployed drops to 2, and the rate "improves" to 2 ÷ 62 = 3.2%. The headline halved while the economy got slightly worse.

Common pitfall

Never read the unemployment rate alone. Always check the labor-force participation rate next to it — if unemployment fell but participation fell too, discouraged workers may be doing the "improving." Broader measures like U-6 (which adds discouraged workers and part-timers who want full-time work) often tell a darker story than the headline U-3 rate.

Economists also sort unemployment by cause, because each type calls for a different fix. Frictional unemployment is people between jobs — a data scientist who quit Friday and starts interviews Monday. Structural unemployment is a skills or geography mismatch — coal miners in a region where the new jobs are in data centers. Cyclical unemployment is the recession kind: demand collapses everywhere at once and layoffs sweep across industries.

This is why zero unemployment is neither achievable nor desirable. A healthy, dynamic economy always has churn — people quitting bad matches to search for better ones is how workers and jobs find each other, like retries in a matching algorithm. The rate consistent with "everyone who wants a job at prevailing wages can find one reasonably fast" is called the natural rate, roughly 4–5% in the US. Below it, employers bid up wages and inflation pressure builds; above it, cyclical slack is wasting human potential. The Fed is legally tasked with balancing both sides — its "dual mandate," coming in Section 4.

Business cycles: the economy's heartbeat

Zoom out on any real GDP chart and you see a rising trend with a wobble around it. The wobble is the business cycle: an expansion climbs to a peak, activity contracts through a recession to a trough, then recovery begins the next expansion. The cycle is recurring but not periodic — expansions have lasted anywhere from one year to over a decade (post-1945 US expansions average about five years; recessions about ten months). Anyone selling you a fixed-length "cycle clock" is selling astrology.

Who decides when a recession officially happened? In the US it's the NBER (National Bureau of Economic Research), a committee of economists who date peaks and troughs using a dashboard of monthly data — payrolls, real income, industrial production, real spending — not the folk rule of "two negative GDP quarters." The catch: they rule only when the evidence is unambiguous, which means after the fact. The 2020 recession's end (April 2020) wasn't declared until July 2021 — fifteen months later. By the time a recession is official, markets have long since moved on.

That's why analysts obsess over indicator timing. Leading indicators (building permits, new factory orders, stock prices, jobless claims, the yield curve) tend to turn months before the economy. Coincident indicators (payrolls, industrial production) tell you where you are. Lagging indicators (the unemployment rate, CPI inflation) confirm what already happened — unemployment often keeps rising well after recovery begins, because firms rehire cautiously.

The most famous leading indicator deserves its own sentence. The yield curve plots government bond interest rates by maturity; normally long rates exceed short rates. When the curve inverts — short rates above long — bond investors are collectively betting the Fed will be cutting rates in the future, which usually means they expect a downturn. An inverted curve has preceded every US recession of the past six decades, typically by 6–24 months, with only one clear false alarm in the mid-1960s (and a long, hotly debated inversion in 2022–24 that was not followed by a recession on schedule). Why it works, and why it might stop working, is a Section 8 (Fixed Income) story.

AD–AS: one mental model for every shock

You now have the dashboard: output, prices, jobs. What you need is a way to reason about how a shock — an oil spike, a stimulus bill, a pandemic — moves the needles. The workhorse model is aggregate demand and aggregate supply, and it's a deliberate echo of the supply-and-demand cross from Section 2, with the whole economy on the axes: total real output on the x-axis, the overall price level on the y-axis.

Aggregate demand (AD) is total spending — C + I + G + NX again, now drawn as a curve. It slopes down: a lower price level stretches money and wealth further and tends to come with lower interest rates, both of which coax out more spending. Aggregate supply (AS) slopes up in the short run for a very human reason: wages and many prices are sticky — renegotiated slowly — so when selling prices rise before costs catch up, producing more is temporarily profitable. In the long run wages catch up and output settles at the economy's potential, set by the productivity fundamentals from earlier.

Now every macro headline becomes a two-step question: which curve moved, and which way?

Shock Curve Output Prices
Stimulus checks, tax cuts, rate cuts AD shifts right ↑ ↑
Oil spike, supply-chain breakdown, war AS shifts left ↓ ↑
Financial crisis, confidence collapse AD shifts left ↓ ↓
Pandemic lockdown Both shift left at once ↓↓ mixed

Real world: 2020–2022 in one framework

The pandemic hit both curves: AS left (factories and services shut) and AD left (spending froze) — output cratered while prices barely moved at first. Then 2021 reversed the demand side violently: trillions in stimulus plus reopening shifted AD hard right while AS was still crippled by supply chains and, in 2022, an energy shock. Demand up plus supply down is the textbook recipe for the inflation surge to ~9% — and the model also explains why it faded as supply chains healed and the Fed leaned on demand.

The supply-shock case is the nasty one, and it earned its own word in the 1970s: stagflation — stagnation plus inflation. It broke the era's favorite empirical relationship, the Phillips curve: through the 1960s, US data traced a tidy trade-off (low unemployment came with higher inflation, and vice versa), and policymakers treated it like a menu. Then the 1973 oil shock shifted AS left and the dots jumped off the curve — high inflation and high unemployment at once, which the "menu" said was impossible.

Deep dive: why the Phillips curve broke — expectations

Milton Friedman and Edmund Phelps predicted the breakdown before it happened, in 1968. Their argument: the trade-off only works while workers are fooled. Run the economy hot and inflation rises; workers eventually notice, demand raises to match, firms' cost advantage evaporates, and unemployment drifts back to its natural rate — but now with permanently higher inflation baked in. Formally, the expectations-augmented version is:

π = πe − b × (u − u*) + supply shocks
π = inflation · πe = expected inflation · u = unemployment · u* = natural rate · b > 0

The policy punchline: the curve is only a usable trade-off when expectations (πe) stay anchored. That is why modern central banks talk obsessively about "anchored expectations" and why they react so aggressively when surveys and market-implied inflation forecasts start drifting — once πe moves, you're fighting the 1970s again. The bond-market gauges of πe live in Section 8.

Reading macro data like an analyst

Here's the part textbooks skip. In markets, macro data isn't trivia — it's a scheduled stream of events that reprice trillions of dollars within seconds of release. The key mechanic: prices already reflect the consensus estimate before the number drops. So markets don't move on whether the data is good; they move on whether it beats or misses expectations. A "bad" jobs number that's less bad than forecast can spark a rally. (Why prices embed expectations is the subject of Section 17 (Market Efficiency).)

Release Cadence Why markets care
CPI Monthly, 8:30 a.m. ET Sets expectations for Fed rate moves; bonds, stocks, and FX reprice within seconds of a surprise.
Jobs report (payrolls + unemployment + wages) First Friday, monthly The single most-watched release; nonfarm payrolls is the headline number of the month.
PCE price index Monthly The Fed's preferred inflation gauge — less famous than CPI, more important to policymakers.
GDP (advance estimate) Quarterly, revised twice Backward-looking, so usually less explosive — unless it surprises on growth direction.
FOMC rate decision 8 meetings/year The policy response itself — the star of Section 4.
ISM manufacturing PMI 1st business day, monthly Survey-based and early — one of the first reads on each month's cycle momentum.
Initial jobless claims Weekly, Thursday The most timely labor signal; noisy, so watch the 4-week average.

Two analyst habits to steal. First, always ask "versus what?" — a payrolls print of +180,000 means nothing until you know consensus was +200,000 (a miss) or +150,000 (a beat). Second, know the regime: in a "good news is bad news" market, a strong jobs report can sink stocks because it implies the Fed keeps rates high. The same number, opposite reaction, depending on what investors fear more — recession or inflation.

Your edge

If you build models on macro data, respect two data-engineering realities. Releases get revised — payrolls are routinely restated by tens of thousands of jobs over the following two months — so backtesting on today's cleaned-up series quietly leaks the future. Serious quant shops use point-in-time ("vintage") datasets that store what was known on each date. And engineer features from surprises (actual minus consensus), not levels: the level was already priced.

Check your understanding

1. Nominal GDP grew 6% last year while the GDP deflator rose 4%. Real growth was roughly…

Real growth ≈ nominal growth minus inflation: exactly 1.06 ÷ 1.04 − 1 ≈ 1.9%, or 2% with the quick subtraction shortcut. Always strip out prices before calling something "growth."

2. Why do most central banks target about 2% inflation rather than 0%?

Zero leaves no safety margin: a small shock tips you into deflation, where falling prices raise real debt burdens and reward waiting instead of spending. A 2% cushion also absorbs the slight upward bias in measured inflation and greases nominal wage adjustment.

3. The unemployment rate falls from 6.3% to 5.8%. Which scenario is consistent with a weaker economy?

The rate only counts people actively looking. If searchers give up, they exit the labor force and the rate falls even though nobody found a job — which is why analysts read the participation rate alongside the headline number.

4. An inverted yield curve is best described as a ___ indicator.

Inversion has preceded every US recession of the past six decades, typically by 6–24 months — the definition of a leading indicator. Lagging indicators like the unemployment rate only confirm turns after the fact.

5. An oil price spike hits the economy. In the AD–AS framework, this…

Oil is an input cost, so it moves the supply curve — leftward, since production gets more expensive at every price level. That pushes output and prices in opposite directions, the signature of a supply shock and of the 1970s.

Key takeaways

  • GDP = C + I + G + NX measures production, with US consumption ~68% of the total. Use real (inflation-adjusted) and per-capita versions for growth and living standards — and remember GDP ignores unpaid work, the environment, and distribution.
  • Growth compounds: 1.5% vs 3% per year turns identical countries into a 2.4× income gap within one lifetime. Long-run growth per person comes almost entirely from productivity.
  • Inflation is a weighted average of basket price changes (CPI). Central banks target ~2% as a buffer against the self-reinforcing deflation trap. Compounding cuts hard: at the 1970s' ~7%, $100 keeps only ~$13 of purchasing power over 30 years.
  • Hyperinflation (>50% per month) is always a fiscal-printing story — Weimar 1923 and Zimbabwe 2008 destroyed their currencies within months.
  • The unemployment rate counts only active job-seekers: read it with labor-force participation, and remember some (frictional) unemployment is healthy churn.
  • Business cycles are recurring but not periodic. Leading indicators (yield curve, permits, claims) turn before the peak; lagging ones (unemployment, CPI) only confirm. NBER dates recessions long after the fact.
  • AD–AS is the diagnostic: demand shocks move output and prices together; supply shocks split them apart (stagflation). It explains 2020–22 in one picture.
  • Markets price the consensus in advance — data moves markets only by surprising, and revisions mean serious analysis needs point-in-time data.

Part I · Economic Foundations

4. Central Banks and Monetary Policy

One committee of twelve people in Washington sets a single interest rate, and within months it moves your mortgage, your savings yield, the stock market, and the value of the dollar. By the end of this section you'll understand how that machine works — and be able to read a Fed headline the way a trader does.

What a central bank is (and is not)

A central bank is a bank for banks and for the government — not for you. You can't open an account at the Federal Reserve. Its customers are commercial banks, which hold accounts there filled with reserves, the electronic cash banks use to settle payments with each other. On top of that plumbing role, a central bank issues the currency, supervises banks, acts as lender of last resort during panics, and — the part markets obsess over — conducts monetary policy: steering interest rates to keep the economy on track.

The United States' central bank is the Federal Reserve (the "Fed"), created in 1913. It's deliberately weird in structure: a seven-member Board of Governors in Washington (appointed by the President, confirmed by the Senate, with staggered 14-year terms) plus twelve regional Federal Reserve Banks (New York, Chicago, San Francisco, and so on). Interest-rate decisions are made by the FOMC (Federal Open Market Committee): the seven governors, the New York Fed president, and four of the other eleven regional presidents on a rotating basis — twelve votes in all, eight scheduled meetings a year.

Congress gave the Fed a dual mandate: stable prices and maximum employment. In practice "stable prices" means inflation around 2% per year, measured by the PCE (Personal Consumption Expenditures) index. The two goals often conflict: cooling inflation usually means slowing the economy, which costs jobs. Monetary policy is the art of trading one against the other, in real time, with noisy data.

Why does everyone insist central banks be independent — free to set rates without political approval? Because politicians face elections, and cheap money feels great for about a year before the inflation bill arrives. The classic cautionary tale: in the early 1970s, Fed chair Arthur Burns kept rates low under pressure from President Nixon, helping ignite the inflation that took a decade — and a brutal recession under chair Paul Volcker, with rates near 20% — to extinguish. Countries with independent central banks reliably have lower, steadier inflation. Independence isn't decoration; it's the whole trick that makes the 2% promise believable.

The pattern is global. The European Central Bank (ECB) sets policy for the 20 euro-area countries, with a single primary mandate — price stability at 2% — and a Governing Council instead of the FOMC. The Bank of England (BoE, founded 1694, the granddaddy of them all) targets 2% inflation through its Monetary Policy Committee. The Bank of Japan (BoJ) spent three decades fighting the opposite problem — deflation — and pioneered nearly every unconventional tool in the modern kit, including quantitative easing (2001) and negative interest rates (2016), which it finally exited in 2024. Different acronyms, same machine.

Key idea

A central bank doesn't set "the" interest rate on your loans directly. It sets one overnight rate at the very base of the system, and lets arbitrage and expectations propagate it outward — like changing a root constant that every other rate in the economy is computed from.

How banks create money (yes, really)

Here's the fact that surprises almost everyone: most money is created not by the government printing it, but by commercial banks making loans. When a bank approves your $900 loan, it doesn't hand you someone else's savings from a vault. It simply credits your account with $900 — a keystroke. Your deposit is brand new money that didn't exist a moment earlier. Loans create deposits, not the other way around.

This works because of fractional-reserve banking: banks keep only a fraction of deposits on hand and put the rest to work. The textbook version chains it into a loop. Say banks hold 10% of each deposit as reserves. You deposit $1,000. Your bank keeps $100 and lends $900. The borrower spends it; the recipient deposits $900 at another bank, which keeps $90 and lends $810. That gets deposited, and $729 gets lent. Each round is 90% of the last — a geometric series, straight out of the same math as Section 1's compounding.

Total deposits = Initial deposit × 1reserve ratio = $1,000 × 10.10 = $10,000
The 1/reserve-ratio factor is the money multiplier — here 10×, so $1,000 of base money supports up to $10,000 of deposits ($9,000 newly created)

Common pitfall: the multiplier is a teaching device

The clean 1/reserve-ratio story is a useful mental model, not a literal description of 2020s banking. The US cut reserve requirements to zero in March 2020, and banks today are limited by capital rules, risk appetite, and whether profitable borrowers exist — not by a reserve count. In the real world causality mostly runs backwards: banks find creditworthy borrowers first, create the deposits, and obtain reserves afterwards. Keep the multiplier as intuition for how banking amplifies base money; don't use it to forecast.

Money creation has a dark twin: money destruction. Because a bank promises deposits back on demand but has locked the funds into long-term loans — a maturity mismatch — no bank can survive everyone asking at once. If depositors merely believe a bank might fail, they rush to withdraw, forcing it to dump assets at fire-sale prices, which causes the failure they feared. That self-fulfilling stampede is a bank run.

Two defenses exist. Deposit insurance — the FDIC (Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation) guarantees up to $250,000 per depositor, per bank — removes the reason for small depositors to run at all. And the central bank stands as lender of last resort for solvent banks caught short. Neither is foolproof: in March 2023, Silicon Valley Bank's clients (mostly startups with balances far above the insured cap) tried to pull $42 billion in a single day, coordinating on Twitter and moving money by app. The mechanics of that failure — an interest-rate bet gone wrong — get a full autopsy in Section 16 (Risk Management), and the wider banking system in Section 6.

The policy rate: what the Fed actually sets

Turn on financial news and you'll hear "the Fed raised rates." The rate in question is the federal funds rate — what banks charge each other for overnight, unsecured loans of reserves. It's the shortest, safest lending rate in the economy, which makes it the base of the entire interest-rate stack. The FOMC doesn't decree a single number; it sets a target range, like 4.25%–4.50%, and then uses its tools to pin the market rate inside it. Rate moves are quoted in basis points (bp): a "25 bp hike" lifts the range by 0.25 percentage points.

How does it pin the rate? The old textbook answer was daily open market operations (OMO): buy Treasury securities to inject reserves and nudge the rate down, sell to drain reserves and nudge it up. That's still the right intuition for how reserves enter the system, but since 2008 the Fed has run an "ample reserves" regime — banks hold trillions in reserves, so tiny daily tweaks to reserve supply no longer move the price. Instead the Fed sets the rate administratively, with two floors:

First, IORB (Interest on Reserve Balances): the Fed simply pays banks interest on reserves parked at the Fed. No bank will lend overnight for less than it can earn risk-free by doing nothing — so IORB acts as a floor for banks. Second, the ON RRP (Overnight Reverse Repurchase) facility does the same job for money-market funds and other non-banks that can't earn IORB: they can always park cash at the Fed at the ON RRP rate, so they won't lend below it either. Move those two administered rates and the whole overnight market moves with them — no daily fine-tuning required. (A third backstop, the discount window, lets banks borrow directly from the Fed at a slightly higher rate, capping spikes.)

Your edge

Think of IORB as a risk-free max() bound in an optimization: no rational lender accepts less than the guaranteed alternative. The entire floor system is one arbitrage constraint, enforced by trillions of self-interested dollars — a distributed system with no central scheduler that still converges every night.

Transmission: one rate moves everything (slowly)

The fed funds rate itself applies only to overnight loans between banks. Its power comes from transmission — the chain reaction that carries it into every corner of the economy. Follow the channels:

Consumer borrowing. Banks price loans off their own funding cost. The prime rate — the benchmark for credit cards and many business loans — sits mechanically at fed funds + 3 points, so every hike passes through within days. Credit-card APRs (prime plus a fat margin) topped 21% on average during the 2023 peak. Thirty-year mortgages price off long-term Treasury yields (see Section 8) rather than the overnight rate, but expectations drag them along: when the Fed turned hawkish in 2022, mortgage rates went from below 3% to about 7% in a year. On a $400,000 loan, that's $1,633 a month at 2.75% versus $2,528 at 6.5% — roughly $322,000 more over the life of the loan. That freezes homebuying, which freezes construction, which cuts jobs.

Business investment. Companies greenlight projects when expected returns beat the cost of capital (Section 14). Raise rates and the hurdle rises; marginal factories, data centers, and startups don't get funded. Asset prices. Stocks and bonds are priced by discounting future cash flows (Section 1, Section 13); a higher discount rate mechanically lowers today's value, hitting long-duration growth stocks hardest — one reason 2022 was brutal for tech. The dollar. Higher US yields attract global capital, bidding up the dollar (Section 10): imports get cheaper (disinflationary), US exports get pricier abroad. Expectations. If firms and workers believe the Fed will keep inflation at 2%, they set prices and wages accordingly — belief does half the work.

Now the catch that makes central banking genuinely hard: the lag. Milton Friedman's famous phrase is that monetary policy works with "long and variable lags" — most estimates say 12–18 months for a hike to fully hit inflation and jobs. The Fed is steering by looking through a delayed feed: today's inflation print reflects decisions from over a year ago. Tighten until inflation visibly breaks and you've almost certainly overdone it.

Analogy

It's a thermostat wired to a radiator with a 15-minute delay. Crank the heat until the room finally feels warm and you've guaranteed a sauna. Good central banking, like good control engineering, means acting on the forecast, not the current reading — and accepting you'll sometimes be wrong in public, at scale.

Monetary policy simulator

Drag the policy rate and watch it ripple through a mortgage, your savings, business borrowing, and the dollar — or jump to a historical moment with the presets.

30-yr mortgage rate (policy + 2.5)5.50%
Monthly payment, $400k loan$2,271/mo
High-yield savings on $10k2.50% · $250/yr
Business loan (prime rate)6.00%
US dollar◆ near neutral
Inflation pressure◆ roughly balanced
Jobs & hiring◆ roughly balanced

Around 2.5–3% is many economists’ guess at the neutral rate — the setting that neither stimulates nor restrains the economy. Nobody can observe it directly; the Fed steers toward it like a driver aiming at a line seen only in the rearview mirror.

Inflation and jobs arrows show the direction of pressure, which takes 12–18 months to fully arrive. Simplifications: mortgages actually price off 10-year Treasury yields plus a spread; the savings figure assumes a high-yield account paying policy minus 0.5 points (big banks often pay far less); prime = policy + 3 points is the real US convention.

Worked example: check the widget's math

Set the slider to 4.00%. The mortgage rate becomes 4.00 + 2.50 = 6.50%. Monthly rate r = 0.065 ÷ 12 = 0.005417, n = 360 payments, so payment = 400,000 × r × (1+r)360 ÷ ((1+r)360 − 1) = $2,528 a month. Same formula as Section 1's annuity math — monetary policy is just the time value of money with a committee attached.

QE and QT: policy by balance sheet

In December 2008 the Fed hit a wall: the fed funds rate reached zero and the economy was still collapsing. You can't meaningfully cut below zero — savers would rather hold cash — a constraint called the zero lower bound. So the Fed reached for a second lever: instead of changing the price of overnight money, change the supply and price of long-term money directly.

That lever is quantitative easing (QE): the central bank buys huge quantities of long-term government bonds and mortgage-backed securities, paying with newly created reserves. Extra demand pushes bond prices up, and — the seesaw you'll master in Section 8 — when a bond's price rises, its yield falls. Lower long-term yields mean cheaper mortgages and corporate debt even when the overnight rate is already at zero. Investors who sold their safe bonds to the Fed then reach for other assets — corporate bonds, stocks — easing conditions further.
