And the purchase program itself is a loud signal that rates will stay low, which shapes expectations.

The reverse gear is quantitative tightening (QT): the Fed lets bonds mature without buying replacements, so reserves quietly drain out of the system month by month. No dramatic sales — more like slowly releasing pressure from a valve, precisely because everyone remembers how violently markets react to abrupt moves (the 2013 "taper tantrum," when merely talking about slowing QE spiked yields).

The scale is worth staring at. Before 2008 the Fed held under $1 trillion in assets. Three rounds of QE took it to about $4.5 trillion by 2014. Then COVID: in 2020 the Fed bought $120 billion of bonds per month at the peak, and the balance sheet touched nearly $9 trillion in 2022 before QT began shaving it back.

Common pitfall: "QE is just printing money, so hyperinflation is coming"

QE creates bank reserves, not wallets full of cash — reserves sit inside the banking system and don't buy groceries. A decade of massive QE (2009–2019) coincided with inflation stubbornly below 2%. The 2021–2022 inflation burst had QE in the mix, but alongside pandemic supply-chain chaos and trillions in direct fiscal transfers (Section 5) — checks that, unlike reserves, really did land in spendable accounts. Be suspicious of one-variable explanations of inflation.

Deep dive: September 2019, when QT broke the plumbing

The first QT experiment ended with a bang. By September 2019 the Fed had drained reserves from about $2.8 trillion to $1.4 trillion — and discovered, live, that "ample" had quietly become "scarce." On September 17, the repo rate (overnight lending against Treasury collateral) spiked from about 2% to nearly 10% intraday as banks ran short of spare cash on a corporate-tax-payment day. The fed funds rate broke above its target range — the one thing the system is built to prevent.

The Fed responded within hours, injecting tens of billions through repo operations and soon resuming asset purchases to rebuild the reserve buffer. The lesson analysts took away: nobody, including the Fed, knows exactly where the minimum comfortable level of reserves sits — so QT is always a game of approaching an invisible cliff slowly. When you hear "reserves are getting scarce" in 2020s Fed coverage, this episode is the ghost in the room.

Reading the Fed like a market participant

Eight times a year, the FOMC meets for two days and releases a statement at 2:00 p.m. Eastern, followed by the chair's press conference at 2:30. Those ninety minutes are the closest thing markets have to a scheduled earthquake. Detailed minutes follow three weeks later, and four meetings a year (March, June, September, December) come with the Summary of Economic Projections (SEP) — including the famous dot plot, where each of the 19 Fed officials anonymously plots where they think the policy rate should sit at the end of each of the next few years. The median dot becomes the headline: "the Fed projects two cuts next year."

Fed-watchers sort officials into hawks — who lean toward higher rates because they weight the inflation mandate — and doves, who lean toward lower rates to protect employment. Handicapping the committee's hawk–dove balance, and who holds a vote this year, is a legitimate full-time job on trading desks.

Why do markets convulse over a single adjective? Because modern monetary policy works substantially through forward guidance — communication about the future path of rates. Ten-year yields, mortgage rates, and stock valuations depend on the expected path over years, not tonight's rate. So when the Fed's statement drops the word "patient," or the chair says "some time" instead of "considerable time," markets reprice the entire path within seconds. The current setting is almost old news; the path is the product. Traders read expectations straight off fed funds futures — that's what a headline like "markets price a 78% chance of a March cut" means (futures live in Section 9).

Your edge

Traders literally run a diff on the FOMC statement — news wires publish red-lined versions minutes after release, word-level changes highlighted like a pull request. With your background you could build that pipeline in an afternoon: scrape the statements, diff the token streams, timestamp the changes against tick data. That's an event study, and it's a standard quant research pattern you'll meet again in Section 18.

A practical reading list for any Fed event, in the order the market processes it: (1) the rate decision itself — usually fully priced in advance; (2) the statement diff — what changed since last time; (3) the dot plot, if it's a SEP meeting — where the median moved; (4) the press conference — where an unscripted sentence can move markets more than the decision did. If the Fed does exactly what futures predicted and says nothing new, prices barely move; surprise, not action, is what moves markets — a theme Section 17 makes precise.

Real world: one word, trillions repriced

In January 2015 the Swiss National Bank abandoned its currency cap with no warning at all; the franc moved ~20% against the euro in minutes and several brokers went insolvent. That's the extreme case of why central banks now telegraph moves — and why, when a Fed chair merely hints ("we're not even thinking about thinking about raising rates" — Jerome Powell, 2020), it is front-page financial news.

Check your understanding

1. The Fed's dual mandate consists of which two goals?

Congress assigned the Fed price stability (interpreted as ~2% PCE inflation) and maximum employment. The dollar, stocks, and specific loan rates are affected by policy but are not mandate targets.

2. When a commercial bank makes a new loan, what happens to the money supply?

Loans create deposits: the bank credits the borrower's account with new money that didn't exist before. This is how most money in the economy comes into being.

3. How does the Fed keep the federal funds rate inside its target range today?

In the modern ample-reserves "floor system," the Fed pays interest on reserves (IORB) and offers the ON RRP facility to non-banks; arbitrage keeps the market rate pinned near those administered rates. Reserve requirements are zero, and daily fine-tuning OMO is history.

4. The Fed hikes rates by a surprise 100 basis points. Which reaction is most likely, all else equal?

Higher US yields attract foreign capital (stronger dollar) and make existing lower-coupon bonds less attractive (prices fall as yields rise). The inflation effect is real but takes 12–18 months, not a month.

5. What does the dot plot show?

Each of the 19 officials anonymously marks where they think rates should be at future year-ends; the median is quoted as "the Fed's projection." It's a forecast snapshot, not a promise — and market-implied odds come from fed funds futures instead.

Key takeaways

  • A central bank is the bank for banks and the government; the Fed's FOMC sets policy under a dual mandate of ~2% inflation (PCE) and maximum employment, and its independence is what makes the inflation target credible.
  • Most money is created by commercial banks when loans create deposits; the money multiplier (1 ÷ reserve ratio) is useful intuition, but modern banks are constrained by capital and loan demand, not reserve counts.
  • Bank runs are self-fulfilling panics born of maturity mismatch; deposit insurance (FDIC, $250k) and the lender of last resort exist to prevent them — SVB 2023 showed the app-era version.
  • The federal funds rate is an overnight bank-to-bank rate steered through a floor system: IORB for banks, ON RRP for money funds, with rates quoted in basis points (25 bp = 0.25%).
  • Transmission runs through mortgages, credit cards (prime = policy + 3), business hurdle rates, asset prices, and the dollar — with a 12–18 month lag that forces the Fed to steer by forecast.
  • QE buys long-term bonds with new reserves to push long yields down when the policy rate hits zero; QT lets them run off. It is not literal money-printing into wallets, and its inflation effect is contested.
  • Markets trade the expected path of rates, not the current level — so statement wording, dot plots, and press-conference phrasing move prices more than the decision itself.

Part I · Economic Foundations

5. Fiscal Policy, Government Debt and Global Trade

Central banks steer the economy by setting the price of money; governments steer it by taxing, spending and borrowing — and every country does all of this while trading with everyone else. By the end of this section you'll be able to judge whether a national debt is sustainable, explain why trade isn't a scoreboard, and read a headline about tariffs or a falling currency without flinching.

The government's other lever: taxing and spending

Fiscal policy is the demand-side lever that Section 4 didn't cover. Where the central bank changes the price of borrowing, the government changes spending and taxes directly: build a bridge, cut payroll taxes, mail out checks. The money lands in specific hands immediately — no waiting for banks to pass a rate cut along.

Some of it happens with no vote at all. Automatic stabilizers are built-in negative feedback: in a recession, incomes fall, so tax bills fall, while unemployment insurance and food assistance payments rise — the deficit widens on its own and props up spending. In a boom the same machinery runs in reverse. Discretionary stimulus — the CARES Act of 2020, the 2009 Recovery Act — requires Congress to act, which means recognition lags, debate lags and implementation lags.

Analogy: autoscaling vs paging the on-call

Automatic stabilizers are autoscaling — traffic spikes, capacity adjusts, nobody gets paged. Discretionary stimulus is paging a human to provision servers by hand: potentially much bigger, but slow, and sometimes the extra capacity arrives after the traffic spike has passed. That timing risk is a core critique of discretionary fiscal policy.

How much bang per dollar? The fiscal multiplier captures the knock-on effects. Pay a contractor $100 billion to build roads; the contractor's workers spend part of their new income at restaurants; the restaurant owners spend part of theirs; and so on. If each recipient spends 60 cents of every marginal dollar — a marginal propensity to consume of 0.6 — the naive total is a geometric series: 100 × (1 + 0.6 + 0.36 + …) = 100 ÷ (1 − 0.6) = $250 billion. You know this series; it's the same recursion as expected visits in a Markov chain.

Real-world estimates land far below 2.5, and they disagree — for structural reasons, not sloppy econometrics. Money leaks out each round into savings, taxes and imports. If the economy is already at full capacity, extra demand mostly bids up prices instead of output. And if the central bank fears inflation, it raises rates to offset the stimulus, deliberately shrinking the multiplier toward zero. Empirical estimates cluster around 0.5–1.5: highest in deep recessions with rates stuck near zero (nothing to crowd out, no Fed pushback), lowest in booms. When you see two economists quote different multipliers, ask what state of the world each one assumed.

Deficit vs debt: a flow and a stock

These two words get mangled daily, and the distinction is one you already know from programming. The budget deficit is a flow — the shortfall this year, like the delta in one iteration of a loop. The national debt is a stock — the accumulator variable. The deficit is the derivative; the debt is the integral. A government can shrink its deficit every year while the debt still grows, exactly as a function can rise while its slope falls.

Raw dollar figures are nearly useless — "$36 trillion of debt" tells you nothing without a denominator, just as "500ms latency" means nothing without knowing the workload. The right scale is the debt-to-GDP ratio: debt divided by the annual income of the whole economy (Section 3). A household owing $360,000 is drowning on a $40,000 income and comfortable on $900,000. US federal debt sits around 120% of GDP in the mid-2020s gross; Japan carries roughly 240%; Germany about 63%.

Who is actually owed the money? Mostly not foreign rivals — mostly the country's own institutions. US federal debt takes the form of Treasury securities, which you'll meet properly in Section 8 (Fixed Income). The rough breakdown:

Holder of US federal debt Rough share (mid-2020s) What that means
US government accounts (Social Security trust fund, etc.) ~20% One arm of the government owes another
Federal Reserve ~12% Bought during QE (Section 4); interest largely flows back to the Treasury
US private investors — funds, banks, pensions, insurers, households ~43% Americans' own savings, parked in the safe asset
Foreign investors (Japan, China, UK among the largest) ~25% The mirror image of trade flows — see the balance of payments below

Notice that "we owe it to ourselves" is three-quarters true for the US. That doesn't make debt free — interest payments still redistribute from taxpayers to bondholders — but it changes what kind of problem it is.

Can a government go broke?

It depends entirely on what currency the debt is in. A government that borrows in its own currency — the US, Japan, the UK — can always print the money to pay; a technical sovereign default on own-currency debt is a choice (or a political accident, like a debt-ceiling standoff), never an arithmetic necessity. A government that borrows in someone else's currency can genuinely run out. Argentina's dollar debts have defaulted repeatedly; Greece in 2010 discovered that the euro is effectively a foreign currency for its members — Athens can't print euros — and its private creditors eventually took a haircut of more than 50% (more in Section 17).

"Can always print" is not "can safely print." Covering deficits with newly created money — monetization — taxes everyone who holds the currency through inflation, a revenue stream economists call seigniorage. Inflation is the escape valve, and it works: it quietly shrinks the real value of old fixed-rate debt. But push it hard and you get the costs from Section 3 — scrambled price signals, savaged savers, and eventually lenders who demand double-digit yields or refuse your currency altogether. Weimar Germany and 2018 Venezuela are the valve jammed fully open.

There's a quieter cost even without an inflation crisis. Crowding out is the claim that heavy government borrowing competes with companies for the same pool of savings, raising interest rates and displacing private investment — the factory not built because the savings bought Treasuries instead. It bites hardest at full employment, when resources are genuinely scarce; in a deep recession with idle savings, there may be little to crowd out. This is precisely why the multiplier debate above never resolves.

Real world: markets discipline even money-printers

In September 2022 the UK — an own-currency borrower — announced large unfunded tax cuts. Within days, 30-year government bond yields jumped by more than a full percentage point, the pound slumped, and pension funds hit margin spirals severe enough that the Bank of England intervened. The prime minister was gone within weeks. No default, no arithmetic crisis — just the bond market repricing fiscal credibility in real time. Own-currency status buys patience, not immunity.

Debt sustainability: the r vs g race

Here is the single most useful equation in fiscal policy. Let d be the debt-to-GDP ratio, r the average nominal interest rate the government pays, g the nominal growth rate of GDP, and the primary deficit the gap between spending and revenue before interest. Then the ratio evolves as:

dt+1 = dt × 1 + r1 + g + primary deficit
d = debt ÷ GDP · r = avg nominal interest rate on debt · g = nominal GDP growth · primary deficit as % of GDP

The intuition: interest makes the numerator (debt) compound at r, while growth plus inflation make the denominator (GDP) compound at g. If g exceeds r, the ratio has a built-in shrink factor every year — the economy outgrows its old debts — and even a permanent modest deficit just glides toward a finite plateau. If r exceeds g, interest compounds faster than income, and only primary surpluses can hold the line.

Worked example: a 30-year trajectory

Start at debt = 100% of GDP, with r = 2%, g = 6% (say 3.5% real growth + 2.5% inflation), and a primary deficit of 1% of GDP, forever. Year 1: 100 × (1.02 ÷ 1.06) + 1 = 97.2%. Year 2: 97.2 × 0.9623 + 1 = 94.6%. After 10 years: 76.5%. After 30: 49.7% — half the starting ratio, despite never once balancing the budget. The path is gliding toward a plateau of 1 ÷ (1 − 0.9623) = 26.5%. Growth did all the work.

This is exactly how the United States escaped its Second World War debts. In 1946, federal debt held by the public stood near 106% of GDP; by 1974 it was about 23%. Washington never "paid it off" — primary budgets were only roughly balanced. Instead, nominal GDP grew around 7% a year while the average interest rate on the debt stayed near 2–3%, helped by financial repression: rate caps and regulations that kept captive savers in low-yielding bonds. Each year the denominator quietly outran the numerator. The opposite regime — austerity with r above g — is a grind: Italy ran primary surpluses through most of the 1990s and 2000s and barely held its ratio flat, because high rates kept refilling the tub as fast as it drained.

Now run the race yourself. The simulator below iterates the equation above for 30 years — try the presets first, then find the knife-edge where a path flips from plateau to runaway.

Debt dynamics simulator

Drag r, g and the primary deficit and watch 30 years of debt/GDP unfold — start with the presets, then try to build your own doom loop.

Debt/GDP in year 30—
Change vs today—
Long-run plateau—

Deep dive: deriving the debt equation

Let D be debt in dollars, Y nominal GDP, and PD the primary deficit in dollars. Each year the debt grows by interest plus new borrowing: Dt+1 = Dt(1 + r) + PD. GDP grows as Yt+1 = Yt(1 + g). Divide the first equation by the second and write d = D ÷ Y, pd = PD ÷ Y:

dt+1 = dt × 1 + r1 + g + pd1 + g
The simulator drops the (1+g) under pd — a <0.5% approximation at realistic rates.

This is a linear recurrence dt+1 = a·dt + b with a = (1+r)/(1+g). From any discrete-systems course: it converges to the fixed point b ÷ (1 − a) when |a| < 1 (that is, g > r) and diverges when a > 1 (r > g). The whole sustainability debate is a fight about one eigenvalue.

Trade: why it isn't zero-sum

Switch from one government's books to the links between countries. The deepest result in trade economics is comparative advantage: both sides gain from trade even when one of them is better at producing everything. What matters is not who is best — absolute advantage — but each country's opportunity cost, the concept from Section 2.

Take two countries, Acadia and Bolandia, making chips and shirts with 120 labor-hours each. Acadia needs 2 hours per chip and 3 per shirt; Bolandia needs 8 and 4. Acadia is faster at both. But every chip costs Acadia only ⅔ of a shirt, while it costs Bolandia 2 shirts — so Acadia should make chips, and Bolandia, whose shirts cost it only half a chip, should make shirts. Under autarky, splitting hours evenly, the world produces 37.5 chips and 35 shirts. Reassign the same 240 hours — Acadia mostly chips, Bolandia all shirts — and output rises to 45 chips and 40 shirts. Nothing new was invented; the schedule just improved. If they then trade 10 chips for 12 shirts, Acadia ends with 35 chips + 22 shirts (vs 30 + 20 alone) and Bolandia with 10 chips + 18 shirts (vs 7.5 + 15). Both consume more of both goods. Any price between ⅔ and 2 shirts per chip makes both better off — this is a positive-sum assignment problem, not a tug-of-war.

So why is trade politically radioactive? Because the gains are diffuse and the losses are concentrated. Every consumer saves a little on cheaper shirts; the shirt-town in Acadia loses its factory, and its workers can't costlessly become chip designers. The "China shock" research found that US regions exposed to Chinese import competition after 2000 suffered deep, persistent hits to employment and wages. Trade grows the pie and simultaneously creates identifiable losers — both statements are true, and honest policy debates are about compensation and transition, not about denying either half.

Enter the tariff — a tax on imports, the classic tool of protectionism. The crucial question is incidence: who really pays? The exporter writes no check to US customs; the importer does, and studies of the 2018–19 US tariffs found the cost passed through almost entirely into US prices. Consumers pay more; protected domestic producers gain some; the government collects revenue; and two slivers of value — output produced at higher cost than the world price, and purchases deterred by the artificial markup — vanish entirely as deadweight loss. One famous estimate: the 2018 washing-machine tariffs preserved manufacturing jobs at a consumer cost of roughly $800,000 per job per year.

The balance of payments: a forced mirror

Every cross-border transaction a country makes is recorded in its balance of payments — and it is literally double-entry bookkeeping at national scale, which means it contains an identity you can lean on like a conservation law. The current account tracks trade in goods and services plus cross-border income; the capital account (formally the financial account) tracks who bought what assets. By construction, the two must mirror: current account + capital account = 0.

Follow one import. An American buys a $1,000 machine from Osaka. The exporter now holds 1,000 US dollars, which are useless in Japan until they come back — as a purchase of something American. If not American goods (that would cancel the deficit), then American assets: Treasuries, Apple shares, a Cleveland apartment block. So a trade deficit is not money leaking into the void. It means the country is consuming and investing more than it produces, with foreigners financing the gap by accumulating claims on it. Equivalently — one identity, rearranged — a current-account deficit equals the gap between domestic investment and domestic savings.

Common pitfall: reading the deficit as a scoreboard

A trade deficit is not "losing at trade," and a surplus is not winning — Germany ran huge surpluses through a decade of stagnant wages, while the US ran huge deficits through its strongest growth runs. The deficit is an accounting mirror of savings and investment choices, and its twin is foreigners' eagerness to hold US assets. Judge it by what the borrowed resources bought: productive investment is a very different story from consumption.

The US case has one more twist: the dollar is the world's reserve currency, so global demand for safe dollar assets is enormous and structural. Foreigners line up to buy Treasuries; that capital inflow is the mirror image of the persistent US trade deficit — and it circles back to the table above showing who holds the debt.

Exchange rates: floating, pegged, and the Big Mac test

An exchange rate is just a price — yen per dollar — set in the largest market on Earth, with over $7 trillion changing hands daily. Under a floating exchange rate (the dollar, euro, yen, pound), that price moves continuously with supply and demand. Under a currency peg (the Hong Kong dollar, the Gulf currencies against the dollar), the central bank promises a fixed rate and stands ready to buy or sell reserves to defend it. Pegs deliver stability until they don't: a peg abandoned under speculative attack — Britain in 1992, Thailand in 1997 — snaps in a violent devaluation.

What moves a floating currency? Four forces dominate. Interest-rate differentials: if US rates rise above Japan's, holding dollars pays better, capital flows in, the dollar strengthens — connecting FX directly to the central-bank decisions of Section 4. Inflation differentials: a country inflating at 30% a year watches its currency lose purchasing power and, eventually, exchange value. Trade and capital flows: persistent demand for a country's exports or assets bids up its currency. And risk appetite: in a panic, money stampedes into dollars, yen and Swiss francs — the safe haven currencies — regardless of fundamentals.

The long-run anchor is purchasing power parity (PPP): identical goods should cost the same everywhere once converted, or arbitrage would fix it. The Economist's Big Mac index turns this into a joke that works: the burger is a standardized basket of tradable ingredients, local labor and rent. Suppose a Big Mac costs $5.80 in the US and ¥480 in Japan. The PPP exchange rate is 480 ÷ 5.80 ≈ 83 yen per dollar; if the market rate is 150, the yen looks about 45% undervalued against burger parity. PPP deviations persist for years — haircuts and rent don't ship — but the index reliably flags extremes, and currencies do drift toward PPP over decades.

Key idea: closing Part I — the four macro levers

You now hold the full demand-management toolkit. Monetary policy (Section 4) works through the price of money: policy rates and the central bank's balance sheet. Fiscal policy works through the government's budget: taxes and spending. Markets referee both in real time — the bond market prices fiscal credibility into yields (Section 8), the currency market prices the policy mix (Section 10), and equities price the growth consequences (Section 7). When both levers push the same way, expect regime-sized moves: 2021's floored rates plus trillions of stimulus fed the inflation that Section 4's tightening then had to break. Every macro headline you'll ever read is some combination of these four dials — and Part II begins with the machine that transmits them: the financial system.

Your edge: macro data is free and clean

Everything in this section is public, well-curated time series: FRED (fred.stlouisfed.org) has debt-to-GDP, deficits and interest costs; the BEA publishes the full balance of payments; the BIS posts effective exchange rates. Rebuilding Figure 5.1's quadrants from raw FRED series, or backtesting the r-vs-g recurrence against actual US history, is an afternoon in pandas — and exactly the kind of portfolio piece that shows a hiring manager you can connect an equation to a dataset.

Check your understanding

1. Which of these is a stock rather than a flow?

The debt is the accumulated total — the integral. Deficits, growth and interest payments are all per-period flows that feed into it.

2. A country has debt at 100% of GDP, pays r = 3% on it, grows nominally at g = 5%, and runs a primary balance of exactly zero. Over time its debt ratio will…

With a zero primary balance, the ratio is multiplied by (1.03 ÷ 1.05) ≈ 0.981 each year. Growth outruns interest, so the ratio melts — the post-WW2 US mechanism.

3. Acadia produces both chips and shirts more efficiently than Bolandia. Why does Acadia still gain from trading with it?

Comparative advantage runs on opportunity cost, not absolute efficiency. Every hour Acadia spends on shirts is an hour not spent on chips, where its edge is far bigger — so it pays to buy shirts from Bolandia.

4. The US runs a $900 billion trade deficit this year. Which statement must also be true?

The balance of payments is double-entry: a current-account deficit is mirrored, dollar for dollar, by a capital-account surplus. The dollars spent on imports return as purchases of Treasuries, stocks and property.

5. Studies of the 2018–19 US tariffs found that the cost was borne mostly by…

Pass-through was close to complete: US import prices rose by roughly the tariff amount. That's incidence — the legal payer and the economic payer are different questions, and revenue never offsets the deadweight loss.

Key takeaways

  • Fiscal policy moves demand directly through taxes and spending; automatic stabilizers act instantly, discretionary stimulus is powerful but slow, and the multiplier depends on slack, leakages and the central bank's reaction.
  • Deficit is the flow, debt is the stock — and only debt-to-GDP, not raw dollars, tells you whether the burden is heavy.
  • An own-currency sovereign can't be forced into arithmetic default, but inflation is the escape valve and markets can still repunish fiscal recklessness through yields and the currency.
  • Debt sustainability hinges on r vs g: when growth exceeds the interest rate, even permanent modest deficits plateau; when r > g, only primary surpluses prevent a doom loop — the post-WW2 US grew out of 106% debt/GDP without ever repaying it.
  • Comparative advantage makes trade positive-sum even against a country that's better at everything, but the gains are diffuse while the losses are concentrated on specific workers and towns.
  • Tariff incidence usually lands on domestic consumers, with part of the loss pure deadweight — value destroyed rather than transferred.
  • The balance of payments is double-entry bookkeeping: a trade deficit is exactly matched by foreigners accumulating claims, so it reflects savings-versus-investment choices, not "losing."
  • Exchange rates respond to rate differentials, inflation gaps, trade flows and risk appetite; purchasing power parity — Big Mac included — anchors them only over long horizons.

Part II · Financial Markets & Instruments

6. The Financial System: A Guided Tour

Before you study stocks, bonds, or derivatives, you need the map: who the players are, what each one does, and what actually happens in the seconds after you tap Buy. This section is your orientation tour — by the end you will be able to place any headline, job posting, or trade confirmation on the map, and you will have run your own orders through a live order book.

The core job: routing money from savers to users

Strip away the glass towers and the jargon, and the financial system does one job: it moves money from people who have more than they currently need (savers) to people who can put it to productive use (users of capital — firms building factories, governments building roads, households buying homes). In exchange, the users hand back a security or a promise: a share, a bond, a loan agreement — a claim on future cash.

There are exactly two routes the money can take. In direct finance, you buy the claim yourself in a market: you purchase Apple shares or a Treasury bond, and your money flows (roughly) straight to the firm or government. In indirect finance, you hand your money to an intermediary — a bank, a pension fund, an insurer — and it decides who gets funded. Your checking account is indirect finance: the bank pools your deposit with millions of others and lends the pool out as mortgages and business loans.

Why do intermediaries exist at all, when a market could match savers and borrowers directly? Three reasons, all of which should ring a bell from Section 2 (Microeconomics):

Information. Lending suffers from information asymmetry: the borrower knows far more about their repayment odds than you do. A bank employs credit analysts, sees the borrower's cash flows, and screens thousands of applications — it amortizes the cost of due diligence over a huge loan book, the way one trained ML model serves millions of queries.

Maturity transformation. Savers want their money back on demand; borrowers want it for 30 years. A bank performs maturity transformation — it borrows short (your deposit, withdrawable today) and lends long (a 30-year mortgage), betting that not everyone withdraws at once. This is enormously useful and inherently fragile: when everyone does withdraw at once, you get a bank run — the failure mode behind much of Section 17 (Crises).

Diversification. With $1,000 you can fund a slice of one borrower; a fund with $10 billion holds thousands of loans or stocks, so one default barely dents it. Pooling gives small savers diversification they could never build alone — the statistics of this are the heart of Section 15 (Portfolio Theory).

Analogy

Think of the financial system as the internet's routing layer for money. Savers are clients, users of capital are servers, markets are peer-to-peer connections, and intermediaries are load balancers: they pool traffic, hide the messy topology, and add redundancy — at the cost of a fee and a new single point of failure.

The cast, mapped

Financial headlines assume you know who everyone is. Here is the full cast in one table — skim it now, then use it as a lookup while you read the rest of Part II. Every row is either a channel for money, a service provider to the channels, or infrastructure underneath them.

Player What it does How it makes money
Commercial bank Takes deposits, makes loans, runs payments (JPMorgan Chase's retail arm, Bank of America) Interest spread: charge borrowers more than it pays depositors
Investment bank Helps firms issue stocks/bonds, advises on M&A, trades (Goldman Sachs, Morgan Stanley) Fees on deals and underwriting; trading revenue
Asset manager Runs mutual funds, index funds, ETFs for the public (BlackRock, Vanguard, Fidelity) Management fee: a % of assets per year
Hedge fund Flexible, often leveraged strategies for institutions and the wealthy (Citadel, Bridgewater) Classically “2 and 20”: 2% of assets + 20% of profits
Private equity / VC PE buys whole companies to restructure; VC funds startups (Blackstone, KKR; Sequoia, a16z) Fees plus a share of exit profits (“carry”)
Pension fund Invests retirement savings over decades (CalPERS) Not-for-profit for members; managers earn fees
Insurer Collects premiums, invests the pool (“float”), pays claims (Allianz, MetLife) Underwriting margin + investment returns on the float
Sovereign wealth fund Invests a nation's surplus (Norway's $1.7T oil fund, Singapore's GIC) Returns accrue to the state
Exchange Runs the marketplace and publishes prices (NYSE, Nasdaq, CME) Trading fees, listing fees, and — increasingly — selling market data
Broker Routes customer orders to venues (Schwab, Interactive Brokers, Robinhood) Commissions, interest on cash, payment for order flow
Market maker Quotes two-sided prices all day so you can always trade (Citadel Securities, Jane Street) Earns the bid–ask spread, many thousands of times a day
Custodian Safekeeps assets, tracks ownership records (BNY, State Street) Small fees on enormous asset piles
Rating agency Grades bond issuers' default risk (Moody's, S&P, Fitch) — see Section 8 Issuers pay to be rated (a conflict worth remembering)
Data vendor Sells prices, news, analytics (Bloomberg, Refinitiv, FactSet) Subscriptions — a Bloomberg Terminal runs ~$30k/year

Real world: scale

BlackRock alone manages over $11 trillion — more than the GDP of every country except the US and China. When you hear “institutional investors,” picture a handful of asset managers, pension funds, and sovereign funds that collectively own most of the stock market. Retail investors — individuals like you — are the minority of volume in most markets.

The referees: who regulates what

In Section 2 you met market failures: information asymmetry, externalities, monopoly power. Financial regulation is the institutional response to exactly those failures. Securities rules force disclosure (fixing information asymmetry); bank capital rules limit systemic risk (a negative externality — a bank's collapse hurts people who never banked there); antitrust and market-structure rules police concentrated venues. Each US regulator owns one slice:

Regulator Watches Exists because…
SEC Securities markets: stocks, bonds, funds, disclosure Investors can't verify claims themselves — so issuers must publish audited truth (see Section 11)
Federal Reserve Banks' safety, plus monetary policy (Section 4) Bank failures cascade; someone must be lender of last resort
FDIC Insures bank deposits up to $250,000 per depositor, per bank Deposit insurance stops bank runs before they start
FINRA Brokers and their salespeople (industry-funded self-regulator) Front-line policing of the people who touch retail money
CFTC Derivatives and commodities futures (Section 9) Leverage in derivatives can blow holes in the real economy
FCA (UK) / ESMA (EU) Rough overseas equivalents of the SEC + FINRA Same failures, different jurisdictions

Notice the pattern: no single regulator sees everything. A bank holding company might answer to the Fed for its balance sheet, the SEC for its brokerage arm, the CFTC for its swaps desk, and the FDIC for its deposits. This fragmentation is a recurring plot device in crisis post-mortems — risky activity migrates to whichever corner is watched least, a phenomenon called regulatory arbitrage.

Buy side vs sell side: the industry's two halves

Practitioners split the industry into two camps, and the split shows up in every job posting you will ever read. The buy side manages money: asset managers, hedge funds, pension funds, PE/VC, insurers, sovereign funds. Its product is investment performance, and it earns fees on the assets it runs. The sell side sells services to the money: investment banks, brokers, market makers, and research desks. Its products are execution, new securities, advice, and analysis, and it earns fees, commissions, and spreads.

The economics differ sharply. Buy-side revenue scales with assets under management — a fund charging 0.5% on $50 billion earns $250 million a year whether markets are open or not. Sell-side revenue scales with activity: deals closed, trades executed, spreads captured. That is why sell-side hiring booms with deal cycles while big asset managers grind on steadily.

Career-wise: the sell side hires analysts into investment banking (building deal models, brutal hours), sales & trading (pricing and executing), and research (writing on companies — the skills of Sections 12–13). The buy side hires portfolio managers, research analysts, and — in force since the 2010s — quantitative researchers and data scientists who build signals from data (Section 18). Sell-side experience is a classic on-ramp to buy-side seats; Section 20 (Career Roadmap) maps the paths in detail.

Your edge

Data roles now live on both sides: market makers and hedge funds run some of the most demanding ML and low-latency engineering anywhere, while banks and asset managers hire heavily for risk analytics, surveillance, and client data. When you read a posting, first ask: buy side or sell side? That single bit tells you the business model, the pace, and how your work gets monetized.

Primary vs secondary markets: issuance and trading

Every security lives two lives. It is born in the primary market, where the issuer sells it and receives the cash. It then spends decades in the secondary market, changing hands between investors while the issuer gets nothing further. When Apple stock trades $10 billion in a day, Apple's bank account doesn't move a cent.

The flagship primary-market event is the IPO. A private company hires investment banks to run underwriting: the banks value the company, write the prospectus (a legal disclosure document filed with the SEC — readable free at sec.gov/edgar), drum up demand from buy-side institutions in a roadshow, set the price, and buy the shares from the company to resell to investors. Bond issuance works the same way but faster and more routine — large firms tap the bond market the way you'd call an API, sometimes raising billions in an afternoon (Section 8).

Worked example: IPO economics

A company sells 10 million new shares at $20: gross proceeds $200 million. The underwriters' fee (the gross spread) is a typical 7%, or $14 million, so the company nets $186 million. If the stock closes its first day at $26, that 30% “pop” means the company arguably left 10M × $6 = $60 million on the table — money captured by the investors who got IPO allocations instead of by the firm. Underpricing is one of the best-documented puzzles in finance.

Why does the secondary market matter so much if issuers get nothing from it? Because liquidity in the secondary market is what makes the primary market possible. You'll only buy a 30-year bond at issue if you know you can sell it Tuesday. Secondary prices also do the economy's homework: they continuously re-value every firm, which tells capital where to flow next — the price-signal machinery from Section 2 operating at millisecond resolution. How stocks and their exchanges work in detail is Section 7.

Common pitfall

“I bought shares, so I gave the company money.” Only in the primary market. Buying AAPL on the secondary market sends your money to whoever sold you the shares — the company was paid once, at issuance, possibly decades ago.

Market microstructure: inside the order book

Now zoom all the way in, to the data structure at the center of every modern market: the order book. It is literally two priority queues. On one side sit bids — resting offers to buy, sorted highest-first. On the other sit asks — resting offers to sell, sorted lowest-first. The gap between the best bid and best ask is the bid–ask spread, and the total size resting at each price level is the book's depth.

You interact with the book through two order types. A market order says “fill me now at whatever prices are available” — it consumes resting orders starting from the best price. A limit order says “fill me only at this price or better” — if nobody's willing, it joins the queue and waits. Matching follows price-time priority: best price wins, and among equal prices, first-come-first-served — a stable sort you could implement in an afternoon, running at nanosecond scale.

Worked example: walking the book

Using Figure 6.3's book, send a market buy for 1,000 shares. You lift 200 @ $100.05, then 400 @ $100.10, then 300 @ $100.15, then 100 @ $100.20. Average fill = (200×100.05 + 400×100.10 + 300×100.15 + 100×100.20) ÷ 1,000 = $100,115 ÷ 1,000 = $100.115 — 6.5¢ per share worse than the best ask, a $65 cost known as slippage. Your own order moved the market: that's price impact, and it's why large investors slice big orders into thousands of small ones.

Who keeps the queues populated? Market makers. They post bids and asks simultaneously, hoping to buy at $99.95 and sell at $100.05 all day — earning the spread as compensation for the risk that prices lurch while they're holding inventory. Most are now HFT firms whose edge is speed: co-located servers, microwave links between cities, FPGA-parsed market data. The good: spreads on liquid US stocks fell from ~12 cents in the 1990s to ~1 cent today. The debated: whether the speed race spends real resources winning races that don't help anyone — Section 17 revisits this, including the 2010 Flash Crash.

Key idea

The spread is the price of immediacy. Market orders pay it; limit orders earn it (in exchange for execution risk — the market may run away without filling you). Every execution strategy in existence is a tradeoff along this line.

Order book simulator

Send orders into a live book. Try the default (market buy, 600 shares) and check the average fill — then send a 2,000-share market order and watch it walk the book.

Last price$100.00
Bid–ask spread$0.10
Avg fill price—
Price impact—

Book seeded: 5 bid levels, 5 ask levels around $100. Submit an order to begin.

Play with three scenarios. First, the default market buy of 600 fills 200 @ $100.05 and 400 @ $100.10 — average $100.0833, already a third of a cent of slippage. Second, a limit buy of 500 at $100.05 fills the 200 on offer and leaves 300 resting as the new best bid, tightening the spread to $0.05 — you just provided liquidity. Third, hammer the book with repeated 2,000-share market buys and watch the ask side evaporate and the last price climb: that is price impact made visible, and it's exactly what execution algorithms are built to minimize.

A trade, end to end

You tap Buy on your phone. What actually happens? More than you'd think, across three very different timescales.

Milliseconds — routing and execution. Your broker's systems validate the order and route it to an execution venue: a public exchange, or (for most retail orders) a wholesale market maker who pays the broker for the privilege. The venue's matching engine crosses your order against the book, and you get a confirmation before your thumb leaves the screen. Economically, the shares are yours from this instant.

Hours — clearing. Overnight, a clearing house (in US stocks, the NSCC) performs novation: it inserts itself as buyer to every seller and seller to every buyer, so nobody needs to trust a stranger. It also does netting — a firm that bought 5 million shares and sold 4.9 million settles only the net 100,000. Netting routinely shrinks settlement obligations by ~98%, a compression trick any data engineer will appreciate.

One day — settlement and custody. On T+1 (trade date plus one business day, the US standard since May 2024), settlement happens: cash and shares swap at the Depository Trust Company, the central vault where nearly all US securities live as electronic entries. Your broker's custodian records you as beneficial owner. The whole apparatus exists to crush counterparty risk — the risk that the stranger on the other side simply doesn't pay.

Real world: January 2021

When meme-stock volumes exploded, the clearing house demanded billions more in collateral (margin) from brokers to cover the two-day settlement gap that then existed. Several brokers restricted buying in GME because they literally couldn't post the deposit fast enough. The invisible plumbing became front-page news — and the episode accelerated the US move from T+2 to T+1.

Deep dive: where your “free” retail order really goes

Most US retail brokers charge zero commission because of payment for order flow (PFOF): wholesale market makers like Citadel Securities pay the broker a fraction of a cent per share to execute your order internally rather than send it to an exchange. Why pay for it? Retail orders are “uninformed” in the statistical sense — a random retail buy predicts nothing about the next price move, so making markets against retail is safer than quoting on an exchange where sharper counterparties lurk. Wholesalers typically fill retail slightly inside the public quote (price improvement of a fraction of a cent), and brokers must report execution quality. Critics argue the improvement is measured against a quote the wholesalers themselves help set, and that the conflict — your broker is paid by your counterparty — deserves more scrutiny; the EU banned PFOF outright from 2026. Either way, “free” means the spread, not the commission, is where the money moves.

Zoom back out. You now hold the whole map: two channels (markets and intermediaries), a cast of institutions on the buy and sell sides, referees for each arena, a primary market where securities are born, a secondary market where an order book prices them tick by tick, and a clearing-and-settlement pipeline that makes strangers safe to trade with. The next four sections walk through what actually flows through these pipes: equities, bonds, derivatives, and currencies, commodities & alternatives.

Check your understanding

1. A bank funds 30-year mortgages using deposits customers can withdraw at any time. This is called…

Borrowing short (deposits) to lend long (mortgages) is maturity transformation — a core service of banks, and the reason bank runs are possible when everyone demands their short-term money at once.

2. A hedge fund analyst and an investment-bank M&A analyst are, respectively…

Hedge funds manage money (buy side); investment banks sell services — deals, underwriting, execution — to the money (sell side).

3. The book shows asks of 200 shares at $100.05 and 400 at $100.10. A market buy for 600 shares fills at an average of…

(200 × 100.05 + 400 × 100.10) ÷ 600 = 60,050 ÷ 600 = $100.0833. It's a size-weighted average, not a simple midpoint — the extra 3.3¢ over the best ask is slippage.

4. You buy 100 shares of AAPL through your broker today. Who receives your money?

Secondary-market trades move money between investors. Apple was paid once — in the primary market, when the shares were originally issued.

5. What does a clearing house (CCP) fundamentally do?

Through novation the CCP guarantees both legs of every trade, and through netting it shrinks what actually has to move at settlement. Central custody is the depository's job (the DTC), not the clearing house's.

Key takeaways

  • The financial system routes money from savers to users of capital via two channels: direct finance (markets) and indirect finance (intermediaries such as banks and funds).
  • Intermediaries exist to solve information asymmetry, perform maturity transformation, and provide diversification — services no individual saver can replicate alone.
  • Buy side manages money and earns fees on assets; sell side sells execution, research, and new issues, and earns on activity. Every finance job sits on one side or the other.
  • Regulators map to market failures: the SEC forces disclosure, the Fed and FDIC contain systemic risk and bank runs, FINRA polices brokers, the CFTC covers derivatives.
  • Issuers get paid only in the primary market (IPOs, bond issues, via underwriters); the secondary market prices those claims continuously and gives them liquidity.
  • The order book is two price-time priority queues; the bid–ask spread is the price of immediacy, and big market orders walk the book, paying slippage.
  • A trade executes in milliseconds but settles at T+1: clearing houses net trades and guarantee both sides, then cash and securities swap at the depository, with custodians keeping the records.

Part II · Financial Markets & Instruments

7. Equities: The Stock Market

Stocks are the asset class most people mean when they say "the market," and they're where most long-run wealth in public markets has been created — and occasionally vaporized. By the end of this section you'll know exactly what a share is, where equity returns actually come from, how indices are built, and how to read a stock quote like an analyst instead of a tourist.

What a share actually is

A share of stock is a fractional ownership claim on a corporation. If a company has issued 1 billion shares and you own 100 of them, you own 0.00001% of the business — a ten-millionth of every factory, every patent, every dollar of profit it ever earns. Equity is the formal word for this ownership interest.

Two legal features make this claim interesting. First, shareholders are the residual claimants: employees, suppliers, tax authorities, and lenders all get paid first, and shareholders get whatever is left. That's why equity is riskier than debt — and why it can pay so much more. Second, limited liability caps your downside at what you paid. If the company collapses owing billions, your shares go to zero but nobody comes for your house. Unlimited upside, floored downside — that asymmetry is the deal.

Why would a company sell pieces of itself? Because growing costs money, and equity is money you never have to repay. A founder with a great idea but no capital sells 30% of the company to investors, uses the cash to build the product, and keeps 70% of something big instead of 100% of nothing. The alternative — borrowing — creates a fixed obligation that can bankrupt you in a bad year. Equity investors share the pain of bad years, which is exactly why they demand a share of the good ones.

Analogy

Think of a company's claims as a priority queue. Debt holders enqueued first get dequeued (paid) first, with a fixed payout. Shareholders sit at the back holding a claim on whatever remains — which is why the value of a share is so sensitive to small changes in expectations. If a firm is worth 100 and owes 90, equity is worth 10; if the firm's value rises just 10% to 110, equity doubles to 20.

Market cap, share classes, and dilution

The headline size of a public company is its market capitalization ("market cap"): share price × shares outstanding. A $50 stock with 4 billion shares is a $200 billion company; a $500 stock with 20 million shares is a $10 billion one. This is the first myth to kill: the share price alone tells you nothing about how big or "expensive" a company is. Price per share is just market cap divided by an arbitrary share count — the same pizza cut into 8 slices or 80.

Analysts sort companies into buckets: large-cap (>$10B), mid-cap ($2–10B), and small-cap (<$2B). Size matters because it correlates with risk, liquidity, and analyst coverage.

Not all shares are equal. Companies can issue multiple share classes with different voting rights. Alphabet's Class A shares (GOOGL) carry one vote each, Class C (GOOG) carry none, and the founders' Class B shares carry ten — letting insiders keep control while selling most of the economics. Meta and many tech firms use the same structure. When you see "dual-class," read: the founders answer to almost no one.

Your ownership percentage isn't fixed, either. When a company issues new shares — to raise cash, pay employees in stock, or fund an acquisition — every existing share becomes a smaller fraction of the company. That's dilution. Own 10% of a firm with 1 million shares, and if it issues 250,000 new ones, you now own 100,000 of 1,250,000 shares: 8%. Dilution isn't automatically bad — if the cash raised creates more value than the ownership given up, everyone wins — but stock-based compensation that quietly grows the share count 3% a year is a real cost that headline profits can hide. You'll learn to spot it in Section 11 (Financial Statements).

Finally, most companies aren't on the stock market at all. A public company has shares anyone can buy on an exchange and must file audited reports with the SEC; a private company doesn't. The number of US public companies has roughly halved since the mid-1990s (from ~8,000 to ~4,000): regulation post-Enron made being public costlier, and a flood of private equity and venture money means firms can raise billions without ever listing. Companies now go public later, larger, and more reluctantly — which means more of the early growth accrues to private investors before the public ever gets a ticket.

Where returns come from: price + dividends = total return

Owning a stock pays you in exactly two ways. The price can rise (price appreciation), and the company can mail you cash (a dividend). Add them and you get total return — the only return that matters:

Total return = P1 − P0 + DP0 = price return + dividend yield
P0 = starting price · P1 = ending price · D = dividends received over the period

Dividends look small — a 2–3% dividend yield next to double-digit price swings — but reinvested over decades they are enormous, because reinvesting buys more shares, which pay more dividends, which buy more shares. It's the compounding loop from Section 1 running on two engines at once: your per-share price grows at the price return while your share count grows at the dividend yield, so wealth compounds at roughly (1 + g)(1 + d) − 1.

Worked example

$10,000 in a stock returning 5% in price with a 3% yield, dividends reinvested, for 30 years. The compound rate is 1.05 × 1.03 = 1.0815, i.e. 8.15% per year. After 30 years: $10,000 × 1.081530 ≈ $104,900. Price alone: $10,000 × 1.0530 ≈ $43,219. The "small" dividend, reinvested, ends up responsible for more than half of the final wealth — and your share count has grown 1.0330 ≈ 2.43× along the way.

Total return machine

Set a price return and a dividend yield, then flip reinvestment on and off — watch what decades of reinvested dividends do to the gap.

Total return ends at—
Price-only ends at—
Dividends added—
Share count growth—

How much do equities pay for all this risk? Over the last century, US stocks have delivered roughly 7% per year after inflation — the long-run equity risk premium over safe government debt has been around 4–6 percentage points. At 7% real, purchasing power doubles about every 10 years (the Rule of 72 from Section 1). No other broadly accessible asset class comes close over long horizons.

The catch is the ride. Equity volatility runs around 15–20% a year, and the market suffers a drawdown of 30–50%+ roughly once a decade: −86% peak-to-trough in 1929–32, about −50% in 1973–74, 2000–02, and 2008–09, and −34% in five weeks in March 2020. The ~7% real average is earned through those crashes, not around them. That's why time horizon is the single most important variable in equity investing: over one year, stocks are close to a coin flip; over 20–30 years, the historical odds of beating cash have been overwhelming. Money you need next year does not belong in stocks.

Common pitfall

Past return is not a promise. The ~7% real figure is a century-long US average that includes surviving two world wars and winning the economic lottery; Japan's market spent three decades underwater after 1989. History tells you what equities can do and what they charge in volatility — it does not guarantee your particular 20 years will match the average.

Indices and index funds: buying the haystack

A stock index is a rule-based basket of stocks used to measure "the market." The ones you'll hear daily:

Index What's in it Weighting Holdings
S&P 500 Largest US companies; the default "US market" Market cap ~500
Nasdaq-100 Largest non-financial Nasdaq listings; tech-heavy Market cap (modified) 100
Dow Jones Industrial Average 30 blue-chip names picked by committee Price (a historical oddity) 30
Russell 2000 US small-caps Market cap ~2,000
MSCI World / ACWI Developed (and, for ACWI, emerging) markets globally Market cap 1,400+

Almost every serious index is cap-weighted: each company's weight is its market cap divided by the sum of all members' caps. This is just a weighted average with weights wi = capi ÷ Σcap, and it has a beautiful property — the index holds each company in proportion to its actual size, and it rebalances itself for free as prices move. The Dow, by contrast, is price-weighted: it effectively holds one share of each member, so a company's influence depends on its per-share price, which we just established is meaningless. It survives on brand recognition, not merit.

You can't buy an index directly — it's a formula, not a product. The practical vehicles are index funds and ETFs (exchange-traded funds), which hold the index's stocks at the index's weights and charge a tiny expense ratio — as low as 0.03% per year, versus ~1% for typical actively managed funds. That fee gap, compounded over 30 years, eats roughly a quarter of your final wealth, which is why cheap index funds have absorbed trillions of dollars and why Section 17 (Market Efficiency) will explain how hard it is for active managers to earn their fees back. One ETF share can make you a part-owner of 500 (or 8,000) businesses at once — instant diversification, formalized in Section 15 (Portfolio Theory).

Valuation shorthand: EPS, P/E, and earnings yield

Is a stock cheap or expensive? Not by its price — by its price relative to what you get. The workhorse metrics: EPS (earnings per share) is net profit ÷ shares outstanding, and the P/E ratio is what you pay per dollar of annual profit:

P/E = price per shareEPS  ·  earnings yield = EPSprice = 1P/E
A stock at $184.32 with EPS of $7.49 trades at P/E ≈ 24.6, an earnings yield of ≈ 4.1%

The earnings yield (just 1 ÷ P/E) puts stocks in the same units as bonds: a P/E of 25 means each dollar invested currently produces 4 cents of annual profit. That makes cross-asset comparison natural — is 4.1% of growing corporate earnings better than 4.5% of fixed bond coupons? That question is half of finance.

A high P/E is not "overpriced" and a low P/E is not "a bargain" — a P/E is a claim about the future. Paying 45× current earnings only makes sense if you believe earnings will grow fast; paying 11× says you expect stagnation. This is the classic growth versus value framing: growth investors pay up for tomorrow; value investors demand a discount today. Neither is inherently right — the question is always whether the price already reflects the future you expect.

P/E explorer

Drag price and earnings for a fast grower and a steady utility — see how the same $1 of profit gets very different price tags.

Zippy P/E—
Zippy earnings yield—
Utility P/E—
Utility earnings yield—

P/E comes with sharp edges. If earnings are negative, P/E is meaningless (a company losing money has no P/E, not a "low" one). If earnings are cyclical, P/E inverts your intuition: a steel maker at the top of a boom shows huge earnings and a deceptively low P/E right before profits collapse, and a sky-high P/E at the bottom of a recession may mark the cheapest moment to buy. One-off items (a lawsuit settlement, an asset sale) can distort a single year's E. This is exactly why full valuation — discounted cash flows, comparables, and adjustments — gets its own treatment in Section 13 (Valuation).

Corporate actions: dividends, buybacks, splits, and going public

Companies return cash to shareholders two ways. Dividends you've met. A buyback (share repurchase) is the mirror image of dilution: the company buys its own shares on the market and retires them, so every remaining share owns a bigger slice. Suppose a firm earns $1B on 500M shares (EPS $2.00) and buys back 25M shares: the same $1B is now spread over 475M shares, so EPS rises to $2.11 — a 5.3% bump with zero change in the underlying business. In a frictionless world buybacks and dividends are economically equivalent ways to hand cash back; in practice buybacks are more tax-efficient in the US and more flexible (cutting a dividend is a scandal, pausing a buyback is a footnote), which is why buybacks now exceed dividends among large US firms.

A stock split changes nothing real. A 4-for-1 split turns one $500 share into four $125 shares; your stake, the market cap, and the P/E are all identical. Splits exist mainly for optics and round-lot convenience. This buries the myth for good: a $500 stock is not "more expensive" than a $50 one, and a split does not make a company "cheaper" — although markets sometimes pop on split announcements anyway, a small irrationality we'll file under Section 17 (Behavioral Finance).

How do companies get onto the market in the first place? The classic route is the IPO (initial public offering): investment banks (Section 6) value the company, drum up institutional buyers, and sell new shares to the public, collecting fees of ~4–7% — and IPOs are famously often "popped" on day one, meaning the sellers left money on the table.

A direct listing skips the banks' underwriting machine: existing shares simply start trading on the exchange (Spotify in 2018, Coinbase in 2021), which raises no new cash but avoids underpricing and most fees.

A SPAC (special purpose acquisition company) is a publicly listed shell full of cash that hunts for a private company to merge with, taking it public through the back door. SPACs boomed in 2020–21 with lighter disclosure than IPOs, and the average post-merger SPAC investor lost badly — a useful reminder that innovations in market plumbing often redistribute money toward whoever designed the plumbing.

Reading a stock screen like an analyst

Open any quote page and you'll face a wall of numbers. Here's the decoder ring. OHLC is the day's open, high, low, and close — the atomic unit of price data (and, as a time series, the raw material for everything in Section 18). Volume is how many shares traded; a price move on heavy volume carries more conviction than the same move on a trickle. The 52-week range instantly locates today's price in its recent history.

Beta measures how the stock moves with the overall market: beta 1.2 means it tends to move 1.2% for every 1% market move — it's literally the slope coefficient from regressing the stock's returns on the market's, a calculation you could run in three lines of Python (and will, in Section 15). Float is the share count actually available to trade after excluding insiders' locked-up holdings; a small float plus heavy demand is the recipe for violent price moves (see: meme stocks, 2021).

Your edge

Every field on that screen is a column in a dataset. OHLCV time series are freely downloadable, beta is a linear regression, cap weighting is a normalized vector, and total return is a cumulative product. When we hit Section 18 (Quant Finance), you'll compute all of this from raw data — most finance professionals can read the screen; far fewer can rebuild it.

Deep dive: why the share count on a screen can lie to you

"Shares outstanding" comes in flavors. Basic shares count what exists today; diluted shares add everything that could become shares — employee options, restricted stock units, convertible bonds. A company with 100M basic shares and 12M in-the-money options is really a 112M-share company for valuation purposes, and careful analysts always divide by the diluted count. The gap between basic and diluted EPS on an income statement (Section 11) is a quick read on how much of the company is being quietly handed to employees each year.

Float can diverge wildly from shares outstanding. A newly listed company might have 90% of its shares locked up by insiders for six months post-IPO; when the lockup expires, supply floods the market. Checking the float and the lockup calendar is standard diligence before touching a recent IPO.

Check your understanding

1. Stock A trades at $500 with 20 million shares outstanding; Stock B trades at $50 with 4 billion shares. Which company is bigger?

Size is market cap = price × shares. B: $50 × 4B = $200B. A: $500 × 20M = $10B. Price per share alone tells you nothing — it depends on an arbitrary share count.

2. You invest $10,000 at a 5% annual price return with a 3% dividend yield, reinvesting dividends, for 30 years. Your wealth compounds at approximately…

Reinvested returns compound multiplicatively: (1.05)(1.03) − 1 = 8.15%. The extra 0.15% over the naive 8% seems tiny but adds up — the exact 30-year result is ~$104,900 versus $43,219 from price alone.

3. In a price-weighted index like the Dow, which company has the most influence?

Price weighting is effectively holding one share of each member, so influence scales with per-share price — an arbitrary number. A $500 small company outweighs a $50 giant, which is why serious indices are cap-weighted.

4. A steel producer reports record profits at the peak of a construction boom and trades at a P/E of 6. The most careful interpretation is:

Cyclical companies show their lowest P/Es when earnings peak, right before profits fall. A low P/E on boom-time earnings can be more dangerous than a high P/E on trough earnings.

5. A company does a 4-for-1 stock split. What happens to the value of your holding?

A split changes the unit of account, not the business. Market cap, your ownership percentage, and the P/E are all unchanged. Contrast with a buyback, which does change per-share economics by shrinking the share count.

Key takeaways

  • A share is fractional ownership: a residual claim on profits and assets, with losses capped by limited liability — last in line, but with all the upside.
  • Company size is market cap (price × shares), never price per share; splits and share counts are just slicing choices.
  • Total return = price appreciation + dividends. Reinvested dividends compound your share count and can account for over half of long-run wealth.
  • Equities have paid roughly 7% real per year over the last US century — but through 30–50% drawdowns roughly once a decade. The premium is payment for surviving the ride, and past averages are not promises.
  • Serious indices (S&P 500, Russell 2000, MSCI World) are cap-weighted; the price-weighted Dow is a legacy oddity. Index funds and ETFs are the cheap, practical way to own the whole basket.
  • P/E = price per dollar of earnings; its inverse, the earnings yield, makes stocks comparable to bonds. A high P/E is a bet on growth — and P/E breaks on negative or cyclical earnings.
  • Buybacks and dividends both return cash; buybacks shrink the share count and lift EPS. Dilution does the opposite. IPOs, direct listings, and SPACs are just three doors into the public market.
  • On a stock screen, check internal consistency: price ÷ EPS should equal the P/E, beta is a regression slope, and float — not shares outstanding — is what actually trades.

Part II · Financial Markets & Instruments

8. Fixed Income: Bonds and Interest Rates

The bond market is bigger than the stock market, and it is where interest rates — the price of money itself — actually get set. By the end of this section you will be able to price a bond from scratch, explain why prices and yields move in opposite directions, read a yield curve, and estimate exactly how much a rate move will cost a bondholder.

Bond anatomy: a loan cut into pieces

A bond is a loan that has been sliced into standardized, tradable pieces. When you buy a bond you are the lender; the issuer is the borrower. Because the payments are contractually fixed in advance, this whole asset class is called fixed income.

Three numbers define the contract. The face value (or par value, typically $1,000) is what you get back at the end. The coupon is the interest payment — a 5% coupon on $1,000 face pays $50 a year (US bonds actually pay half every six months; we will use annual payments to keep the math clean). The maturity is when the principal comes back. That is it: a bond is a fully specified cash-flow schedule, known on day one. The name "coupon" is literal — paper bonds once had detachable coupons you clipped and mailed in to collect interest.

Who issues them? Four big families:

  • Treasuries — debt of the US federal government (see Section 5 for why it borrows). T-bills mature within a year, notes run 2–10 years, bonds 20–30. Treated as the closest thing to a risk-free benchmark, so everything else is quoted relative to them.
  • Corporates — companies borrowing for factories, acquisitions, or buybacks. Riskier than Treasuries, so they must pay more (we quantify how much in the credit subsection).
  • Munis — state and local government bonds, often federally tax-exempt, which is why their quoted yields look low.
  • Agencies — government-sponsored entities such as Fannie Mae, mostly issuing bonds backed by pools of home mortgages.

Now the surprise for stock-market-centric readers: globally, bonds outstanding total roughly $140 trillion, versus roughly $110–120 trillion for all stocks. Why? Every company has exactly one class of common stock but may have dozens of bond issues alive at once; governments — the biggest borrowers on Earth — issue bonds but no equity at all (the US Treasury market alone is around $27 trillion); and mortgages, car loans, and credit-card balances get bundled into bonds too. Almost all of this trades over the counter through dealer banks rather than on an exchange like the NYSE — the plumbing from Section 6 in action.

Pricing a bond: it is just discounted cash flows

Here is the good news: you already learned the entire pricing model in Section 1. A bond is a known list of future cash flows, and the price of any known cash-flow list is the sum of its present values. No new theory required — only a discount rate.

P = Σt=1…n C ÷ (1 + y)t  +  F ÷ (1 + y)n
P = price · C = annual coupon in dollars · F = face value · y = market yield per year · n = years to maturity

The discount rate y is the yield to maturity (YTM): the going market rate for money of this maturity and riskiness. Crucially, y is set by the market every minute, while C, F, and n are frozen in the contract. Keep that asymmetry in mind — it is about to explain everything.

Worked example: a 3-year, 5% coupon bond at three different yields

Face value $1,000, so the cash flows are $50, $50, $1,050 at years 1, 2, 3.

Market yield 5% (equal to the coupon): 50÷1.05 + 50÷1.05² + 1,050÷1.05³ = 47.62 + 45.35 + 907.03 = $1,000.00. Exactly par.

Market yield 7%: 50÷1.07 + 50÷1.07² + 1,050÷1.07³ = 46.73 + 43.67 + 857.11 = $947.51. Below par.

Market yield 3%: 48.54 + 47.13 + 960.90 = $1,056.57. Above par.

Same bond, same cash flows — three prices. Only the discount rate moved.

Your edge

Bond pricing is a five-line function: loop over t, accumulate cf / (1+y)**t, done. Every readout in this section's widget — price, duration, the whole price–yield curve — falls out of that one loop. When a finance person says "we ran the bond math," they mean someone vectorized this in NumPy.

The seesaw: why price and yield move in opposite directions

This is the single most important fact in fixed income, and once you see it you cannot unsee it: when market yields rise, existing bond prices fall — and vice versa. Not sometimes. Always, mechanically, by arithmetic.

The intuition needs no formula. Suppose you own that 5% coupon bond, and new bonds of identical maturity and risk start being issued with 7% coupons because rates rose. Nobody will pay $1,000 for your $50-a-year bond when $70-a-year is on the shelf next to it. To find a buyer, your bond's price must drop until its fixed cash flows deliver the buyer a 7% return — which is precisely $947.51 for the 3-year bond above. The cash flows are bolted down by contract; price is the only part that can move. So when the required yield goes up, price is forced down, and when yields fall, old higher-coupon bonds become prized and trade above face value.

Analogy: the seesaw

Picture a playground seesaw with price on one seat and yield on the other. Push yield up, price sinks; push yield down, price rises. They are two views of the same object: yield is just the price restated as an annualized return. Saying "bonds sold off today" and "yields rose today" is the same sentence.

The relationship between coupon and market yield sorts every bond into one of three states:

State Condition Price 3-year example
Premium coupon > market yield above $1,000 $1,056.57 at 3%
Par coupon = market yield exactly $1,000 $1,000.00 at 5%
Discount coupon < market yield below $1,000 $947.51 at 7%

One trap to defuse early: there are three different "yields" floating around, and news headlines mix them freely. The coupon rate is frozen at issue ($50 ÷ $1,000 = 5%, forever). The current yield is coupon over today's price ($50 ÷ $947.51 = 5.28%). The YTM (7% here) is the full internal rate of return, counting both the coupons and the guaranteed drift from $947.51 back up to $1,000 at maturity. YTM is the one that matters for comparing bonds; the other two are partial views.

Key idea

A bond's cash flows never change; its price does. Yield up ⇒ price down. Buy at a discount and the market yield exceeds the coupon; buy at a premium and it is the reverse. Everything else in fixed income is a footnote to this seesaw.

The bond seesaw

Drag the market yield past the coupon rate and watch the price cross par — then stretch the maturity and see the seesaw get more violent.

Price ($1,000 face)$859.53
Trading atDiscount
Modified duration7.42 yrs
Current yield5.82%

The yield curve: the market's forecast in one line

Take one issuer — the US Treasury — and plot the yield of its bonds against their maturities, from 3-month bills out to 30-year bonds. That plot is the yield curve, and it is arguably the most-watched chart in finance, because it compresses the market's entire forecast of growth, inflation, and central-bank policy into a single line.

Why does its shape carry information? A 10-year yield has to compete with the alternative of rolling over short-term bills for ten years. So long yields embed the market's expected path of the short rates the Fed controls (Section 4), plus a term premium — compensation for locking up money longer and eating more price risk while you wait. Three canonical shapes result:

  • Normal (upward-sloping): long yields above short. The default state — steady growth expected, term premium doing its job.
  • Flat: little difference across maturities. A transition state — the market thinks the rate cycle is near a turning point.
  • Inverted: short yields above long. The market is saying today's policy rate is unusually high and will be cut — and central banks cut aggressively mostly when the economy is heading into trouble.

That last shape has a famous track record: an inversion of the 10-year-minus-2-year (or 10-year-minus-3-month) Treasury spread has preceded every US recession of the past half-century, typically by 6 to 24 months — the pattern behind the recession indicators in Section 3. It is a strong signal, not a law of physics: the lead time is wildly variable, and the deep 2022–2024 inversion was followed by an unusually long wait, reminding everyone that "preceded every recession" is not the same as "is always followed promptly by one."

Yield curve explorer

Flip between the three classic shapes and read what each one says about the future (illustrative data).

A normal curve: longer lending earns more. The market expects steady growth and policy roughly where it is, and investors collect a term premium for tying money up.

Duration and convexity: how hard does the seesaw swing?

The seesaw tells you the direction of a price move; duration tells you the size. Play with the widget above and you will notice the pattern yourself: stretch maturity from 10 to 30 years and the price curve gets dramatically steeper. Long bonds are the far end of the seesaw plank — same push, much bigger swing.

The cleanest way to see why: Macaulay duration is the weighted-average arrival time of the bond's cash flows, where each date is weighted by the present value it delivers. To a data person, that is just a center of mass. Picture the discounted cash flows as weights sitting on a plank: the small coupons barely matter, and the giant final payment drags the balance point out toward maturity. For our 10-year, 5% bond at a 7% yield the balance point sits at 7.94 years. The farther out your money's center of mass, the longer it stays exposed to compounding at the wrong rate — and the harder the price reacts.

For day-to-day use, traders quote modified duration (Macaulay ÷ (1 + y)), because it plugs straight into the most-used rule of thumb in fixed income:

ΔP ÷ P ≈ −Dmod × Δy
ΔP ÷ P = percent price change · Dmod = modified duration · Δy = change in yield (in percentage points)

Worked example: pricing a rate shock

Our 10-year, 5% bond at a 7% yield: price $859.53, modified duration 7.42 (the widget's default readout). Yields jump one percentage point to 8%. Predicted move: −7.42% × $859.53 ≈ −$63.78, so about $795.75. Exact repricing gives $798.70. The linear rule nailed the damage to within $3 — and note which way it missed: the true price fell less than predicted.

That systematic miss is convexity. The price–yield curve bows toward the origin, so the straight-line duration estimate always sits below it: for a plain bond, losses come in smaller than the linear forecast and gains come in bigger. Duration is the first derivative of price with respect to yield; convexity is the second — a Taylor expansion you have met many times, wearing a suit. It is why the rule of thumb is trustworthy for small moves (a few basis points) and only approximate for big ones.

Real world: 2022, the worst bond year in modern history

Duration stopped being an abstraction in 2022. Bonds had been issued for years at rock-bottom coupons, which makes durations long (small coupons = the plank's weight is almost all at the far end). Then the Fed hiked from near 0% to over 4% in nine months. Run the rule: broad bond-index duration around 6.5 and yields up roughly 2.5 points ⇒ ≈ −16% before coupon income.
