The Bloomberg US Aggregate index finished 2022 down about 13% — its worst year since records began in 1976 — and long-maturity Treasuries lost around 30%, a stock-crash-sized loss in the "safe" asset.

Silicon Valley Bank was the corporate casualty of the same arithmetic: it parked deposits in long-duration Treasuries and agency mortgage bonds, rates rose, and the mark-to-market hole grew to roughly the size of its entire equity. Depositors noticed and ran. There was no exotic credit bet involved — just unhedged duration. Section 16 dissects the risk-management failure in full.

Common pitfall

Duration measures sensitivity to a small, parallel shift in yields — it is a local linear model, not a crystal ball. For big moves you need the convexity term; for twists in the curve's shape (short yields up, long yields down) a single duration number can badly mislead.

Credit: getting paid for the risk of not getting paid

Everything so far assumed the cash flows arrive. For Treasuries that is a working assumption; for everyone else there is default risk, and the market charges for it.

The pricing of that risk starts with a credit rating — a letter grade on repayment odds from Moody's, S&P, or Fitch. The scale runs from AAA (a fortress) down through the alphabet to D (already in default). One line matters more than all others: BBB− and above is investment grade (IG); BB+ and below is high yield — universally nicknamed junk. The line is regulatory as well as reputational: many pension funds and insurers are barred from holding junk, so a downgrade across it (a "fallen angel") triggers forced selling.

The price of credit risk is the credit spread: the bond's yield minus a same-maturity Treasury yield. A BBB corporate yielding 5.5% while the 10-year Treasury pays 4% carries a 150-basis-point spread. Spreads breathe with the market's mood, which makes them a superb fear gauge: US high-yield spreads run roughly 3–4 points in calm times, blew out past 20 points in the 2008 crisis, and spiked above 10 in March 2020 before the Fed intervened. When spreads gap wider, the bond market is repricing the probability of a wave of defaults — often before equity markets fully react.

Default is also not a total loss. Bondholders stand ahead of stockholders in bankruptcy (the payoff hierarchy from Section 7), and the recovery rate on senior unsecured corporate bonds has historically averaged around 40 cents on the dollar. So a rough model of a fair spread is expected loss: default probability × (1 − recovery), plus a cushion for risk aversion and illiquidity — a one-line expected-value calculation you could code in your sleep.

Money markets: the plumbing where cash lives

Below one year to maturity, fixed income stops feeling like investing and starts feeling like infrastructure. The money market is where corporations, funds, and governments park and borrow cash by the trillions, overnight to a few months.

The instruments are refreshingly simple. T-bills pay no coupon at all: you buy at a discount and collect face value — pay $980.39 today for $1,000 in six months and you have locked in 2% over the period (about 4% annualized). Commercial paper is the corporate version — unsecured IOUs blue-chip firms roll over routinely to fund payroll and inventory.

The strangest-sounding and most important one is repo (a repurchase agreement): I sell you a Treasury today and commit to buying it back tomorrow at a slightly higher price. Squint and it is an overnight loan collateralized by the Treasury, with the price difference as interest. Trillions of dollars of repo roll every night; it is how dealers finance their inventories, and it anchors SOFR, the benchmark rate that replaced LIBOR in most floating-rate contracts. The Fed itself steers short-term rates partly through repo operations (Section 4). When people say the financial system's plumbing froze in 2008, the repo and commercial-paper markets are literally what they mean.

Finally, money-market funds are where all of this gets packaged for everyone else: mutual funds holding T-bills, repo, and commercial paper, engineered to hold a $1.00 share price so they feel like a bank account with a yield. That $1.00 is a promise, not a law.

Real world: breaking the buck

In September 2008, the Reserve Primary Fund — a money-market fund holding Lehman Brothers commercial paper — marked its shares at $0.97 when Lehman failed. "Breaking the buck" by three cents sounds trivial; it triggered a $300+ billion institutional run on money funds within days, froze the commercial-paper market that companies use to make payroll, and forced the US Treasury to guarantee money funds outright. The lesson: the most boring corner of finance is the one nobody can function without.

Deep dive: real-world bond math conventions

Everything above used annual coupons for clarity, but US convention is semiannual: a 5% coupon pays $25 every six months, and quoted YTMs are semiannual rates doubled (a "bond-equivalent yield"). The mechanics are identical — halve the coupon and rate, double the period count. Our 10-year, 5% bond at a 7% quoted yield becomes 20 payments of $25 discounted at 3.5% per half-year, giving $857.88 rather than $859.53. Same seesaw, slightly different arithmetic.

Two other conventions you will hit in practice. First, prices are quoted per $100 of face ("95.75" means 95.75% of par) and clean — excluding interest accrued since the last coupon, which the buyer pays on top (the "dirty" price is what actually changes hands). Second, day-count conventions (30/360 for corporates, actual/actual for Treasuries) decide exactly how that accrued interest is computed. None of this changes the concepts; all of it changes the fourth decimal place, which is precisely the sort of thing an analytics team gets paid to reconcile.

One elegant special case worth knowing: a zero-coupon bond (like a T-bill, or Treasury STRIPS at longer maturities) has its entire cash flow at maturity, so its Macaulay duration equals its maturity — the balance-point picture with one single weight at the far end of the plank. Zeros are therefore the purest, most rate-sensitive bonds you can hold.

Check your understanding

1. Market interest rates rise from 4% to 6%. What happens to the price of an existing fixed-coupon bond?

The cash flows cannot change, so the price must. Nobody pays par for a 4% coupon when new bonds pay 6%; the price drops until the old bond's fixed payments yield 6% from the lower purchase price.

2. A bond with a 6% coupon trades at $1,080 (above its $1,000 face). What do you know about its yield to maturity?

Premium price means coupon > market yield. Paying $1,080 for cash flows built on $1,000 also guarantees a capital loss at maturity, dragging the all-in return (YTM) below the 6% coupon rate.

3. The 2-year Treasury yields 5.0% while the 10-year yields 4.2%. Historically, what has this configuration signaled?

That is an inverted curve. Long yields sit below short ones only when the market expects short rates to be cut a lot — and deep cuts historically arrive with recessions. Inversions have preceded every US recession of the past half-century, with variable lead times.

4. A bond fund has a modified duration of 6. Yields rise by 0.5 percentage points. Approximately what happens to the fund's value?

ΔP ÷ P ≈ −D × Δy = −6 × 0.5% = −3%. Duration converts a yield move into a percent price move — and thanks to convexity the true loss would be slightly smaller than 3%.

5. High-yield credit spreads jump from 350 to 800 basis points in a month. What is the bond market saying?

The spread is the market's charge for default risk over Treasuries. Widening from 3.5 to 8 points means investors demand far more compensation to hold risky corporate debt — a classic fear signal that often precedes trouble showing up in equities.

Key takeaways

  • A bond is a tradable loan with contractually fixed cash flows: coupons plus face value at maturity. Globally, bonds outstanding exceed the value of all stocks.
  • Price = sum of discounted cash flows, exactly as in Section 1. The cash flows are frozen; the market yield — and therefore the price — is not.
  • The seesaw: yields up ⇒ prices down, always. Coupon above market yield ⇒ premium; below ⇒ discount; equal ⇒ par. YTM is the all-in return; the coupon rate never changes.
  • The yield curve plots Treasury yields across maturities and encodes the expected path of Fed policy. Inversions have preceded every US recession in half a century, with 6–24-month (and sometimes longer) lead times.
  • Duration is the center of mass of discounted cash flows and the price-sensitivity dial: ΔP ÷ P ≈ −D × Δy. Convexity makes real losses slightly smaller and gains slightly bigger than that linear estimate.
  • Low coupons and long maturities mean high duration — the arithmetic behind 2022's record bond losses and SVB's collapse (Section 16).
  • Credit spreads price default risk over Treasuries; the BBB−/BB+ line splits investment grade from junk, and widening spreads are one of the market's best fear gauges.
  • Money markets — T-bills, repo, commercial paper, money funds — are the system's cash plumbing; when they seize (2008), everything else stops.

Part II · Financial Markets & Instruments

9. Derivatives: Futures, Options and Swaps

Derivatives are contracts whose value is computed from something else — a stock, a bond, a barrel of oil, an interest rate. Master this section and you will be able to read an option chain, price out a hedge, and explain why the same instrument that saves a farmer's season can vaporize a hedge fund.

A contract about a price, not the thing itself

A derivative is a legal agreement whose payoff is a function of some other price. That other price — the stock, the commodity, the interest rate — is called the underlying. You never need to own the underlying to trade the derivative, any more than you need to own a database to write a query against it.

Analogy

Think of a derivative as a derived column: it stores no data of its own, just a formula like payoff = max(price − 100, 0) evaluated against a live feed. All the machinery in this section is about who gets paid when that formula is evaluated, and when.

Two kinds of people show up to trade these contracts, and both are necessary. A hedger already carries a risk — a farmer exposed to wheat prices, an airline exposed to jet fuel — and uses the contract to shed it. A speculator has no such exposure and takes the other side hoping to profit. Speculators get the bad press, but without them hedgers often couldn't find a counterparty at a fair price. Speculators are the liquidity providers of the risk-transfer business.

Two properties make derivatives different from the assets in Section 7 (Equities) and Section 8 (Fixed Income):

They are zero-sum before costs. Every derivative has two sides, and one side's gain is exactly the other side's loss. A stock can make both buyer and (earlier) seller money over time because the company itself creates value; a futures contract cannot. After commissions and spreads, the average derivative trader is slightly underwater by construction. That is fine — for a hedger, the "loss" is an insurance premium happily paid.

They embed enormous leverage. A few thousand dollars of collateral can control a six-figure exposure. Leverage scales your P&L in both directions with the same multiplier: a 3% move in the underlying can be a 60% move in your account. Derivatives are professional risk-transfer tools first; the retail lottery-ticket use case came later, and most retail option buyers lose money. Keep that ordering in mind for the rest of this section.

Key idea

Derivatives don't create or destroy risk — they relocate it, from someone who doesn't want it to someone who is paid to hold it. Pricing a derivative is pricing that relocation.

Forwards and futures: locking a price today

The oldest derivative is a promise. A forward is a private contract: I agree today to sell you 50,000 bushels of wheat in December at $6.00 per bushel, whatever the market price is then. No money changes hands now; we just shake on the price.

Worked example: the farmer and the flour mill

A farmer will harvest 50,000 bushels in December and needs $5.20/bu to cover costs. Today's December price is $6.00. She sells forward at $6.00, locking in revenue of 50,000 × $6.00 = $300,000 and a profit of $40,000.

If December spot wheat is $5.00: she sells her crop for $250,000, but her forward position gains ($6.00 − $5.00) × 50,000 = $50,000. Total: $300,000. If spot is $7.00: crop fetches $350,000, forward loses $50,000. Total: $300,000 again. The hedge doesn't make her rich — it makes her predictable. The airline hedging jet fuel runs the same trade in mirror image, buying forward to cap its costs.

Forwards have two problems: you must find a counterparty who wants your exact size and date, and you must trust them to pay up in December — counterparty risk. A futures contract solves both. It is a forward that has been standardized (fixed size, fixed delivery months, fixed quality specs) and listed on an exchange like the CME, with a clearinghouse inserted in the middle so that neither side ever faces the other directly.

The clearinghouse protects itself with two mechanisms. First, both sides post margin — a good-faith deposit, typically 3–12% of the contract's value. Second, positions are marked to market every day: each evening, that day's gain or loss is moved in cash between the two margin accounts. Losses are never allowed to pile up unpaid. If your balance falls below the maintenance margin, you get a margin call: top up or be liquidated. Figure 9.1 walks through a week of this for our farmer.

That Wednesday row is the hidden cost of hedging with futures: even a hedge that is guaranteed to work out at delivery can demand cash today. Being "right eventually" doesn't help if you can't fund the margin calls along the way. The practical differences in one line each: futures are standardized, exchange-traded, clearinghouse-guaranteed, marked to market daily, and easy to exit; forwards are custom, private, carry counterparty risk, settle once at the end, and are hard to exit early. Most physical commodity futures are closed out before delivery — hedgers usually want the price protection, not the truckloads of wheat (more on commodities in Section 10).

Options: the right, but not the obligation

A future obligates both sides. An option is asymmetric: the buyer gets a right, the seller takes on an obligation. A call is the right to buy the underlying at a fixed price; a put is the right to sell. That fixed price is the strike, the deadline is the expiry, and the price you pay up front for the right is the premium. The seller — called the writer — pockets the premium and must perform if the buyer exercises. US equity options cover 100 shares per contract, so a quoted premium of $3.02 costs $302.

Because the buyer will only ever exercise when it helps them, option payoffs are kinked — the famous hockey sticks in Figure 9.2. A call buyer's loss is capped at the premium while the upside is open-ended; the call writer's profile is the exact mirror, capped gain and open-ended loss. Zero-sum, always.

An option's value at any moment splits into two parts. Intrinsic value is what exercising immediately would earn: for a call, max(S − K, 0). Time value is everything above that — payment for the possibility that things improve before expiry. Related jargon: moneyness. A call with the stock above the strike is in the money (ITM); stock near the strike is at the money (ATM); stock below the strike leaves the call out of the money (OTM) — pure time value, pure hope.

Here is a realistic option chain for a $100 stock with 30 days to expiry:

Strike Call bid/ask Call delta Put bid/ask Impl. vol Call moneyness
95 6.15 / 6.30 0.79 0.85 / 0.95 25% ITM: $5 intrinsic + ~$1.20 time
100 2.95 / 3.10 0.53 2.60 / 2.75 25% ATM: all time value
105 1.10 / 1.20 0.27 5.75 / 5.90 25% OTM: all time value

Worked example: leverage cuts both ways

Buy the 100-strike call at $3.02 ($302 per contract). If the stock closes at $110 at expiry, the call is worth $10.00 — a gain of $6.98 per share, or +231%, versus +10% for the stock. If the stock closes at $100 (unchanged!), the call expires worthless: −100% versus 0% for the stock. The option didn't just lever the direction — it added a deadline. You now need to be right about what, how much, and by when. That triple hurdle is why most retail option buyers lose money even when their directional view is decent.

What an option is worth: volatility and the Greeks

Why is the ATM call above worth $3.02 rather than $1 or $8? Intuitively: an option is worth the probability-weighted average of its payoffs. Anything that widens the distribution of possible stock prices — more time, more volatility — raises the value of a contract that keeps the upside and discards the downside. Practitioners summarize an option's sensitivities with the Greeks, and if you know calculus you already know what they are: partial derivatives of price with respect to each input.

Delta (∂V/∂S) is the slope: a 0.53-delta call gains about $0.53 when the stock gains $1. Delta also doubles as a rough probability of finishing ITM — see the chain above, where the deep ITM call has delta 0.79 and the OTM call 0.27. Theta (∂V/∂t) is time decay: the option bleeds value every day the stock fails to move, and the bleed accelerates near expiry (Figure 9.3). Vega (∂V/∂σ) is sensitivity to volatility itself.

Of the pricing inputs — stock price, strike, time, interest rate, volatility — only one is not directly observable: future volatility. So volatility is the thing option markets actually trade. Quote an option price and I can invert the pricing model to recover the volatility that price implies — the implied volatility in the chain above. When traders say options are "expensive," they mean implied volatility is high relative to what they expect to be realized.

The model everyone inverts is Black-Scholes. You do not need its derivation (that lives in Section 18 (Quant Finance)); you need to know it exists, what goes in, and that it is trivially implementable.

Deep dive: Black-Scholes in ten lines
C = S·N(d1) − K·e−rT·N(d2)
C = call price · S = stock price · K = strike · r = risk-free rate · T = years to expiry · N = standard normal CDF
d1 = ln(S ÷ K) + (r + σ²/2)·Tσ·√T   d2 = d1 − σ·√T
σ = annualized volatility · put price via put-call parity: P = C − S + K·e−rT

As a CS person you can ship this before lunch: N(x) = 0.5 * (1 + erf(x / sqrt(2))) using the error function in any math library, then the two formulas above — about ten lines of Python. Sanity check: S=100, K=100, T=30/365, r=4%, σ=25% gives C ≈ $3.02 and P ≈ $2.69, matching the option chain earlier. Implied volatility is the inverse problem: given a market price, root-find for σ (bisection or Newton's method — the vega is the derivative you need). Note what the buyer's edge must be: the formula assumes volatility; if you pay 25% implied and the stock only realizes 18%, you overpaid even if the model is perfect.

Black-Scholes also gives closed-form Greeks: the call delta is simply N(d1) — which is where the 0.79 / 0.53 / 0.27 column in the chain came from.

Strategies: composing hockey sticks

Options are payoff Lego. Because each leg is a simple kinked function, you can compose positions whose expiry P&L has almost any shape — and you should always look at that shape before trading. Three canonical compositions:

A covered call = own the stock + write a call. You collect the premium as income but surrender any gains above the strike. A protective put = own the stock + buy a put: an insurance policy with the premium as the deductible-plus-premium cost and the strike as the floor. A straddle = buy a call and a put at the same strike: you don't know direction, you're betting on magnitude — a volatility trade. And the mirror image of insurance is the cash-secured put: sell someone else downside protection and keep the premium if it isn't needed.

Build each one below. Watch the readouts, not just the line: breakeven, max gain, max loss are the three numbers that define a strategy.

Option payoff builder

Pick a strategy, drag the strike and premium, and read the P&L-at-expiry line against just holding the stock. Stock legs assume shares bought at $100; all figures are per share (×100 for one contract).

Breakeven(s)—
Max gain—
Max loss—
Net premium—

━ strategy P&L at expiry  ·  ╌ stock only (bought at $100)  ·  ● breakeven. For the straddle, equal strikes is the classic straddle; drag the put strike K2 below the call strike to build a strangle.

Play with the covered call at strike 80: there are premiums at which the position can never break even — you sold away all the upside for less than the downside you kept. The builder also makes the straddle's problem obvious: you pay two premiums, so the stock must move a lot in either direction before you earn a cent. If everyone expects a big earnings move, that expectation is already in the premiums via implied volatility — you profit only if the move beats the market's forecast.

Common pitfall: selling options is not free money

Short option strategies win small amounts very often — the writer keeps the premium in most months — which makes the P&L history look like a beautiful, smooth, upward line. Right up until it isn't. The distribution is extremely skewed: frequent small gains, rare catastrophic losses. Traders call it picking up pennies in front of a steamroller. Funds that sold volatility this way (see the February 2018 "Volmageddon" episode, and Section 17 for the general pattern) lost years of gains in days. If a strategy's backtest never loses, ask what tail it is short — your statistics background is exactly the edge here: always look at the full distribution, not the mean.

Swaps: trading cash-flow streams

A swap is an agreement to exchange one stream of cash flows for another. The workhorse is the interest-rate swap: one side pays a fixed rate, the other pays a floating rate that resets with the market — today usually SOFR, the benchmark that replaced LIBOR. Both legs are computed on a notional amount that is never actually exchanged — it is just the number the percentages multiply. Headlines about "hundreds of trillions in derivatives outstanding" are quoting notionals; the actual net cash at risk is a tiny fraction of that.

Worked example: fixing a floating loan

TechCo borrowed $100M from its bank at SOFR + 1.25%, floating. The CFO can't budget around a rate that resets constantly, so TechCo enters a swap: pay 4.00% fixed, receive SOFR, on a $100M notional. Only the net moves each period:

Year 1, SOFR averages 3.50%: TechCo owes 4.00% and is owed 3.50%, so it pays the dealer the net 0.50% = $500,000. Year 2, SOFR averages 4.50%: now TechCo receives the net 0.50% = $500,000. Combine the swap with the loan and the SOFR terms cancel: TechCo's all-in cost is 4.00% + 1.25% = 5.25% fixed, forever, no matter what the Fed does. Same trick, reversed, is how banks and insurers manage the rate risk in their bond portfolios.

The other swap you must know is the credit default swap (CDS): insurance on a bond. The protection buyer pays a periodic fee (say 1% of notional per year); if the referenced borrower defaults, the seller covers the loss. Useful in moderation — a lender can hedge a loan without ending the relationship, and CDS spreads are a real-time market vote on credit risk, often faster than rating agencies.

Real world: CDS and 2008

Two features made CDS the star of the 2008 crisis. You can buy protection on bonds you don't own — like insuring your neighbor's house — so total CDS notional grew to a multiple of the underlying debt. And because CDS were then private, uncleared contracts with little margin, sellers could write essentially unlimited protection. AIG's financial products unit sold protection on mortgage securities against which it held almost no reserves; when housing cracked, collateral calls arrived it could not meet, and the US government committed roughly $182 billion so AIG's failure wouldn't cascade through every counterparty. Post-crisis reforms pushed standard swaps into clearinghouses with daily margin — futures-style plumbing, precisely because that plumbing worked. The full story is in Section 17 (Crises).

Your edge

A swap is just two projected cash-flow vectors netted against each other, and its fair value is the difference of their present values — discounting you already know from Section 1. Swap desks literally hire people to build and maintain those discount curves in code. If you can write clean numerical Python, this corner of finance is one of the most natural entry points (see Section 20).

Check your understanding

1. A farmer shorts wheat futures at $6.00 to hedge her harvest, and by delivery wheat trades at $6.80. Which statement is right?

The hedge locks the price in both directions: the $0.80 futures loss is offset by an extra $0.80 on the physical crop. She gave up the upside — that is the cost of certainty, not a mistake. And no one is exempt from margin.

2. You are long one crude oil futures contract (1,000 barrels) bought at $80. Today's settlement price is $78. What happens to your margin account today?

Mark-to-market is unconditional: a $2 drop × 1,000 barrels = $2,000 leaves your account this evening. Whether you then get a margin call depends on where that leaves your balance relative to maintenance margin.

3. You paid $6 for a call with a $100 strike. At expiry the stock is at $104. Which is true?

Moneyness and profit are different questions. Intrinsic value at expiry is max(104 − 100, 0) = $4, so you exercise (worthless it is not) — but you paid $6 for it, so P&L = 4 − 6 = −$2. Breakeven was $106.

4. A strategy of selling out-of-the-money puts has been profitable 34 of the last 36 months. What is the most statistically sound reaction?

Short-option P&L is heavily skewed — the mean of the distribution can be negative even with a 94% win rate if the rare losses are large enough. "Picking up pennies in front of a steamroller" is a statement about tails, and hit rate ignores tails.

5. A firm pays 3% fixed and receives SOFR on a $50M notional swap. SOFR averages 2% this year. What cash actually moves?

Swaps settle net: 3% owed minus 2% received = 1% of $50M = $500,000 paid by the firm. The notional is only a reference number. And paying fixed while receiving floating hurts when floating rates are low — the fixed payer wins when rates rise.

Key takeaways

  • A derivative's value is a formula evaluated against an underlying price. It relocates risk from hedgers to speculators — both roles are essential — and it is zero-sum before costs.
  • Futures lock in a price today via a standardized, clearinghouse-guaranteed contract; daily mark-to-market and margin calls mean even a "correct" hedge can demand cash along the way.
  • Options are asymmetric: buyers pay a premium for capped-loss, open-ended-gain payoffs; writers accept the mirror image. Value = intrinsic + time value, and the deadline is half the bet.
  • The Greeks are just partial derivatives: delta is the slope versus the stock, theta the daily time decay (which accelerates near expiry), vega the sensitivity to volatility — the one input the market actually trades, via implied volatility and Black-Scholes.
  • Strategies compose kinked payoffs: covered calls trade upside for income, protective puts buy a floor, straddles bet on magnitude rather than direction. Judge every strategy by breakeven, max gain, max loss.
  • Selling options harvests frequent small gains against rare huge losses — a skewed distribution that a win rate cannot summarize. Most retail option buyers lose money; these are professional hedging tools first.
  • Swaps exchange cash-flow streams on a notional that never moves; an interest-rate swap converts floating debt to fixed. CDS are default insurance whose unmargined excesses helped detonate 2008 — and why swaps now clear like futures.

Part II · Financial Markets & Instruments

10. FX, Commodities and Alternative Investments

Beyond stocks and bonds sits a sprawling world: the currency market that dwarfs every other, commodities you can only sensibly touch through futures, and the "alternatives" — real estate, private equity, hedge funds, crypto — that dominate institutional portfolios and finance-job postings alike. By the end of this section you'll be able to read an FX quote, hedge a currency exposure, compute a cap rate, and decode a 2-and-20 fee structure.

The FX market: the biggest market you've never seen a building for

The foreign exchange (FX) market turns over roughly $7.5 trillion per day (BIS 2022 survey). For scale: that's more in a week than global stock markets trade in a year. There's no exchange floor — it's a decentralized, 24-hour-a-weekday network of bank dealers, an over-the-counter market like the swaps you met in Section 9 (Derivatives).

Currencies trade in pairs, and the quote convention trips up everyone at first. EUR/USD = 1.10 means one euro costs 1.10 US dollars. The first currency (EUR) is the base currency — the thing being priced, always one unit. The second (USD) is the quote currency — the units on the price tag. So when EUR/USD rises from 1.10 to 1.20, the euro got stronger and the dollar got weaker, even though "USD went up" is what your eye wants to read.

Price moves are measured in pips (0.0001 for most pairs). On a standard €100,000 position, one pip is worth 0.0001 × 100,000 = $10 — which tells you why FX traders care about the fourth decimal. Most volume is spot (exchange now, settle in two days), but a huge share is forwards and swaps — the derivative machinery from Section 9 applied to money itself.

What moves currencies? Mostly the forces you met in Section 5 (Fiscal Policy, Debt & Trade): interest-rate differentials (capital flows toward higher yields, set by the central banks of Section 4), inflation differentials (high inflation erodes purchasing power and, eventually, the exchange rate), and trade and capital flows (persistent deficits need financing). Layer on risk sentiment — in a panic, money runs to the dollar, yen and Swiss franc — and you have most of the story.

Rate differentials also power the most famous FX strategy: the carry trade. Borrow yen at 0.5%, convert, invest in Australian assets at 4.5%, and pocket the 4-percentage-point gap — as long as exchange rates sit still. That's the catch. Carry trades earn small steady gains and then, occasionally, lose catastrophically when everyone unwinds at once: a 5% yen rally erases more than a year of carry in days. Traders call it "picking up nickels in front of a steamroller," and the August 2024 yen-carry unwind — which briefly knocked global equities down several percent — showed the steamroller is real.

Currency moves: who wins?

Drag EUR/USD away from the 1.10 baseline and watch who gains and who bleeds — the same move, four opposite experiences.

At the 1.10 baseline — no change yet.

Who Position Dollar amount now vs 1.10 baseline
US importer owes a €1,000,000 invoice $1,100,000 —
US exporter books €1,000,000 of sales $1,100,000 —
US tourist faces a €3,000 hotel bill $3,300 —
US investor holds €100,000 of European stocks (flat in €) $110,000 —

Payers of euros (importer, tourist) want a weak euro; receivers and holders of euro assets (exporter, investor) want a strong one. Same price, opposite rooting interests.

How companies live with FX

For a multinational, currency risk isn't a trading opportunity — it's weather. It comes in two flavors. Transaction exposure is risk on actual cash flows: you've agreed to pay or receive foreign currency at a future date, and the rate can move before it does. Translation exposure is an accounting effect: a US parent must restate its German subsidiary's euro results in dollars, so reported revenue swings with the exchange rate even if the business itself never changes. A subsidiary earning €50M shows up as $55M at 1.10 but only $50M at parity — a $5M "decline" with zero operational cause. (You'll see this flagged as "constant-currency growth" when you read filings in Section 11.)

Transaction exposure is the one companies actively hedge, usually with the forwards from Section 9. The logic: give up the upside to delete the downside, because a manufacturer's edge is manufacturing, not currency speculation.

Worked example: a European exporter hedges dollar revenue

A German machine-tool maker signs a contract to receive $5,000,000 in six months. Its costs are in euros, so a weakening dollar (EUR/USD rising) directly shrinks its margin. It sells the dollars forward at today's six-month forward rate of 1.10, locking in $5,000,000 ÷ 1.10 = €4,545,455, guaranteed.

Six months later, suppose spot has moved to 1.20. Unhedged, the $5M would convert to only $5,000,000 ÷ 1.20 = €4,166,667. The hedge preserved €378,788 — likely more than the entire profit margin on the machines. If instead spot had fallen to 1.00, the unhedged value would have been €5,000,000 and the hedge "cost" €454,545 of foregone upside. That's the deal: certainty in both directions.

Your edge

Corporate FX desks and treasury analytics teams are heavy consumers of data skills: forecasting exposures from ERP data, computing hedge ratios, simulating rate scenarios. "Treasury analyst" is one of the most underrated data-adjacent entry points into finance.

Deep dive: where forward FX rates come from

Forward rates are not a forecast — they're arbitrage arithmetic, exactly like the futures pricing in Section 9. If US one-year rates are 4% and euro rates are 2%, the one-year forward must satisfy F = S × (1 + rUSD) ÷ (1 + rEUR) = 1.10 × 1.04 ÷ 1.02 ≈ 1.1216. Why: holding dollars for a year earns 4%; holding euros earns 2% plus whatever the currency does. If the forward sat anywhere else, you could borrow one currency, lend the other, lock the forward, and print riskless profit. This relationship is called covered interest parity, and it's one of the most reliably enforced identities in finance. Note the implication: the higher-yielding currency trades at a forward discount — the forward market "gives back" the carry.

Commodities: futures curves, oil and the strange case of gold

Commodities — oil, gas, wheat, copper, gold — are the only asset class you could theoretically dump in your driveway. That's exactly why almost nobody holds them physically: storage, insurance and transport are expensive, and a barrel of oil pays no dividend while it sits there. So financial investors get exposure through the futures contracts from Section 9: standardized, liquid, no warehouse required.

But futures introduce a subtlety that catches even professionals: the shape of the futures curve drives your returns as much as the commodity's price does. Plot the futures price against contract maturity. If longer-dated contracts cost more than spot, the curve is in contango — normal when storage is costly. If they cost less, it's in backwardation — typical when the physical commodity is scarce right now.

Here's why it matters. Futures expire, so a long-term investor must roll: sell the expiring contract, buy the next one out. In contango you repeatedly sell the cheap near contract and buy the expensive far one, and as each contract ages it tends to slide down the curve toward spot. That systematic drag is negative roll yield — an oil ETF can lose money over a year in which spot oil is flat, purely from rolling. In backwardation the same mechanics work in your favor. If you've built a data pipeline with scheduled jobs, the analogy is apt: roll yield is a recurring cost (or credit) baked into the schedule, independent of what the underlying data does.

Two market notes. Oil is the macro commodity: benchmarks WTI (US, NYMEX) and Brent (global, ICE) trade in contracts of 1,000 barrels, and the price feeds straight into the inflation dynamics of Section 3. In April 2020, WTI futures famously settled at negative $37 — storage was full, and holders paid to escape taking physical delivery. That episode is the cleanest proof you'll find that a futures price is about delivery logistics, not just abstract value.

Gold is the odd one out. It has almost no industrial use in proportion to its price and generates no cash flow, yet central banks hold about 35,000 tonnes of it. The honest framing: gold is the anti-currency — a monetary asset that no government can print. It tends to rise when real interest rates fall (cash pays less, so gold's zero yield hurts less) and when trust in monetary policy wobbles. You can't value it with the discounting tools of Section 1; you can only reason about its supply, its demand and its 5,000-year incumbency as the asset people run to.

Real estate and REITs: leverage plus a yield you can compute

Real estate is the world's largest asset class by value, and its defining feature is leverage. Nobody buys a $500,000 building with $500,000 of cash; they put down $100,000 and borrow $400,000. Now a 10% rise in the property's price ($50,000) is a 50% gain on your equity — and a 20% fall wipes your equity to zero. Same asset, radically different risk, purely because of the financing. Keep that multiplier in mind for Section 16 (Risk Management); it's the engine of most property busts, 2008 included.

The valuation workhorse is beautifully simple. A property's net operating income (NOI) is rent minus operating costs (maintenance, insurance, property tax, management — but not the mortgage). The cap rate is:

Cap rate = NOI ÷ Price
NOI = annual rent − operating costs (excluding mortgage) · Price = purchase price · result = unlevered yield

Worked example

A rental property costs $500,000, brings in $42,000 of annual rent and costs $12,000 a year to run. NOI = 42,000 − 12,000 = $30,000, so the cap rate is 30,000 ÷ 500,000 = 6.0%. Compare that to the 10-year Treasury from Section 8: if Treasuries yield 4%, the property offers a 2-percentage-point spread as compensation for illiquidity, vacancies, leaky roofs and tenant risk. If Treasuries rose to 6%, that spread vanishes — which is exactly why property prices sag when rates climb.

Cap rate calculator

Price a property like an analyst: change the numbers and watch the yield spread against Treasuries.

NOI (rent − costs)$30,000
Cap rate6.00%
Spread vs Treasury+2.0 pp

Because buildings are lumpy and illiquid, markets built a liquid wrapper: the REIT. A REIT is a listed company that owns portfolios of properties — apartments, warehouses, cell towers, data centers — and, in exchange for paying no corporate tax, must distribute at least 90% of taxable income as dividends. You get diversified property exposure with stock-market liquidity: buy VNQ or a single REIT in seconds. The trade-off: because REITs trade like the stocks of Section 7, they crash like stocks too — in 2008 they fell alongside equities even as "real estate diversification" was the reason people held them. Liquidity has a price: it imports equity-market volatility.

Private equity and venture capital: illiquidity as a business model

Private equity (PE) buys entire companies off the public market — or ones that were never on it. The signature move is the leveraged buyout (LBO), which is the real-estate leverage trick applied to a whole business. One worked paragraph: a PE firm buys a company generating $125M of EBITDA for 8× EBITDA = $1,000M, funding it with $400M of its own equity and $600M of debt loaded onto the company. Over five years the firm pushes EBITDA to $150M and uses the company's cash flow to pay debt down to $300M. It exits at the same 8× multiple: $1,200M, minus $300M of debt, leaves $900M of equity — 2.25× the money in, roughly a 17.6% annualized return. Notice the sources of profit: some operational improvement, but a lot of leverage and debt paydown. That's the LBO in one breath.

Venture capital (VC) plays the opposite statistical game. Instead of leveraging stable cash flows, it buys lottery tickets governed by a power law. A stylized fund makes twenty $1M investments: thirteen go to zero, five limp back their $1M each, one returns 5×, and one returns 30×. Total: $40M on $20M invested — a respectable 2× — and the single 30× winner returned more than everything else combined. For a data scientist this should feel familiar: it's an extreme-skew distribution where the mean is meaningless without the tail, and "median startup outcome" is a rounding error above zero. VC diligence is therefore tail-hunting, not average-estimating.

Both run on the same fund plumbing. Investors — pensions, endowments, sovereign funds — are limited partners (LPs); the firm managing the money is the general partner (GP). LPs commit capital that the GP calls down over several years as it finds deals. Fees follow the famous 2-and-20: roughly 2% annual management fee plus 20% of profits — the profit share is called carried interest — often only above a hurdle rate of ~8%. The cash-flow rhythm produces the J-curve: early years are all outflows (capital calls, fees) and markdowns, then exits start returning cash and the curve climbs.

Common pitfall: smooth ≠ safe

Private funds report returns from appraised valuations, updated quarterly by the GP itself, not from market prices. Stale, managed marks make the return series look eerily smooth — low "volatility," low "correlation" with stocks. Much of that is measurement artifact, not economics: if you only reprice your house once a year, its price chart is smooth too. Practitioners call this volatility laundering. When you build risk models in Section 16, de-smoothing private-asset returns is a real analytical task.

Hedge funds: the strategy zoo and the fee arithmetic

A hedge fund is best defined by what it's allowed to do that a mutual fund isn't: short selling, heavy leverage, derivatives, concentration. The name is historical; many hedge funds hedge nothing. The main strategy families:

Strategy The bet Example
Long/short equity Stock picking on both sides — long the good, short the bad Long AMD, short a weaker rival; market moves partly cancel
Global macro Big directional calls on rates, currencies, commodities Soros shorting the pound in 1992
Arbitrage / relative value Tiny pricing gaps between related securities, levered up Convertible bond vs the issuer's stock
Quantitative / systematic Statistical signals traded by algorithm at scale Renaissance, Two Sigma — where your skill set aims (Section 18)
Event-driven Mergers, bankruptcies, restructurings Buying a takeover target below the deal price

Now the fee arithmetic, because it's brutal and interview-relevant. Take 2-and-20 with the common simple convention: management fee first, then 20% of what remains. A fund earning 10% gross nets its investor (10% − 2%) × 0.80 = 6.4%. Compound that for a decade on $100: gross wealth grows to $259, net wealth to $186. The manager's take is the gap — $73, which is 46% of the $159 of gross profit. To justify itself net of fees, a hedge fund can't merely match the market; it needs persistent, large outperformance — exactly the thing Section 17 (Market Efficiency) says is rare.

One more filter for reading hedge-fund marketing: survivorship bias. Hedge fund indices are built from funds that voluntarily report; funds that blow up stop reporting and vanish from the history, and new funds often "backfill" only their good early years. Studies put the resulting overstatement of average returns at several percentage points a year. You already know this bug from ML — it's training on a dataset where the failures were silently dropped. Always ask what happened to the funds that aren't in the sample.

Crypto as an asset class: how an analyst frames it honestly

We'll cover blockchain mechanics in Section 19 (FinTech); here, treat crypto purely as a portfolio asset. Three facts dominate. First, volatility: bitcoin's annualized volatility has historically run 60–80%, versus roughly 15–20% for the S&P 500 — drawdowns of 70–80% have happened repeatedly. Second, custody risk: owning crypto means controlling cryptographic keys, and custody failures — lost keys, hacked wallets, or the exchange itself collapsing, as FTX did in 2022 — can produce a 100% loss with no market move at all. That failure mode simply doesn't exist for a Treasury bond in Section 6's custodial plumbing.

Third, and analytically deepest: no cash flows. A stock has earnings, a bond has coupons, a building has rent — so the discounting machinery of Section 1 and Section 13 (Valuation) can produce an intrinsic value to argue about. Bitcoin pays nothing, so its value rests entirely on scarcity (a hard-coded 21M supply cap) plus what the next holder will pay. That doesn't make it worthless — gold has traded on the same basis for millennia — but it changes the epistemics: there is no model that says bitcoin is "cheap at $40,000," only supply, demand, and narrative. The honest analyst's framing: a high-volatility, custody-fragile, cash-flow-free speculative asset whose diversification benefit is unstable — its correlation with tech stocks, near zero in the 2010s, jumped sharply in the 2022 tightening cycle, exactly when diversification was needed. Sized accordingly (small, survivable), it's a position; sized on faith, it's a lottery ticket with a login page.

Why alternatives at all — and the Part II instrument map

After all these fees, lockups and custody nightmares, why do pensions and endowments pour trillions into alternative investments? One word: correlation. Portfolio risk depends not just on each asset's volatility but on how assets move together — combine assets with correlation below 1 and the portfolio's volatility drops below the weighted average of the parts, a free lunch you'll derive properly in Section 15 (Portfolio Theory). Real assets that track inflation, macro funds that profit in chaos, farmland that ignores the Nasdaq: each imperfect correlation is a diversification credit. Two caveats keep you honest: correlations tend to spike toward 1 in a crisis (2008 taught that painfully), and some of the advertised low correlation is the appraisal smoothing you saw above. The pitch is real, but smaller than the brochure.

That closes Part II. You've now met every major instrument class — here's the map to keep:

Instrument What it is Who uses it Risk level Where you'll meet it in a job
Common stock (s07) Ownership share of a company's profits Everyone: retail to pensions Medium–high Equity research, any valuation work
Index funds / ETFs (s07) One ticker holding a whole market basket Retail, advisors, institutions Medium Asset management, fund analytics
Treasuries (s08) Loans to the US government; the risk-free benchmark Central banks, funds, banks Low (rate risk only) Every discount rate you'll ever compute
Corporate bonds (s08) Loans to companies, priced by credit spread Insurers, pensions, credit funds Low–medium Credit analysis, ratings, risk teams
Futures / forwards (s09) Locked-in future price for an asset Hedgers and speculators High (levered) Commodities desks, treasury, clearing
Options (s09) The right, not obligation, to buy/sell at a set price Hedgers, traders, employees High (asymmetric) Derivatives pricing, market risk, comp packages
Swaps (s09) Exchanging cash-flow streams (fixed vs floating) Banks, corporates, funds Varies Bank ALM, rates desks, XVA analytics
FX (s10) One currency priced in another Corporates, banks, macro funds Medium (levered: high) Treasury, international anything
Commodities (s10) Physical goods accessed via futures Producers, consumers, CTAs High Energy trading, inflation modeling
REITs (s10) Listed companies owning income property Income investors, allocators Medium–high Real-asset research, dividend models
PE / VC funds (s10) Locked-up funds buying private companies Institutions, the wealthy High + illiquid Deal teams, LP analytics, fund reporting
Hedge funds (s10) Flexible private funds; short, leverage, derivatives Institutions, the wealthy Varies wildly Quant research — the natural home for your skills
Crypto (s10) Digital scarce assets with no cash flows Retail, some institutions Very high FinTech, digital-asset risk teams

Check your understanding

1. EUR/USD rises from 1.10 to 1.25. Who is hurt?

EUR/USD rising means each euro costs more dollars. Anyone who must pay euros — like the importer — now needs more dollars to settle the same invoice. Euro receivers and euro-asset holders gain, and the European tourist finds America cheaper.

2. An oil ETF rolls its long futures position monthly while the curve is in steep contango and spot oil stays flat all year. The ETF most likely…

In contango the next contract is always pricier than the expiring one, and each contract tends to slide down toward spot as it ages. That negative roll yield produces losses even when spot goes nowhere.

3. A hedge fund returns 10% gross. Under 2-and-20 (management fee taken first, then 20% of the remainder), the investor nets about…

(10% − 2%) × 0.80 = 6.4%. The 2% management fee comes off the top, then the manager keeps 20% of the remaining 8%.

4. A building generates $30,000 of NOI and is priced at $600,000. Its cap rate is…

Cap rate = NOI ÷ price = 30,000 ÷ 600,000 = 5.0%. (20% is the inverted ratio — price over NOI gives the "multiple," 20×, not the yield.)

5. The core risk of a carry trade (borrow yen at 0.5%, invest in Australian dollars at 4.5%) is that…

Carry accrues slowly, but the FX loss when the low-rate funding currency rallies can arrive all at once — especially in a crowded unwind, as in August 2024. Steady nickels, occasional steamroller.

Key takeaways

  • FX quotes price the base currency in units of the quote currency: EUR/USD = 1.10 means one euro costs $1.10, and a rising quote means a stronger euro.
  • Currencies move on rate differentials, inflation gaps and capital flows; the carry trade harvests the rate gap and periodically gets crushed when it unwinds.
  • Companies hedge transaction exposure with forwards — trading upside away to lock certainty — while translation exposure is an accounting effect you'll meet in filings.
  • Commodity investors live on the futures curve: contango bleeds negative roll yield, backwardation pays positive roll yield, regardless of what spot does. Gold is the cash-flow-free anti-currency.
  • Real estate = leverage plus a computable yield: cap rate = NOI ÷ price, judged as a spread over Treasuries; REITs make property liquid at the cost of stock-like volatility.
  • PE and VC run on GP/LP fund mechanics, 2-and-20 with carry, and a J-curve; VC returns follow a power law where one winner carries the fund, and smooth private "returns" partly reflect stale appraisals.
  • Fee drag compounds: 10% gross becomes 6.4% net under 2-and-20 — $259 vs $186 on $100 over a decade — and survivorship bias inflates the track records you're shown.
  • Crypto is a high-volatility asset with custody risk and no cash flows to discount; alternatives in general earn their place only through genuine (not appraisal-smoothed) low correlation — the bridge to Section 15.

Part III · Corporate Finance & Accounting

11. Financial Statements: The Language of Business

Every company on Earth describes itself with the same three documents, and every analyst job you might want assumes you can read them fluently. By the end of this section you will be able to walk a single transaction through all three statements and explain why the balance sheet can never, ever fail to balance.

Why statements exist, and where to find them

A company is a black box that takes in money and (hopefully) produces more money. Financial statements are the API documentation for that box — a standardized, periodic report of what happened inside. They exist because the people who supply capital (shareholders, lenders) are usually not the people running the business, and they need a trustworthy way to check on their money. Regulators, tax authorities, employees, suppliers, and competitors read them too, but the primary audience is investors and creditors.

There are three statements, and they answer three different questions. The income statement asks: did we make a profit this period? The balance sheet asks: what do we own and owe right now? The cash flow statement asks: where did the cash actually go? A period report, a snapshot, and a reconciliation — think of them as a diff, a database dump, and a transaction log.

To keep the reports comparable, companies follow accounting rulebooks. US companies use GAAP; most of the rest of the world uses IFRS. They agree on the big ideas and differ in details (inventory methods, lease treatment, when development costs can be capitalized), so cross-border comparisons need care but not fear. An independent accounting firm performs an audit and signs an opinion that the statements fairly present reality — a useful check, though auditors verify process more than they hunt fraud.

For US public companies, everything is free. The 10-K is the audited annual report; the 10-Q is the quarterly version. Both live on EDGAR, the SEC's filing database (sec.gov/edgar). One career note before we dive in: this is the single most job-critical section of this course for analyst roles. Interviews for equity research, investment banking, credit, and FP&A all test exactly the material on this page — especially the three-statement linkage.

The accrual revolution: earned, not received

Here is the single biggest aha in accounting. Statements are not built on cash. They are built on accrual accounting: revenue is recorded when it is earned (you delivered the goods or service), and expenses are recorded when they are incurred (you consumed the resource) — no matter when money changes hands. The goal is to measure economic performance in the right period, not to track the bank account.

Meet our running example company: Cascade Roasters, Inc., a coffee roaster with $1,000M of annual revenue (we will use its full numbers in a moment). In December, Cascade ships $120k of beans to a hotel chain that pays 60 days later. Under accrual rules, December's income statement shows $120k of revenue recognition, and the balance sheet shows a $120k account receivable — an IOU asset. The bank account shows nothing until February.

Worked example: accrual and cash telling opposite stories

Two gyms open in January. Gym A sells 100 annual memberships at $1,200 each, all paid up front: $120,000 of cash lands in January, but it has only earned one month of service, so January revenue is $10,000 — the other $110,000 sits on the balance sheet as deferred revenue, a liability (it owes 11 months of gym).

Gym B bills companies $10,000 for January corporate memberships, payable in March. January revenue: $10,000. January cash: $0. Same January performance on the income statement; wildly different bank accounts. Accrual profit and cash flow are two different signals about the same business — you need both.

Expenses mirror this. If Cascade prepays a year of insurance, the cash leaves now but the expense hits the income statement one-twelfth per month. If employees earn December wages paid in January, December bears the expense. The machinery that makes this work is the subject of the next three subsections.

The income statement, line by line

The income statement is a subtraction cascade from the top line to the bottom line. Here is Cascade Roasters' year, in $ millions, with the margin at each level — profit as a percent of revenue, the normalized metric analysts actually compare across companies.

Line $M Margin What it means
Revenue 1,000.0 — value of goods/services delivered this year
− COGS 400.0 — green coffee, roasting labor, packaging
= Gross profit 600.0 60.0% profit on the product itself
− Operating expenses 300.0 — S&M 150, R&D 40, G&A 110
= EBITDA 300.0 30.0% operating profit before D&A
− D&A 50.0 — wear on roasters, trucks, software
= EBIT 250.0 25.0% operating profit
− Interest expense 20.0 — cost of the $300M of debt
= Pre-tax income 230.0 23.0% what the tax authority sees
− Tax (21%) 48.3 — 230 × 0.21
= Net income 181.7 18.2% the bottom line, owned by shareholders

Now the vocabulary. COGS covers only the direct cost of what was sold; subtracting it gives gross profit. Operating expenses (opex) are the overhead of being a company: S&M (sales & marketing), R&D (research & development), and G&A (general & administrative — executives, lawyers, HR). Subtract those and you get EBITDA, beloved by bankers because it strips out financing and accounting-policy noise.

Then come the non-cash charges. Depreciation expenses a machine's cost over its life instead of all at once; amortization does the same for intangibles — together, D&A. Subtracting D&A gives EBIT, then interest and tax take their cuts, leaving net income. Note the ordering encodes a priority queue of claims from Section 6: suppliers and employees get paid inside COGS and opex, lenders take interest, the government takes tax, and shareholders get whatever residual remains.

The balance sheet: an invariant that never breaks

The balance sheet is a snapshot at one instant — midnight on the last day of the period. Its whole structure is one identity:

Assets = Liabilities + Equity
what the company owns = who financed it (lenders) + who owns the residual (shareholders)

An asset is a resource with future value; a liability is a promise to outsiders; equity is whatever is left for the owners. Why does it always balance? Because of double-entry bookkeeping: every transaction is recorded twice, in a way that preserves the identity. In CS terms, Assets = Liabilities + Equity is an invariant, and every transaction is an atomic commit that must leave the invariant intact. Borrow $50? Cash (asset) +50 and debt (liability) +50. Buy a $30 roaster with cash? One asset up 30, another down 30 — net zero. Earn $10 of profit? Assets are up 10 and equity is up 10. There is no legal sequence of entries that can break it; if a balance sheet does not balance, someone made an arithmetic error, full stop.

The statement is sorted by liquidity. Current assets (cash, receivables, inventory) convert to cash within a year; non-current assets like PP&E are the long-lived machinery. Same split on the right: accounts payable and short-term debt are current liabilities; bonds and long-term loans are non-current. The gap between the two short-term sides is working capital — the money trapped in the gears of daily operations. Remember it: it is the villain of the cash flow story below.

Two more residents you will meet constantly. Goodwill appears when a company overpays (relative to identifiable net assets) in an acquisition — it is the accounting placeholder for "brand, team, synergies," and it gets written down when acquisitions sour. And equity itself is often called book value — the accountant's estimate of net worth, which can differ wildly from the stock market's estimate (Section 7): the market prices future cash flows, while the book records historical costs.

The cash flow statement: profit is an opinion, cash is a fact

Accrual accounting is powerful, but it runs on estimates: when revenue is earned, how long a roaster lasts, which receivables will actually be paid. Cash is the one number with no judgment in it — hence the analyst proverb, profit is an opinion, cash is a fact. The cash flow statement reconciles the two, sorted into three buckets.

CFO — cash flow from operations. Almost every company builds this with the indirect method: start from net income, then repair the accrual distortions. Add back non-cash expenses (D&A reduced profit but no cash left the building). Then adjust for working capital changes: if receivables grew, you booked revenue you have not collected — subtract it; if inventory grew, you spent cash on beans you have not sold — subtract; if payables grew, suppliers financed you — add. For Cascade in a steady year: net income 181.7 + D&A 50 ± working capital 0 = CFO 231.7.

CFI — cash flow from investing. Dominated by capex, plus acquisitions and purchases or sales of securities. Cascade spends 80 on new roasting lines: CFI −80. CFF — cash flow from financing: money moving between the company and its capital providers — borrowing and repaying debt, issuing shares, buybacks, and dividends. Cascade pays a 30 dividend and raises nothing: CFF −30. Net change in cash: 231.7 − 80 − 30 = +121.7, so cash climbs from 100 to 221.7. That ending number is the same "cash" line on the balance sheet — first glimpse of the linkage.

FCF = CFO − capex
free cash flow: cash the business generates after maintaining and growing its asset base — the raw material of valuation (Section 13)

Free cash flow for Cascade is 231.7 − 80 = 151.7. Why do analysts obsess over it instead of net income? Because growth consumes cash even while it manufactures profit. A company doubling every year must buy inventory and extend credit ahead of the revenue: profits look great, working capital devours the bank account. Plenty of profitable companies have died mid-growth-spurt because they ran out of actual money.

Common pitfall

"Net income up, so cash must be up" is the classic beginner error — and its mirror image matters too. Amazon ran near-zero accounting profit for years while gushing operating cash; many dot-com era firms showed rising profits while cash bled out through receivables. When profit and CFO diverge for several periods in a row, that gap is the first thing to investigate (Section 12 turns this into formal ratios).

How the three statements connect

The statements are not three files; they are three views of one database, joined on shared keys. Learn these four joins and you can rebuild any of them from the other two:

  • Net income → equity and CFO. The income statement's bottom line flows into balance-sheet equity as retained earnings (net income minus dividends), and the same number is the first line of the cash flow statement.
  • Capex → PP&E and CFI. Cash spent on equipment leaves through CFI and lands on the balance sheet as PP&E.
  • Borrowing → cash and debt via CFF. Raise debt and CFF brings cash in while the liability side records the obligation.
  • Ending cash closes the loop. The cash flow statement's final number is the balance sheet's cash line.

Worked example: the classic interview question

"Walk me through $10 of extra depreciation" — asked in nearly every banking and research interview. At a 21% tax rate: Income statement: D&A +10, so EBIT −10, tax −2.1, net income −7.9. Cash flow statement: start −7.9, add back the non-cash +10, so CFO +2.1 — cash actually rises because of the tax shield. Balance sheet: cash +2.1 and PP&E −10, so assets −7.9; retained earnings −7.9 on the other side. Assets down 7.9 = equity down 7.9. Balanced. Now try it live below.

Three-statement flow-through: Cascade Roasters

Drag the sliders and watch all three statements recompute ($ millions) — changed lines are shaded. Then press "Trace $100 of extra revenue" and step through the path a dollar of growth takes.

Income statement

Revenue
− COGS
= Gross profit
− Opex
= EBITDA
− D&A
= EBIT
− Interest
= Pre-tax
− Tax (21%)
= Net income

Cash flow statement

Net income
+ D&A (non-cash)
− Δ working capital
= CFO
− Capex
= CFI
+ New debt
− Dividends
= CFF
= Δ Cash
Ending cash

Balance sheet (year-end)

Cash
Op. working capital
PP&E (net)
= Total assets
Debt
Equity
= Total L + E
Assets − (L + E)
Net margin18.2%
Free cash flow (CFO − capex)$151.7M
Balance checkBalanced

Model assumptions: opex $300M and D&A $50M held flat; operating working capital = 20% of revenue; new debt is raised at year-end, so it adds cash but no interest this year; tax is zero when pre-tax income is negative; dividend fixed at $30M. Negative ending cash means the company would need financing.

Two things to notice as you play. First, the balance check never budges from zero — not because the widget cheats, but because the model is built from balanced entries, exactly like real accounting. Second, crank revenue growth and watch CFO lag net income: every extra dollar of sales traps twenty cents in working capital. That is Figure 11.4 happening under your fingers.

Reading a real 10-K in 15 minutes

A 10-K runs 100–300 pages, and most of it is boilerplate. Analysts triage. Here is a 15-minute routine that extracts perhaps 80% of the value:

  • Minutes 1–3: the three statements. Revenue trend, margins, CFO versus net income, debt level, cash. You now know the shape of the business.
  • Minutes 4–8: MD&A. Management explains, in prose, why the numbers moved. Skim for what changed year over year and how they describe demand, pricing, and costs.
  • Minutes 9–11: segment data. A "cloud company" that earns 70% of profit from legacy licenses is a different investment than its headline suggests. Segments reveal the real mix.
  • Minutes 12–14: footnotes, selectively. Revenue recognition policy, debt maturities, and anything the auditor flagged as a critical audit matter. Footnotes are where inconvenient facts go to hide in plain sight.
  • Minute 15: risk factors, diff only. The section is mostly legal boilerplate — but compare it against last year's filing and read what is new. Companies quietly disclose emerging problems here first. (A CS reflex that serves you well: never read the file, read the diff.)

Red flags preview

Keep a mental alarm list while you skim: net income growing while CFO shrinks for multiple periods; receivables or inventory growing much faster than revenue; frequent "one-time" charges that recur every year; auditor changes; heavy reliance on non-GAAP metrics that always look better than the GAAP ones. Section 12 turns these into quantitative ratio tests.

Your edge

Every filing on EDGAR is machine-readable — modern filings carry XBRL tags, and the SEC publishes bulk financial-statement datasets. While most analysts read one 10-K at a time, you can parse thousands: screen for the receivables-versus-revenue divergence above across the whole market, or diff risk-factor text programmatically. Statement literacy plus data skills is precisely the combination Section 18 builds toward.

Deep dive: why revenue recognition is where fraud lives

Most accounting scandals are, at bottom, revenue-timing scandals, because "earned" involves judgment. Under the ASC 606 standard, a company must identify its contractual performance obligations and recognize revenue as each is satisfied — but multi-year contracts, bundled products, and bill-and-hold arrangements leave room to be aggressive. Classic tricks: channel stuffing (shipping unordered product to distributors at quarter-end and booking it as sales), recognizing multi-year contract value up front, and booking barter or round-trip transactions as revenue.

The tell is almost always on the other statements: fictional revenue creates receivables that never convert to cash, so days sales outstanding stretches and CFO decouples from net income. Enron, one of the largest frauds in history, reported rising profits while operating cash flow told a much uglier story. This is why the linkage matters — a lie on one statement must leave fingerprints on the other two, because the invariant always has to hold.

Check your understanding

1. Cascade delivers $200k of coffee to a client in December; the client pays in February. Under accrual accounting, when does Cascade record the revenue?

Accrual accounting recognizes revenue when it is earned — at delivery. Until payment arrives, the $200k sits on the balance sheet as an account receivable.

2. Depreciation increases by $10 (tax rate 21%), nothing else changes. What happens to the company's cash?

Net income falls $7.9 (10 less 2.1 of tax saved), but the full $10 is added back on the cash flow statement since no cash left. Net effect: +10 − 7.9 = +$2.1 — the tax shield.

3. Why can a legitimate balance sheet never fail to balance?

The identity is an invariant. Every transaction is a balanced entry — like an atomic commit that must leave the invariant intact — so no sequence of valid entries can break it.

4. A fast-growing retailer reports record net income, but CFO has been negative for four straight quarters. The most likely explanation is:

Working capital consumes cash during growth: inventory is bought and credit extended before revenue is collected. Profit is an opinion; cash is a fact — and this divergence is normal for fast growers but deadly if financing dries up. (Faster depreciation would raise CFO, not lower it.)

5. A company reports CFO of $500M and capex of $180M. Its free cash flow is:

FCF = CFO − capex = 500 − 180 = $320M: the cash left over after operating the business and maintaining its asset base. Capex is very much a cash outflow, just reported in CFI rather than CFO.

Key takeaways

  • Three statements, three questions: the income statement measures period profit, the balance sheet snapshots what is owned and owed, the cash flow statement tracks actual cash. US GAAP and IFRS are the two rulebooks; audited 10-Ks and 10-Qs are free on EDGAR.
  • Accrual accounting records revenue when earned and expenses when incurred — so reported profit and the bank balance can tell opposite stories in any given period.
  • The income statement is a cascade — revenue → gross profit → EBITDA → EBIT → net income — and the margin at each checkpoint tells you where the money leaks.
  • Assets = Liabilities + Equity is an invariant enforced by double-entry bookkeeping: every transaction is a balanced entry, so the sheet cannot fail to balance.
  • Profit is an opinion, cash is a fact: CFO starts from net income, adds back non-cash charges, and subtracts the working capital that growth traps. FCF = CFO − capex.
  • The statements link through shared lines: net income feeds retained earnings and the top of the CFS; capex flows through CFI into PP&E; the CFS's ending cash is the balance sheet's cash.
  • Read 10-Ks by triage — statements, MD&A, segments, footnotes, then the diff of the risk factors — and treat a persistent profit-versus-CFO divergence as the number-one red flag.
  • For analyst interviews, the three-statement walk-through (e.g., $10 of depreciation) is the single most-tested skill in this course. Drill it until it is reflex.

Part III · Corporate Finance & Accounting

12. Ratio Analysis: Reading a Company Like a Pro

In Section 11 you learned to read the three financial statements — the raw tables. Ratios are the queries you run against those tables, and by the end of this section you'll be able to size up a company's profitability, safety, efficiency, and price tag in about ten minutes.

Why ratios: the great normalizer

Raw financial numbers are almost meaningless on their own. "This company earned $1.25 billion last year" — is that good? For a startup, spectacular. For Walmart, a catastrophe. The number only becomes information when you divide it by something: profit per dollar of sales, profit per dollar of assets, debt per dollar of earnings. Division strips out size, and once size is gone you can compare a corner grocer to a global chain, or this year's company to the same company a decade ago.

Two golden rules govern every ratio you will ever compute. Rule one: always compare against peers. A 2.5% net margin is dreadful for a software firm and perfectly healthy for a supermarket — every industry has its own gravity. Rule two: always compare against history. A company whose margin slid from 8% to 5% over three years is telling you a story that a single snapshot never could. A ratio in isolation, with no peer and no trend, is close to worthless.

And one rule about what ratios are for: they generate questions, not answers. If inventory turnover suddenly halves, the ratio doesn't tell you why — maybe demand collapsed, maybe management is deliberately stocking up before a price increase, maybe a new warehouse just opened. The ratio's job is to point your flashlight at the right paragraph of the 10-K. Analysts who treat ratios as verdicts get burned; analysts who treat them as anomaly detectors get promoted.

Your edge: this is feature engineering

You already do this for a living. Raw statements are the source tables; ratios are derived features — normalized, scale-invariant, comparable across entities and time, exactly like converting raw counts to per-capita rates or click totals to click-through rates before feeding a model. Quant equity funds literally use these ratios as model inputs (Section 18). The whole section is one big feature_pipeline.py for companies.

Throughout this section we'll follow two deliberately opposite companies: SteadyMart, a grocery chain ($50 billion of revenue, thin margins, warehouses full of inventory), and CloudNine, a SaaS firm ($2 billion of revenue, fat margins, no inventory at all). Same ratios, wildly different profiles — and that contrast is the fastest way to build intuition.

Profitability: margins, ROA, ROE, ROIC

Profitability ratios come in two families: profit per dollar of revenue (margins) and profit per dollar of capital (returns). The margins form a ladder down the income statement from Section 11:

  • Gross margin = (Revenue − COGS) ÷ Revenue. What's left after the direct cost of the product itself. SteadyMart: (50,000 − 37,500) ÷ 50,000 = 25%. CloudNine: (2,000 − 400) ÷ 2,000 = 80% — serving one more customer costs a SaaS firm almost nothing.
  • Operating margin = Operating income ÷ Revenue. After SG&A, R&D, depreciation. SteadyMart: 2,000 ÷ 50,000 = 4%. CloudNine: 400 ÷ 2,000 = 20%.
  • Net margin = Net income ÷ Revenue. The bottom line. SteadyMart: 1,250 ÷ 50,000 = 2.5%. CloudNine: 300 ÷ 2,000 = 15%.

Now the returns family. ROA (net income ÷ total assets) asks how much profit each dollar of stuff produces: SteadyMart 1,250 ÷ 20,000 = 6.25%; CloudNine 300 ÷ 3,000 = 10%. ROE (net income ÷ equity) asks how much each dollar of the owners' money earns: SteadyMart 1,250 ÷ 8,000 = 15.6%; CloudNine 300 ÷ 2,400 = 12.5%. Notice something strange: CloudNine crushes SteadyMart on every margin, yet SteadyMart's ROE is higher. Hold that thought — the DuPont decomposition below explains it.

The most important return of all is ROIC, because it ignores financing tricks and measures the business engine itself:

ROIC = NOPAT ÷ Invested capital = EBIT × (1 − t) ÷ (Equity + Debt − Cash)
NOPAT = net operating profit after tax · t = tax rate · invested capital = all funding actually at work

SteadyMart: NOPAT = 2,000 × (1 − 0.23) ≈ 1,540; invested capital = 8,000 + 7,500 − 1,000 = 14,500; ROIC ≈ 10.6%. CloudNine: NOPAT ≈ 308; invested capital = 2,400 + 200 − 1,200 = 1,400; ROIC ≈ 22%. Here CloudNine's quality shows: it needs barely any capital to run.

Key idea: ROIC vs the cost of capital is THE test

Every dollar a company invests has a price — the cost of capital, the return its lenders and shareholders demand (formalized as WACC in Section 14). A company creates value only when ROIC exceeds that hurdle. ROIC of 10.6% against an 8% cost of capital creates value; ROIC of 7% against 9% destroys value, and growing faster just destroys it faster. This single comparison is the spine of valuation in Section 13.

DuPont: taking ROE apart like an engineer

ROE is the headline number shareholders watch, but it hides how the return was earned. In the 1910s an analyst at DuPont noticed you can factor it into three ratios that each tell a separate story — multiply revenue/revenue and assets/assets into the definition and the algebra telescopes:

ROE = Net incomeRevenue × RevenueAssets × AssetsEquity = Margin × Turnover × Leverage
margin = pricing power · asset turnover = asset efficiency · leverage = balance-sheet amplification

The classic contrast is a supermarket versus a luxury brand. A supermarket earns roughly 2% net margin but flips its asset base 3× a year and runs 3× leverage: 2% × 3.0 × 3.0 = 18% ROE. A luxury handbag maker earns a 30% margin but turns its assets only 0.5× a year with modest 1.2× leverage: 30% × 0.5 × 1.2 = 18% ROE. Identical destination, opposite routes — one is a volume machine, the other a pricing machine.

Our two companies replay the same story. SteadyMart: 2.5% × 2.50 × 2.50 = 15.6%. CloudNine: 15% × 0.67 × 1.25 = 12.5%. That's why the "worse" business had the higher ROE: it compensates for razor-thin margins with relentless turnover and a leveraged balance sheet. The decomposition also flags risk — an ROE built on the leverage lever can be inflated at will by borrowing, and it evaporates just as fast in a downturn. Two firms with the same ROE are not the same investment.

Liquidity and solvency: can it pay its bills, and can it survive?

Liquidity asks whether the company can cover the next twelve months of bills; solvency asks whether the whole capital structure is sustainable. Lenders — the people you met in Section 8 (Fixed Income) — live and die by these ratios, and they hard-code them into loan contracts as covenants.

The short-term pair: the current ratio (current assets ÷ current liabilities) and the stricter quick ratio, which throws out inventory because you can't always sell it quickly at full price. SteadyMart: current = 4,700 ÷ 4,500 = 1.04, quick = (1,000 + 700) ÷ 4,500 = 0.38. CloudNine: both = 1,600 ÷ 400 = 4.0. On paper SteadyMart looks scary — but a grocer converts inventory to cash daily, so a low ratio is normal for the industry. Golden rule one again: thresholds are industry-relative. The gap between the two ratios is essentially working capital tied up in inventory.

The long-term set, in the order a credit analyst checks them:

Ratio Formula Rule-of-thumb thresholds lenders use SteadyMart CloudNine
Debt / equity Total debt ÷ equity < 1.0 conservative; > 2.0 aggressive (varies by industry) 0.94 0.08
Net debt / EBITDA (Net debt) ÷ EBITDA < 2× conservative; 2–3× typical investment grade; > 4–5× leveraged / high-yield territory 2.3× net cash
Interest coverage EBIT ÷ interest expense > 4–5× comfortable; 2–3× on watch; < 1.5× distress 5.3× 40×

Work one through: SteadyMart's net debt is 7,500 − 1,000 = 6,500, and EBITDA is 2,000 + 800 = 2,800, so net debt ÷ EBITDA ≈ 2.3× — roughly "it would take 2.3 years of gross operating earnings to repay all debt." That sits comfortably in investment-grade territory. Interest coverage of 2,000 ÷ 375 = 5.3× means operating profit could shrink by four-fifths before interest becomes unpayable. CloudNine holds more cash than debt (net debt = 200 − 1,200 = −1,000): a lender's dream, though shareholders may ask why all that idle cash isn't earning its cost of capital.

Common pitfall: liquidity ≠ solvency

A company can be liquid but insolvent (plenty of cash today, hopeless debt load long-term) or solvent but illiquid (valuable assets, no cash this quarter — the classic bank-run shape from Section 6). Firms almost always die of illiquidity first: the cash runs out before the balance sheet formally fails. Check both, always.

Efficiency and the cash conversion cycle

Efficiency ratios measure how fast a company spins its working capital. Three clocks matter. Inventory turnover (COGS ÷ inventory) counts how many times a year the shelves are cleared: SteadyMart does 37,500 ÷ 3,000 = 12.5 turns, i.e. goods sit for DIO = 365 ÷ 12.5 ≈ 29 days. DSO (receivables ÷ revenue × 365) counts how long customers take to pay: SteadyMart's shoppers pay at the register, so DSO ≈ 5 days; CloudNine invoices enterprises on slow terms, so DSO = 400 ÷ 2,000 × 365 = 73 days. DPO (payables ÷ COGS × 365) counts how long the company takes to pay its own suppliers: SteadyMart stretches to about 39 days.
