Chain the clocks together and you get the single most underrated ratio in finance, the cash conversion cycle:

CCC = DIO + DSO − DPO
days your cash is trapped = days goods sit + days customers owe you − days you owe suppliers

SteadyMart: 29.2 + 5.1 − 38.9 = −4.6 days. Read that timeline carefully: it buys groceries on day 0, sells them around day 29, collects by day 34 — and doesn't pay its supplier until day 39. Customers' cash arrives before the supplier's bill leaves. CloudNine, despite its beautiful margins, runs 0 + 73 − 54.8 ≈ +18 days: every dollar of new sales temporarily consumes cash while invoices age.

Real world: Amazon's negative-CCC superpower

For most of its history Amazon has run a CCC around minus 20 to 30 days: customers pay by card instantly, inventory turns fast, and suppliers wait two to three months. Every incremental sale therefore generates float — an interest-free loan from suppliers that grows automatically with revenue. That float helped fund Amazon's expansion for decades with less need for outside capital. A negative CCC means growth feeds cash instead of eating it, which is why analysts treat it as a structural superpower.

Market ratios: what the price tag says

Everything so far used only the statements. Market ratios add one outside ingredient — the stock price from Section 7 — and ask: what are investors paying for all this? SteadyMart trades at a market cap of 20,000; CloudNine at 9,000.

  • P/E = price ÷ earnings. SteadyMart: 20,000 ÷ 1,250 = 16×. CloudNine: 9,000 ÷ 300 = 30×. The market pays almost twice as much per dollar of CloudNine's profit — it's pricing in growth and that 22% ROIC. Best for stable, profitable firms; meaningless when earnings are negative or temporarily depressed.
  • P/B = price ÷ book value. SteadyMart 2.5×, CloudNine 3.75×. Most useful for banks and insurers, whose assets are financial and marked close to reality; least useful for firms whose real assets (brands, code) never hit the balance sheet.
  • EV/EBITDA = enterprise value ÷ EBITDA. EV adds net debt to market cap, so the ratio is capital-structure-neutral — ideal for comparing firms with different debt loads, and the workhorse of buyout analysis. SteadyMart: (20,000 + 6,500) ÷ 2,800 ≈ 9.5×. CloudNine: (9,000 − 1,000) ÷ 460 ≈ 17.4×.
  • FCF yield = free cash flow ÷ market cap. SteadyMart: 1,150 ÷ 20,000 = 5.75%; CloudNine: 350 ÷ 9,000 ≈ 3.9%. The hardest multiple to fake, because it's built on cash (remember Section 11: cash is the ground truth).
  • Dividend yield = dividends ÷ price. SteadyMart pays 500, so 2.5%; CloudNine pays nothing, reinvesting everything at its 22% ROIC — which, given the ROIC-vs-cost-of-capital test, is exactly what it should do.

A crucial reframe before Section 13: a multiple is not a valuation, it's shorthand for one. "30× earnings" is only cheap or expensive relative to the growth, ROIC, and risk behind it. Valuation is the discipline of checking whether the multiple is justified; ratios just tell you which multiples deserve the interrogation.

Two-company ratio lab: SteadyMart vs CloudNine

Same two companies, five lenses. Click through the ratio families and watch the DuPont view explain how a 2.5%-margin grocer out-ROEs an 80%-gross-margin SaaS firm. All figures in $ millions.

Mini-financials ($M) SteadyMart (grocery) CloudNine (SaaS)
Revenue 50,000 2,000
COGS 37,500 400
Operating income (EBIT) 2,000 400
EBITDA 2,800 460
Interest expense 375 10
Net income 1,250 300
Cash 1,000 1,200
Receivables 700 400
Inventory 3,000 0
Total assets 20,000 3,000
Current assets / current liabilities 4,700 / 4,500 1,600 / 400
Accounts payable (within CL) 4,000 60
Total debt 7,500 200
Shareholders' equity 8,000 2,400
Market cap 20,000 9,000
Free cash flow / dividends 1,150 / 500 350 / 0

Earnings quality and red flags: a forensic mini-checklist

Every ratio above trusts the reported numbers. But Section 11 taught you that accounting runs on accruals — management judgment calls about when revenue is earned and costs are incurred. Earnings quality is the question of how much judgment is propping up the bottom line, and a short forensic checklist catches most of the classic games:

  • Net income growing while operating cash flow shrinks. Profits are an opinion; cash is a fact. When the two diverge for several quarters, the "profit" is increasingly made of accruals — book revenue that hasn't been collected, or costs that haven't been recognized. This single check would have flagged Enron years early.
  • Receivables growing faster than revenue. If sales rise 10% but receivables rise 30%, the company is either extending desperate credit terms or channel stuffing — borrowing next quarter's sales to make this quarter's number. Watch DSO trend upward.
  • Serial "one-time" charges. A restructuring charge every single year is not one-time; it's a recurring cost that management wants you to exclude. Compare "adjusted" earnings to plain GAAP earnings — a persistently widening gap is a tell.
  • Aggressive capitalization. When a company chooses to capitalize everyday costs, expenses vanish from the income statement into the balance sheet, inflating today's profit. WorldCom capitalized $3.8 billion of routine network fees this way — for a while, it worked.
  • Inventory outrunning sales. Stock piling up faster than revenue usually precedes markdowns and margin pain — the turnover ratio sniffs it out before management admits it.

Notice that every flag is itself a ratio or a comparison of growth rates — CFO ÷ net income, ΔAR ÷ Δrevenue, adjusted ÷ GAAP earnings. Forensic accounting is ratio analysis with a suspicious mind. None of these flags proves fraud; each one earns the company a spot on your "read the footnotes" list.

Deep dive: when ratios break

Every ratio has failure modes, and knowing them separates users from tourists. Negative equity: a company that has bought back more stock than it ever earned (Home Depot and Starbucks have both been here) can have negative book equity, making ROE and debt/equity mathematically meaningless — the denominators flip sign. Use ROIC and net debt/EBITDA instead. Negative earnings: P/E is undefined for loss-makers; analysts fall back on EV/Sales or forward earnings estimates, each with its own hazards. EBITDA skepticism: EBITDA ignores capital expenditure entirely, which is why Warren Buffett quips that managements touting EBITDA are hoping you'll forget that depreciation is a real cost — a telecom that must replace its network every seven years cannot pay creditors with EBITDA. Point-in-time denominators: balance-sheet items are snapshots, so a retailer's year-end inventory (measured after the holiday sell-down) understates its true average; careful analysts use averages of beginning and ending balances. The pattern: whenever a ratio looks absurd, check whether a denominator is small, negative, or unrepresentative before drawing any conclusion.

Check your understanding

1. Firm A has 10% net margin, 1.5× asset turnover, 1.0× leverage. Firm B has 2% margin, 2.5× turnover, 3.0× leverage. Both show 15% ROE. Which statement is most accurate?

DuPont shows the levers: A earns its 15% with no leverage at all, while B multiplies a thin margin by 3× borrowed money (2% × 2.5 × 3.0 = 15%). B's ROE would collapse fastest in a downturn, when leverage cuts both ways.

2. A company earns 7% ROIC while its cost of capital is 9%, and it's reinvesting heavily to grow revenue 20% a year. What is it doing to shareholder value?

Value is created only when ROIC exceeds the cost of capital. Earning 7% on money that costs 9% loses 2 cents per dollar invested, so pouring more capital in at that spread just scales up the loss.

3. A retailer has a cash conversion cycle of −8 days. What does that mean?

Negative CCC means DPO exceeds DIO + DSO: money comes in before it goes out. Growth then generates interest-free float rather than consuming cash — the Amazon-style superpower.

4. Net income has grown 15% a year for three years, but operating cash flow has fallen each year and DSO keeps climbing. The most likely explanation?

Rising DSO plus profits diverging from cash means revenue is being recognized well ahead of collection — possibly channel stuffing. (Note the third option has it backwards: depreciation lowers net income, not operating cash flow.)

5. You want to compare two similar manufacturers, one debt-free and one financed 60% with debt. Which multiple compares their operating businesses most cleanly?

P/E is distorted by capital structure — the levered firm's earnings are reduced by interest while its equity is smaller. EV/EBITDA prices the whole business (equity plus net debt) against profit measured before financing costs, making the two comparable.

Key takeaways

  • Ratios normalize away size — they are engineered features derived from raw statements. Always compare against peers and against the company's own history, and treat every ratio as a question generator, not a verdict.
  • Margins (gross → operating → net) measure profit per dollar of sales; ROA, ROE, and ROIC measure profit per dollar of capital. ROIC versus the cost of capital is the single test of whether a company creates value.
  • DuPont factors ROE into margin × turnover × leverage: a 2%-margin supermarket and a 30%-margin luxury house can post identical ROE through opposite levers — and ROE built on leverage is the fragile kind.
  • Lenders live on liquidity and solvency ratios: quick ratio near 1, net debt/EBITDA under ~3×, and interest coverage above ~4–5× are the classic comfort zones, all industry-dependent.
  • The cash conversion cycle (DIO + DSO − DPO) counts the days cash is trapped in operations; a negative CCC means suppliers finance the business and growth generates float instead of consuming cash.
  • Market multiples are shorthand valuations: P/E for stable earners, P/B for banks, EV/EBITDA across capital structures, FCF yield when you want the number hardest to fake.
  • Earnings quality checks are ratios too: cash flow diverging from net income, receivables outrunning revenue, serial one-time charges, and aggressive capitalization are the four flags that catch most accounting games.

Part III · Corporate Finance & Accounting

13. Valuation: What Is a Company Worth?

Every price on a stock screen is an opinion; valuation is how you form your own. By the end of this section you will be able to build a discounted cash flow model from scratch, sanity-check it with multiples, and answer the classic interview question "walk me through a DCF" without breaking a sweat.

Price is what you pay, value is what you estimate

In Section 7 (Equities) you saw that a stock's market price is just the last price at which two strangers agreed to trade. That number is a fact. What the company is actually worth — its intrinsic value — is not a fact. It's an estimate, and every serious valuation comes with error bars, whether the analyst admits it or not.

There are two big families of valuation. Intrinsic valuation asks: what cash will this business hand its owners over its lifetime, and what is that stream worth today? That's the discounted cash flow (DCF) approach, and it's just the time-value machinery from Section 1 pointed at an entire company. Relative valuation asks a lazier but often smarter question: what are similar companies selling for right now? That's the world of multiples and comparable companies.

Analogy

Pricing a house works the same way. Intrinsic: estimate the rent it could earn forever and discount it. Relative: check what the three nearly identical houses on the same street sold for last quarter. Neither is "the truth" — a good agent runs both and gets suspicious when they disagree wildly.

Here's the mindset shift that separates professionals from spreadsheet jockeys: a valuation model is structured estimation under uncertainty. You are not computing the answer; you are organizing your assumptions so that the disagreement between your number and the market's number is legible. If your DCF says $31.50 and the market says $28, the model tells you exactly which beliefs — growth, margins, risk — you'd have to hold for either number to be right. That's the entire point.

Your edge

You already do this. A valuation model is a forward inference with hand-tuned priors: point estimates in, point estimate out, and the honest deliverable is a sensitivity analysis, not a single number. Treat "intrinsic value = $31.50" the way you'd treat a regression prediction with no confidence interval — politely, but skeptically.

A DCF end to end: every single number

Meet Cascade Beverages, our fictional workhorse. It generated free cash flow (FCF) of $100M this year, carries net debt of $200M, has 50M shares outstanding, and trades at $28 per share. FCF is the star of the show: it comes from the statements you built in Section 11 — roughly operating profit after tax, plus depreciation, minus capital expenditure and working-capital growth. It's what's left for the people who financed the business.

A DCF has five moves. Move 1 — project the explicit years. We forecast FCF for 5 years. Assume Cascade grows FCF at 10% per year:

Year FCF ($M) Discount factor (÷1.10t) Present value ($M)
1 110.00 0.9091 100.00
2 121.00 0.8264 100.00
3 133.10 0.7513 100.00
4 146.41 0.6830 100.00
5 161.05 0.6209 100.00
Sum 500.00

(Each present value is exactly $100M because we happened to pick growth equal to the discount rate — 10% growth compounding up, 10% discounting back down. A tidy coincidence, and a reminder that growth only creates value when it beats the discount rate.)

Move 2 — the terminal value. Cascade doesn't vaporize after year 5, so we need the value of everything beyond the forecast horizon. The standard tool is the Gordon growth model: assume FCF grows at a modest constant rate g forever, and value that perpetuity. With terminal growth g = 2% and a discount rate (WACC, next subsection) of 10%:

TV = FCF5 × (1 + g) ÷ (WACC − g) = 161.05 × 1.02 ÷ (0.10 − 0.02) = $2,053.4M
TV = terminal value at end of year 5 · g = perpetual growth rate · WACC = discount rate

Move 3 — discount everything to today. The explicit years are already done ($500M). The terminal value sits at the end of year 5, so it gets the year-5 discount factor: 2,053.4 × 0.6209 = $1,275.0M. Adding up gives enterprise value (EV): 500 + 1,275 = $1,775M. Notice the uncomfortable ratio: the terminal value is 72% of the total. Most of a typical DCF is the part you know least about.

Move 4 — from the business to the shareholders. EV belongs to everyone who financed the company. Lenders have first claim, so subtract net debt to get equity value: 1,775 − 200 = $1,575M. Move 5 — per share. Divide by 50M shares: $31.50. Against the $28 market price, our model implies about 12.5% upside — or, said honestly, our assumptions are about 12.5% more optimistic than the market's.

WACC: the price tag on risk

We discounted at 10%. Where did that come from? The discount rate for a whole company is the WACC — weighted average cost of capital. The logic: a company is financed by a mix of debt and equity, each with its own required return, so the business as a whole must clear a blend of the two. It is literally a weighted average — no scarier than that.

WACC = (E ÷ V) × re + (D ÷ V) × rd × (1 − t)
E = market value of equity · D = market value of debt · V = E + D · re = cost of equity · rd = cost of debt · t = tax rate

The cost of equity is the return shareholders demand. The workhorse estimate is the CAPM: re = rf + β × ERP, where rf is the risk-free rate (a long Treasury yield, from Section 8), beta measures how much the stock swings with the market, and the equity risk premium (ERP) is the extra return the market demands over Treasuries. The full story behind CAPM — diversification, why only market risk gets paid — lives in Section 15 (Portfolio Theory); for now, treat it as a pricing formula for risk.

For Cascade: rf = 4%, β = 1.25, ERP = 5.5% → re = 4% + 1.25 × 5.5% = 10.9%. The cost of debt is what lenders charge — say 6% — but interest is tax-deductible, creating a tax shield, so the after-tax cost at a 25% tax rate is 6% × 0.75 = 4.5%. Weight by market values: equity = 50M × $28 = $1,400M; debt = $300M (gross debt; the $200M net debt is $300M debt minus $100M cash). So:

WACC = (1,400 ÷ 1,700) × 10.9% + (300 ÷ 1,700) × 4.5% = 8.98% + 0.79% ≈ 9.8%
We round to 10% for clean arithmetic — and because pretending WACC is precise to two decimals is theater.

Why does riskier mean a higher discount rate? Because a dollar you might not get is worth less than a dollar you will. Discounting a volatile biotech's projected cash flows at 6% quietly asserts they're as dependable as a utility's — and the model will happily reward that lie with a huge valuation. The discount rate is where skepticism about the cash flows gets encoded. (How companies choose their debt-equity mix — and whether WACC can be engineered down — is the subject of Section 14.)

Common pitfall

Weighting WACC with book values from the balance sheet instead of market values, or using yesterday's interest rates for rf. WACC is a current market opportunity cost: what investors could earn elsewhere today, not what the company paid historically.

The embarrassing truth: sensitivity

Now for the part practitioners whisper about. Nudge WACC from 10% to 9% and terminal growth from 2% to 3%, and Cascade's per-share value jumps from $31.50 to $42.22. Push the other way — WACC 11%, growth 1% — and it drops to $25.04. That's a 69% swing from moving two inputs by one percentage point each, inputs nobody can estimate to within a point anyway. The culprit is the terminal value's denominator, WACC − g: it's a small difference of two uncertain numbers, and dividing by a small shaky number is exactly as unstable as it sounds.

Honest analysts respond in two ways. First, the sensitivity table: a grid of valuations across WACC and g, so the reader sees the whole distribution instead of a false point estimate. Second, the football field chart: the valuation range from each method drawn as horizontal bars, with the market price overlaid. Where the bars overlap is where your conviction should live.

Try both live. The model below is the exact Cascade DCF from above — same base FCF, same net debt, same share count — with the three big assumptions on sliders.

Mini DCF model: Cascade Beverages

Base FCF $100M, net debt $200M, 50M shares, market price $28. Move the sliders and watch the value — and the sensitivity grid — react.

Enterprise value—
Equity value—
Value per share—
vs $28 market price—
Per-share value g −1pp g g +1pp
WACC −1pp — — —
WACC — — —
WACC +1pp — — —

Cells are shaded by upside (toward green) or downside (toward red) vs the $28 market price. The grid clamps WACC − g to at least 0.5pp so the perpetuity never divides by zero — watch values explode as the spread shrinks.

Play with it for two minutes and the lesson lands: the explicit-year sliders barely move the answer; WACC and terminal growth dominate. That's why interviewers probe your discount-rate reasoning and mostly shrug at your year-3 revenue estimate.

Relative valuation: comps and precedents

Relative valuation outsources the hard assumptions to the market. The comps method (comparable companies) runs in five steps. Step 1: choose the peer set — companies with similar business models, growth, margins, and size. This is the step that quietly decides your answer; put a sleepy bottler in a peer set of energy-drink rockets and everything downstream is garbage-in. Step 2: pull each peer's multiples. Step 3: take the median (medians resist the one weird outlier). Step 4: apply it to your company's metric. Step 5: convert to per-share value.

The two workhorse multiples divide the world by capital structure. EV/EBITDA compares the whole business to a pre-interest profit measure (you met EBITDA in Section 12), so it's immune to how much debt each peer carries — ideal when leverage varies across the set, and standard in deal-making. The P/E ratio compares equity price to equity earnings — fine when peers have similar debt loads, and the default for quick public-market comparisons. Mixing them up is a classic rookie tell: never put an equity price over a pre-interest profit measure, or you're comparing numerators and denominators that belong to different claimants.

Worked example

Cascade's EBITDA is $180M. Five peers trade at EV/EBITDA of 9.2×, 10.1×, 10.8×, 11.5×, and 14.0× (median 10.8×). Implied EV = 10.8 × 180 = $1,944M. Subtract $200M net debt → equity $1,744M → ÷ 50M shares → $34.88. Note the same tail-end steps as the DCF: multiples on EV still require the EV → equity → per-share march.

Precedent transactions are comps built from actual acquisitions instead of trading prices. They come out systematically higher because buyers pay a control premium — typically 20–40% over the pre-announcement price — for the right to run the company, fire the CEO, and capture synergies. Use precedents to value a company being sold; use trading comps to value shares you'd merely hold.

Key idea

A multiple is a compressed DCF. EV/EBITDA of 10.8× is shorthand for a whole bundle of growth, margin, and risk assumptions the market is making about the peers. Comps don't avoid assumptions — they borrow the market's, which is great exactly when the market knows more than you and dangerous exactly when it doesn't (see Section 17 on bubbles).

"Walk me through a DCF" — the interview answer

This is the single most common technical question in finance interviews. Here is an answer you can deliver verbatim in about sixty seconds — every clause of which you can now defend:

Say this

  1. "A DCF values a company as the present value of its future free cash flows. I'd start by projecting unlevered free cash flow for five to ten years — revenue growth down to operating profit, taxed, plus depreciation, minus capex and the change in working capital."
  2. "Next I'd estimate a terminal value for everything beyond the projection window, either with the Gordon growth method — final-year FCF times one plus g, over WACC minus g — or an exit multiple on terminal-year EBITDA, and I'd cross-check one against the other."
  3. "Then I'd discount the explicit cash flows and the terminal value back to today at the WACC — cost of equity from CAPM, after-tax cost of debt, weighted by market values. That sum is enterprise value."
  4. "To get to what shareholders own, I'd subtract net debt — and any preferred stock or minority interests — to reach equity value, then divide by diluted shares for a per-share value."
  5. "Finally I'd sensitize it — a WACC-versus-terminal-growth grid — because the terminal value usually carries 60 to 80 percent of the total, and I'd triangulate against trading comps and precedent transactions before trusting the number."

Two follow-ups arrive almost every time. "Why do you subtract net debt?" Because enterprise value belongs to all capital providers and lenders get paid first; equity holders own only what's left. "What dominates the output?" WACC and terminal growth — which is precisely why you sensitize them rather than defend a point estimate. Saying that last part signals you've actually built one.

Where each method breaks — and why analysts triangulate

Every valuation tool has a failure mode, and knowing them is worth more than knowing the formulas. DCF breaks for pre-revenue startups: with negative cash flows for years, essentially 100% of the value sits in a terminal value built on guesses stacked on guesses — the model becomes a random-number generator wearing a suit. Venture investors use staged expected-value thinking and comparables instead. P/E breaks for cyclicals: an automaker looks cheapest (low P/E) at the peak of the cycle, exactly when earnings are about to collapse — the multiple is low because the E is temporarily inflated. Analysts use normalized, mid-cycle earnings instead. Book value breaks for asset-light companies: a software firm's engineers, code, and brand barely appear on its balance sheet (as you saw in Section 11), so price-to-book is nearly meaningless for them.

Banks get their own playbook. For a bank, debt isn't financing — it's raw material (deposits in, loans out), so "enterprise value" and EV/EBITDA stop making sense. Analysts value banks on equity-side measures: P/E, price-to-book (book value actually means something when assets are financial and marked close to reality), and dividend-based models, all disciplined by regulatory capital requirements from Section 6.

Hence the professional habit you saw in the football field: triangulate. Run a DCF for the fundamental logic, trading comps for market context, precedents if a sale is plausible — then interrogate the disagreements. If the DCF says $42 and comps say $30, either your assumptions are heroic or the market is mispricing the sector; both are worth knowing, and the gap itself is the insight. Many investors also demand a margin of safety — acting only when price sits far below the whole range — precisely because they know their estimate has error bars.

Deep dive: two refinements you'll meet in real models

Exit multiples. Instead of Gordon growth, many bankers set the terminal value as a multiple of terminal-year EBITDA — e.g. TV = 10 × EBITDA5 — borrowing today's comps for the far future. It feels more concrete but smuggles relative valuation into your intrinsic model; best practice computes TV both ways and checks that each implies a sane version of the other (a Gordon TV implies an exit multiple, and vice versa).

Mid-year convention. Our model discounts each year's FCF as if it all arrives on December 31, but cash actually dribbles in through the year. Discounting at t = 0.5, 1.5, 2.5… (dividing by (1+WACC)t−0.5) treats flows as arriving mid-year and raises the valuation by roughly half a year of discounting — about 4–5% at a 10% WACC. Small, but interviewers love asking about it.

Check your understanding

1. Your DCF gives an enterprise value of $1,775M. The company has $300M of debt, $100M of cash, and 50M shares. What is the per-share value?

EV belongs to debt and equity holders together. Subtract net debt (300 − 100 = 200) to get equity value of $1,575M, then divide by 50M shares: $31.50. Ignoring debt overstates the answer; ignoring the cash understates it.

2. In a typical 5-year DCF, roughly what share of enterprise value comes from the terminal value?

Terminal value usually carries 60–80% of EV (72% in our Cascade model). That is why WACC and terminal growth dominate the output — and why honest analysts always present a sensitivity grid.

3. You're comping five peers whose debt loads range from zero to heavy. Which multiple keeps the comparison fair?

P/E mixes an equity price with post-interest earnings, so leverage distorts it differently for each peer. EV/EBITDA compares whole-business value to pre-interest profit, neutralizing capital-structure differences.

4. WACC is 8% and terminal growth is 3%. An analyst nudges terminal growth to 4%. What happens to the terminal value?

TV = FCF×(1+g)/(WACC−g). The denominator drops from 0.05 to 0.04 (a 25% boost) and the numerator ticks up too — about a 26% jump in total from a one-point tweak. The WACC−g spread is the most explosive input in the model.

5. Why does a DCF fail for a pre-revenue startup?

The mechanics still run — you can always discount numbers — but with negative explicit-year flows, the answer is nearly 100% terminal value built on guesses. The model outputs false precision rather than insight; comparables and staged expected-value approaches work better.

Key takeaways

  • Price is a fact; intrinsic value is an estimate with error bars. A valuation model organizes your assumptions so disagreement with the market becomes legible.
  • DCF in five moves: project FCF for 5–10 years → terminal value via TV = FCF×(1+g)/(WACC−g) → discount everything at WACC → EV − net debt = equity value → ÷ shares. Cascade: $500M + $1,275M = $1,775M EV → $31.50/share.
  • WACC blends the CAPM cost of equity (rf + β×ERP) with the after-tax cost of debt, weighted at market values. Riskier cash flows get higher rates because a dollar you might not receive is worth less.
  • Terminal value is typically 60–80% of EV, and ±1pp on WACC or g swings the answer by double digits — present sensitivity grids and football fields, not point estimates.
  • Comps borrow the market's assumptions: pick honest peers, use median multiples, EV/EBITDA when leverage differs, P/E when it doesn't. Precedent transactions run 20–40% higher because of the control premium.
  • Every method has a failure mode — DCF for pre-revenue startups, P/E for cyclicals, book value for asset-light firms — and banks get equity-side multiples because their debt is raw material. So professionals triangulate.
  • You can now answer "walk me through a DCF" verbatim — and the follow-ups about net debt and sensitivity that prove you mean it.

Part III · Corporate Finance & Accounting

14. Corporate Finance: Decisions Inside the Firm

So far you have watched companies from the outside — reading their statements, computing their ratios, estimating their value. This section walks you through the door: how the people running the firm decide what to build, how to pay for it, and what to hand back to shareholders, using the exact toolkit you have already assembled.

The CFO's three questions

Strip away the org charts and the earnings calls, and a CFO's job reduces to three questions about every dollar the firm touches:

  • What should we invest in? This is capital budgeting — build the factory, fund the R&D, buy the rival, or do nothing.
  • How should we fund it? This is capital structure — borrow money, issue shares, or use retained profits.
  • What should we give back? This is payout policy — dividends, buybacks, or reinvest everything.

Every corporate headline you will ever read maps to one of the three. "Apple announces $110B buyback" — payout. "Microsoft acquires Activision" — capital budgeting (an acquisition is just a very large project). "Tesla raises $5B in new shares" — capital structure. Once you have this map, financial news stops being noise and becomes a stream of labeled training data.

Key idea

This section is where the course converges. Section 1 gave you discounting, Section 11 gave you the statements, Section 12 gave you ROE and ROIC, and Section 13 gave you valuation. Corporate finance is those four tools pointed inward: the CFO runs the same math on the firm's own projects that an analyst runs on the firm's stock.

Capital budgeting I: the NPV rule

Suppose your firm can spend $1,000 today on a project that returns cash over five years. Should it? The gold-standard answer is net present value (NPV): discount every future cash flow back to today at the appropriate rate, add them up (including the negative outlay), and accept the project if the total is positive.

NPV = Σt=0n CFt ÷ (1 + r)t
CFt = cash flow in year t (CF0 is usually negative) · r = discount rate · accept if NPV > 0

Nothing here is new. This is literally the time-value machinery from Section 1 applied to a project instead of a bank deposit, and it is the same present-value engine that powered the DCF in Section 13. A firm is a portfolio of projects; a DCF values the whole portfolio, capital budgeting values one candidate at a time.

Worked example: two rival projects

Project A ("Steady") costs $1,000 and pays $300 a year for five years. Project B ("Moonshot") costs $1,000, pays nothing for four years, then $2,000 in year 5. At a 10% discount rate:

NPVA = −1,000 + 300×(1 − 1.10−5)÷0.10 = −1,000 + 300×3.7908 = +$137
NPVB = −1,000 + 2,000÷1.105 = −1,000 + 2,000×0.6209 = +$242

Both add value, but if they are mutually exclusive, take B: it adds $105 more in today's dollars. Hold that thought — a popular rival metric is about to disagree.

A useful picture is the NPV profile: plot NPV against the discount rate. At r = 0 the curve starts at the raw sum of cash flows; as r rises, distant cash gets crushed and the curve falls; where it crosses zero is a rate with a famous name you'll meet next.

Your edge

NPV is a fold: cashflows.reduce((s, cf, t) => s + cf / (1 + r) ** t, 0). One line of code, and it is the single most consequential formula in corporate life. When you build financial models, most of the complexity is estimating the inputs (the cash flows), not the math.

Capital budgeting II: IRR, payback, and their traps

In practice, executives quote a project's IRR (internal rate of return) more often than its NPV. IRR is the discount rate that makes NPV exactly zero — the project's implied annualized return. The decision rule: accept if IRR exceeds your hurdle rate. It is intuitive ("this project yields 15%") and unit-free, which is exactly why people love it — and exactly where it bites:

  • Scale-blindness. A $1 project returning $2 next year has a 100% IRR and adds about $0.82 of value at r = 10%. A $1,000 project returning $1,300 has a mere 30% IRR and adds $182. IRR picks the first; your bank account prefers the second. Percentages don't pay salaries — dollars do.
  • Multiple IRRs. If cash flows change sign more than once (invest, earn, then pay cleanup costs), the NPV polynomial can have several roots. The flows −100, +230, −132 have IRRs at both 10% and 20% — the number "the" IRR is simply undefined. (Check: at 15%, NPV = −100 + 230/1.15 − 132/1.15² = +0.19, positive between the two roots.)
  • The reinvestment assumption. IRR implicitly assumes every interim cash flow gets reinvested at the IRR itself. A 40% IRR project assumes you can redeploy each payout at 40% — usually fantasy. NPV assumes reinvestment at the discount rate, which is honest.

The third tool you will meet in the wild is the payback period: how long until cumulative cash flows cover the outlay. Project A pays back in 1,000 ÷ 300 ≈ 3.3 years; Project B not until year 5. Payback is a fine quick-and-dirty liquidity check, but it ignores the time value of money and everything after the payback date — by payback logic, a project that returns your money in year 3 and then explodes beats one that pays a fortune in year 6.

Now the main event. Below, Projects A and B from the worked example face off across every discount rate at once. B has the bigger prize but makes you wait; A drip-feeds cash early. B's curve therefore starts higher but falls faster — and the curves cross. Note the paradox: A has the higher IRR (15.2% vs 14.9%), yet at any rate below ~14.4%, B has the higher NPV. IRR and NPV genuinely disagree, and when they do, trust NPV: it measures dollars of value created at your actual cost of capital.

Project duel — NPV vs IRR

Drag the discount rate. Watch the verdict flip as you cross ~14.4% (rankings swap) and ~15.2% (both go negative) — and notice IRR would have picked A the whole time.

NPV A · Steady (IRR 15.2%)—
NPV B · Moonshot (IRR 14.9%)—
Verdict—

—

Common pitfall

Private equity marketing decks lean hard on IRR because early cash returns inflate it. A fund that returns your money fast at a modest multiple can post a dazzling IRR while creating less wealth than a slower fund with a bigger multiple. Always ask for the dollar multiple alongside the percentage.

The hurdle rate: WACC and the value-creation test

NPV needs a discount rate — but which one? The firm's money comes from two groups with claims on it: lenders and shareholders. Each demands a return: the cost of debt is observable (it is roughly the yield on the firm's bonds, from Section 8), while the cost of equity is the return shareholders require given the stock's risk. Blend them by their share of the capital pie and you get the WACC — the weighted average cost of capital.

WACC = (E ÷ V) × rE + (D ÷ V) × rD × (1 − t)
E, D = market value of equity, debt · V = E + D · rE, rD = cost of equity, debt · t = tax rate (interest is tax-deductible)

Worked: a firm financed 70% with equity costing 9% and 30% with debt costing 5% pre-tax, at a 21% tax rate. After-tax debt costs 5% × 0.79 = 3.95%, so WACC = 0.70×9% + 0.30×3.95% = 6.3% + 1.185% ≈ 7.5%. That 7.5% is not an accounting cost — it is an opportunity cost. Investors could earn it elsewhere at similar risk, so any project earning less makes them poorer than the alternative.

This closes the loop you started in Section 12. There you learned to compute ROIC, the return a business earns on the capital tied up inside it. Now you have the benchmark to judge it:

Key idea: the value-creation inequality

A firm creates value if and only if ROIC > WACC. Earn 15% on capital that costs 7.5% and every reinvested dollar is worth more than a dollar; earn 6% and growth actively destroys value — the firm is a machine for converting good money into bad projects. Revenue can grow, EPS can grow, the CEO can ring the opening bell, and value still burns. This single inequality explains most of what separates great compounders from value traps in Section 13-style valuations.

In practice firms set a hurdle rate at or slightly above WACC and require project sponsors to clear it. The quiet failure mode: divisions with risky projects using the company-wide WACC, understating their true cost of capital — a subsidy from the boring divisions to the exciting ones.

Capital structure: the debt–equity trade-off

Now the funding question. Debt is cheaper than equity — lenders take less risk (they get paid first) and interest is tax-deductible. So why not fund everything with debt? Because debt is a hard promise, and hard promises turn bad years into fatal ones. The trade-off has two arms.

The prize: the tax shield. Interest is deductible, dividends are not. Borrow $100M at 6% and the $6M interest bill cuts taxable income by $6M, saving $6M × 21% = $1.26M in tax every year — a tax shield. If the debt is permanent, that stream, discounted at 6%, is worth 1.26 ÷ 0.06 = $21M, i.e. tax rate × debt. Real money, courtesy of the tax code.

The price: financial distress. Pile on debt and the probability of missing a payment rises. Financial distress costs are not just bankruptcy lawyers — customers abandon a company that might not honor warranties, suppliers demand cash upfront, star employees leave, and management cancels good projects to hoard cash. Value leaks long before any default.

Between prize and price sits leverage's defining property: it amplifies. Take a firm with $100M of assets. Unlevered, a 12% asset return means 12% ROE. Now fund it with $50M equity + $50M debt at 5% (interest $2.5M, taxes ignored for clarity):

Scenario Asset return Profit after interest ROE (on $50M equity)
Good year +12% → $12.0M $12.0M − $2.5M = $9.5M +19.0%
Bad year −4% → −$4.0M −$4.0M − $2.5M = −$6.5M −13.0%

Same business, same swings in asset returns — but the equity holder's ride goes from (12%, −4%) to (19%, −13%). Leverage is a gain knob on the whole distribution, exactly the DuPont mechanism from Section 12 seen from the driver's seat. The general formula, with debt costing rD and leverage L = assets ÷ equity: ROE = L × ROA − (L − 1) × rD. Check: 2 × 12% − 1 × 5% = 19%. ✓

Leverage amplifier

Set the asset return and the leverage ratio (debt costs 5%). Watch what leverage does to a bad year.

ROE unlevered (1×)—
ROE at your leverage—

—

One paragraph of theory to frame it all: in 1958, Modigliani and Miller proved that in a perfectly frictionless world — no taxes, no distress costs, no information asymmetries — capital structure is irrelevant: slicing the pizza into different pieces doesn't change how much pizza there is. That sounds like a useless result, but it is the opposite: it tells you that capital structure matters only through the frictions. Taxes, distress, and signaling are not footnotes to the theory — they are the whole game. It is the finance equivalent of a no-free-lunch theorem: it tells you exactly where to look for the lunch.

Payout policy: dividends vs buybacks

When a firm generates more cash than its positive-NPV projects can absorb, holding it is itself a capital-budgeting decision — one that usually earns money-market returns while shareholders' opportunity cost is the WACC. Time for question three: return it. There are two pipes.

A dividend is a direct cash payment per share; once established, markets treat it as a quasi-promise, which is why cuts are rare and brutal for the stock. A buyback (share repurchase) uses the cash to buy the firm's own shares in the market, shrinking the share count so each remaining share owns a bigger slice. Mechanically the two are close cousins; practically they differ in three ways:

  • Taxes. Dividends force a taxable event on every shareholder every quarter. Buybacks tax only those who choose to sell, and only on their gains — everyone else compounds untaxed. This is a big reason US payout has shifted heavily toward buybacks since the 1980s (SEC rule 10b-18 made them safe from manipulation charges in 1982; a 1% excise tax was added in 2023).
  • Flexibility and signaling. A dividend hike says "we are confident this cash level is permanent." A buyback authorization is a maybe — it can be quietly abandoned. Markets price the difference.
  • Price sensitivity. A dividend's value doesn't depend on the stock price. A buyback's does — completely.

Worked example: when buybacks create or destroy value

A firm has 100M shares trading at $50, and $500M of cash it will spend buying back 10M shares. Suppose your analysis (Section 13) says intrinsic value is $70/share — total intrinsic equity $7,000M. After the buyback, remaining intrinsic value is $7,000M − $500M cash out = $6,500M across 90M shares = $72.22/share. Continuing holders gained $2.22 of value per share, transferred from sellers who accepted $50 for $70 of value.

Now flip it: intrinsic value is only $40 (the stock is overpriced at $50). Post-buyback: ($4,000M − $500M) ÷ 90M = $38.89. Management just paid $50 for $40 bills, and continuing holders footed the difference. A buyback is simply an investment in your own stock — the NPV rule applies to it like everything else. The empirical embarrassment: corporate America's buyback volume peaks near market tops and dries up in crashes, i.e. firms systematically buy high. Funding buybacks with debt at cycle peaks compounds the error with leverage.

Neither pipe creates value by itself — moving cash from the company's pocket to yours is plumbing, not alchemy. Value was created earlier, by ROIC > WACC projects. Payout policy decides who holds the cash and what tax bill attaches; the best payout policy is the honest one: return cash when internal projects can't beat the hurdle, retain it when they can.

M&A: buying growth, and why it usually disappoints

M&A is capital budgeting at maximum stakes: instead of building a project, you buy someone else's — typically paying a 20–40% control premium over the market price. Two species of buyer roam this market. A strategic buyer is a company buying a company — it can justify a higher price because it expects synergies: shared costs, cross-sold products, a merged data platform. A financial buyer — typically a private equity fund running a leveraged buyout — buys with borrowed money, applies the leverage amplifier you just played with, and aims to resell in five to seven years.

Synergies deserve your skepticism. The premium is paid on day one, in cash or shares, with certainty; the synergies arrive later, if integration goes well, if key staff stay, if customers don't flee, if the cultures mesh. Study after study finds most deals transfer value to the target's shareholders (who pocket the premium) rather than create it for the acquirer's. And when several bidders compete, the one who wins is the one who overestimated the target most — the winner's curse, straight out of auction theory.

Wall Street's quick screen for a stock deal is accretion/dilution: does the acquirer's earnings per share rise or fall? Here is the arithmetic for an all-stock deal:

Line BigCo (acquirer) SmallCo (target) Combined
Net income $1,000M $200M $1,200M
Shares outstanding 500M — 500M + 90M new = 590M
Share price / deal price $40 (P/E 20) $3,600M (P/E 18) —
EPS $2.00 — $1,200M ÷ 590M = $2.03

BigCo pays $3,600M in its own stock (3,600 ÷ 40 = 90M new shares) and EPS rises 1.7% — accretive. But look at why: BigCo's shares are "worth" 20× earnings while it paid only 18× for SmallCo's. Whenever you buy earnings at a lower P/E than your own using stock, EPS mechanically rises — even if the deal is a disaster. Accretion is arithmetic, not value creation; the NPV question (are the synergies worth more than the premium?) is the only one that matters.

Real world: one funeral, one wedding

AOL–Time Warner (2000): the largest merger ever announced (~$165B, all stock), sold on internet–media synergy at the very peak of the dot-com bubble. AOL paid with wildly overvalued shares, cultures clashed, dial-up collapsed, and in 2002 the company recorded a ~$99B write-down — for years the largest annual corporate loss in US history. Every failure mode in this section in a single deal: peak-cycle timing, phantom synergies, winner's optimism.

Disney–Pixar (2006): $7.4B in stock for a studio Disney distributed but couldn't replicate. The synergy was real and specific — Pixar's creative engine plugged into Disney's merchandising, parks, and distribution machine — and Disney protected it by leaving Pixar's leadership in charge of animation. Frozen, Toy Story 3, and a revived Walt Disney Animation followed; the price looks almost quaint in hindsight. Good deals tend to share this shape: a specific, verifiable synergy and a plan to protect what you bought.

Deep dive: Modigliani–Miller's two propositions, slightly less compressed

Proposition I (the pizza theorem): with frictionless markets, firm value is set by the cash flows the assets generate, not by how claims on them are sliced. The arbitrage proof is elegant — if a levered firm traded at a premium to an identical unlevered one, you could manufacture "homemade leverage" by borrowing personally to buy the cheap unlevered shares, replicating the expensive firm's payoff and pocketing the difference.

Proposition II: the cost of equity rises linearly with leverage: rE = r0 + (D÷E) × (r0 − rD), where r0 is the unlevered cost of capital. Swapping "expensive" equity for "cheap" debt makes the remaining equity riskier and thus costlier, and in a frictionless world the WACC stays exactly flat. So when a CFO claims a debt raise "lowers our cost of capital," the honest version is: only via the tax shield, and only until distress costs eat it. The hump in Figure 14.3 is the empirical world's amendment to MM.

Check your understanding

1. Two mutually exclusive projects: X has IRR 22% and NPV +$3M at the firm's 9% WACC; Y has IRR 15% and NPV +$11M. Which should the firm take?

When rankings conflict, NPV wins: it counts dollars added at the true opportunity cost of capital. IRR is scale-blind and assumes reinvestment at the IRR itself. "Both" fails because the projects are mutually exclusive.

2. A firm's WACC is 8%. It reinvests heavily in projects returning 6%, and its EPS grows every year. What is happening to shareholder value?

ROIC (6%) is below WACC (8%), so each dollar retained turns into less than a dollar of value. Growth amplifies value destruction when returns are below the cost of capital — EPS growth notwithstanding.

3. A firm runs at 3× leverage (assets ÷ equity) with debt costing 5%. Its assets return −2% this year. Roughly what is its ROE?

ROE = L×ROA − (L−1)×rD = 3×(−2%) − 2×5% = −6% − 10% = −16%. The interest on two turns of debt is owed regardless of how the assets performed — that is the downside of the amplifier.

4. Management buys back stock at $80 when a careful intrinsic-value estimate says shares are worth $60. Who wins?

A buyback is an investment in the firm's own stock, so the NPV rule applies: paying $80 for $60 of value transfers the $20 difference from continuing holders to sellers. Higher EPS and a bigger ownership slice don't offset overpaying.

5. An all-stock acquisition is immediately EPS-accretive because the acquirer trades at P/E 24 and paid P/E 16 for the target. What does the accretion prove about value creation?

Swapping high-multiple paper for low-multiple earnings raises EPS by pure arithmetic, even for a value-destroying deal. Whether value is created depends on whether synergies exceed the control premium — a separate, NPV question.

Key takeaways

  • Corporate finance is three questions: what to invest in (capital budgeting), how to fund it (capital structure), and what to return (payout policy). Every corporate headline maps to one.
  • NPV — Section 1's discounting applied to projects — is the gold standard: accept positive-NPV projects, and when rankings conflict, trust dollars over percentages.
  • IRR is scale-blind, can have multiple roots when cash flows change sign twice, and assumes reinvestment at itself; payback ignores time value and everything after the payback date.
  • WACC is the blended opportunity cost of the firm's capital; value is created if and only if ROIC > WACC — growth below the hurdle destroys value even while EPS rises.
  • Debt buys a tax shield but sells insurance against bad years: leverage multiplies ROE in both directions (2× leverage turns +12%/−4% asset returns into +19%/−13% on equity).
  • Modigliani–Miller says capital structure is irrelevant without frictions — so taxes, distress costs, and signaling are precisely where structure matters.
  • Dividends and buybacks are plumbing, not alchemy: buybacks add value only below intrinsic value, and destroy it when firms buy high — which, empirically, is when they buy most.
  • M&A premiums are certain, synergies are hypotheses; accretion is arithmetic, not proof of value; and in competitive auctions, beware the winner's curse.

Part IV · Investing & Risk

15. Portfolio Theory: Diversification, CAPM and Factors

Individual assets are noisy; portfolios are engineered systems whose risk you can shape with covariance math. This section gives you the core machinery — diversification, the efficient frontier, CAPM, and factor models — and it is the most "data science in disguise" material in the whole book.

Measuring risk and return: mean and standard deviation

Portfolio theory starts by compressing an asset's future into two numbers. The expected return E[r] is the mean of the return distribution — what you'd earn on average across many repetitions. Volatility (σ) is the standard deviation of returns — how widely outcomes scatter around that mean. If a stock has E[r] = 8% and σ = 18%, a typical year lands somewhere in the −10% to +26% band, and roughly one year in three lands outside it.

Convention: both numbers are annualized so everything is comparable. Means scale linearly with time, but standard deviations scale with the square root of time, because variances — not standard deviations — add across independent periods. A stock with 1.2% daily volatility has annualized volatility of about 1.2% × √252 ≈ 19% (252 is the number of US trading days in a year). You already know this rule: it's the same √n that governs the standard error of a sample mean.

σannual = σdaily × √252  ·  E[r]annual ≈ E[r]daily × 252
variance adds over independent periods, so σ grows with √time — the same √n as a standard error

Why does everyone use standard deviation rather than, say, drawdowns or tail quantiles? Convenience. If returns were normally distributed, mean and σ would describe the whole distribution, and everything in this section becomes clean linear algebra. That assumption is good enough to build powerful tools — and quantifiably wrong in the tails.

Common pitfall: the normal assumption

Real markets have fat tails. Under a normal distribution with σ = 1% daily, a −7% day is a 7-sigma event that should happen roughly never in the life of the universe; the S&P 500 has had many. Treat everything in this section as a first-order model that works well in the middle of the distribution. Section 16 (Risk Management) deals honestly with the tails.

Diversification: the only free lunch

Here is the one result in finance that gives you something for nothing. Combine two assets and the portfolio's expected return is just the weighted average — no surprise. But the portfolio's risk is generally less than the weighted average of the two risks, and the discount is controlled entirely by correlation (ρ). For a two-asset portfolio with weight w in asset A:

σp2 = w2σA2 + (1−w)2σB2 + 2w(1−w)ρσAσB
σp = portfolio volatility · w = weight in A · ρ = correlation between A and B · the last term is 2w(1−w) × covariance(A, B)

Read the three terms as a variance decomposition: A's own variance, B's own variance, and a cross term that ρ can shrink or even flip negative. At ρ = +1 the formula collapses to (wσA + (1−w)σB)2 — a perfect square, risk is the plain weighted average, diversification does nothing. As ρ falls, the cross term shrinks and portfolio risk drops while expected return is unchanged. At ρ = −1 you can pick w = σB ÷ (σA + σB) and drive risk to exactly zero.

Worked example

Stocks: E[r] = 8%, σ = 18%. Bonds: E[r] = 3.5%, σ = 6%. Correlation ρ = 0.1. Put 60% in stocks. Expected return: 0.6 × 8 + 0.4 × 3.5 = 6.2%. Variance: 0.36 × 324 + 0.16 × 36 + 2 × 0.6 × 0.4 × 0.1 × 18 × 6 = 116.64 + 5.76 + 5.18 = 127.58 (in %²), so σp = 11.3%. The naive weighted average of volatilities is 0.6 × 18 + 0.4 × 6 = 13.2% — correlation just handed you 1.9 points of risk reduction for free.

Analogy: model ensembling

This is exactly why ensembles beat single models. Average k models with uncorrelated errors and the error variance drops by 1/k; if the errors are correlated, the benefit caps out at the shared component. Diversification is ensembling for money: idiosyncratic noise averages away, the correlated part survives.

That surviving part has a name. Systematic risk is the market-wide component: recessions, rate shocks, pandemics hit everything at once. Idiosyncratic risk is company-specific: a botched product launch, an accounting scandal. Hold N stocks in equal weights and the portfolio variance tends toward σ²(1/N + (1 − 1/N)ρ̄), where ρ̄ is the average pairwise correlation. The 1/N term — the idiosyncratic part — dies fast; the ρ̄σ² floor never moves.

That decay curve is the empirical answer to "how many stocks do I need?": 20–30 well-spread holdings capture most of the diversification benefit, which is why concentrated single-stock positions (say, all your net worth in your employer's equity) carry risk the market never pays you for. That last clause is the punchline of this whole section, and CAPM will make it precise.

The efficient frontier

Now flip from analyzing one mix to choosing among all of them. Sweep the weight w from 0 to 1 (and consider every combination when there are many assets) and plot each portfolio as a point in risk–return space: σ on the x-axis, E[r] on the y-axis. The cloud of achievable portfolios is the feasible set; with many assets it looks like a bullet lying on its side.

Two landmarks matter. The leftmost tip of the bullet is the minimum-variance portfolio — the lowest-risk mix that exists. And the upper edge of the bullet is the efficient frontier: for every level of risk, the portfolio with the highest expected return. Any point inside the bullet is dominated — some frontier portfolio offers more return at the same risk, or the same return at less risk. A rational mean-variance investor therefore lives on the frontier; the only remaining question is where on it, which is a matter of risk appetite.

A result that surprises everyone the first time: with the stock/bond numbers above, the minimum-variance mix is about 7% stocks, 93% bonds, with σ ≈ 5.8% — less than the 6% volatility of bonds alone. Adding a riskier asset lowered total risk, because low correlation lets stock wiggles partially offset bond wiggles. Risk is a property of the portfolio, not a sum over its parts. This is Harry Markowitz's 1952 insight, and it won a Nobel Prize. Play with it yourself:

Diversification lab: two assets, one frontier

Drag the correlation toward −1 and watch the frontier bend left into a hook — at ρ = −1 a 25/75 mix has zero risk. Then sweep your weight along the curve and watch the Sharpe ratio.

Portfolio E[r]—
Portfolio σ—
Sharpe ratio (rf = 2%)—
Min-variance mix—

Adding a risk-free asset: the capital market line

The frontier gets a dramatic upgrade the moment you add a risk-free asset — think 3-month Treasury bills, whose return rf is known in advance and whose volatility is essentially zero. Any blend of the risk-free asset with a risky portfolio P plots on a straight line from (0, rf) through P, because mixing with a zero-variance asset scales both the excess return and the risk linearly.

So which risky portfolio should you draw your line through? The one giving the steepest line — the line that just grazes the frontier. Its point of tangency is the tangency portfolio, and the line itself is the capital market line (CML). The slope of any such line is the portfolio's Sharpe ratio:

Sharpe = E[rp] − rfσp
excess return per unit of risk — the slope of the line from rf through your portfolio; the tangency portfolio maximizes it

The punchline is called two-fund separation: every mean-variance investor should hold the same risky portfolio — the tangency portfolio — and adjust risk only by moving along the line. Conservative? Put 40% in the tangency portfolio, 60% in T-bills. Aggressive? Borrow at (near) the risk-free rate and put 130% in — that's leverage, and it extends the CML past the tangency point. Risk appetite changes your position on the line, never the risky mix itself. In the widget above, the dashed line through your mix is exactly this line — maximizing its slope is the whole game.

CAPM: beta, the security market line, and priced risk

Two-fund separation has a radical implication. If everyone holds the same tangency portfolio, then in equilibrium that portfolio must be the whole market — every asset held in proportion to its market value. The CAPM (Capital Asset Pricing Model, Sharpe 1964) follows: the only risk the market pays you for is an asset's contribution to market risk, measured by beta (β).

Your edge: beta is just OLS

β is literally the slope coefficient from regressing the asset's excess returns on the market's excess returns: β = Cov(ri, rm) ÷ Var(rm). Concretely: if Cov(ri, rm) = 0.000486 on daily data and Var(rm) = 0.000324, then β = 1.5 — the stock moves 1.5% for a 1% market move, on average. The regression's R² tells you how much of the stock's variance is systematic; the residual variance is the idiosyncratic part that diversification kills. And the intercept has a famous name: alpha. When a fund manager claims alpha, they are claiming a positive regression intercept — always ask for the standard error.

E[ri] = rf + βi × ERP
ERP = equity risk premium = E[rm] − rf · βi = Cov(ri, rm) ÷ Var(rm)

Worked example: with rf = 4%, ERP = 5% and β = 1.2, the model says E[r] = 4 + 1.2 × 5 = 10%. Plotting E[r] against β gives the security market line (SML). An asset priced to return more than the line demands is undervalued (positive alpha — buyers should bid its price up); one below the line is overvalued. Note the distinction from the CML: the CML lives in σ-space and holds only for efficient portfolios; the SML lives in β-space and, under CAPM, holds for every asset — because only the systematic slice of risk is priced. Volatility you can diversify away earns you nothing.

How does CAPM hold up empirically? Its core qualitative claim — diversifiable risk is not rewarded, co-movement with the market is what's priced — has aged well. Its quantitative claims have not: measured over decades, the relationship between beta and average return is flatter than the model predicts (low-beta stocks do too well, high-beta stocks too poorly), and beta alone leaves large, persistent return differences unexplained. Yet CAPM remains everywhere in practice because it is simple and disciplined: it is the standard engine for the cost of equity inside Section 13's DCF and WACC machinery, and beta is still the default first coordinate of any risk model.

Factors: return sources beyond beta

Those "large, persistent return differences" got names. A factor is a characteristic that sorts assets into groups with reliably different average returns. Researchers build them the way you build features: compute a signal per stock, rank the universe, go long the top slice and short the bottom, and measure the spread. The canonical set:

Factor Signal (the "feature") Classic finding
Value Price relative to fundamentals (low P/B, P/E — see Section 12) Cheap stocks have outperformed expensive ones long-run
Size Market capitalization Small caps have outperformed large caps (weakest premium)
Momentum Trailing ~12-month return, skipping the last month Recent winners keep winning for months
Quality Profitability, earnings stability, low leverage Profitable, stable firms outperform junk
Low volatility Trailing volatility or beta Boring low-vol stocks beat what CAPM predicts

Fama and French formalized the first extensions in 1992–93; their three-factor model (market, size, value) plus momentum and later profitability became the standard benchmark for judging any fund. The modern regression is your CAPM regression with more columns: ri − rf = α + βmkt·MKT + βsize·SMB + βval·HML + … + ε. Whatever alpha survives that regression is much rarer than CAPM alpha — most "stock-picking skill" turns out to be repackaged factor exposure.

Factor investing — marketed as smart beta — packages these tilts into cheap, rules-based funds. For a data person this is home turf, so bring your ML instincts, especially the paranoid ones. A factor is a feature engineered on a finite, autocorrelated sample; the field has published hundreds ("the factor zoo"), and most fail out-of-sample — textbook overfitting via multiple hypothesis testing. The half-dozen above survive because they replicate across countries, decades and asset classes and have plausible economic mechanisms: either compensation for real risk (value stocks crash hardest in recessions) or persistent behavioral patterns (Section 17) that are costly to arbitrage away. Section 18 shows you how factor research is actually run — and how backtests lie.

Passive vs active: Sharpe's brutal arithmetic

Active management tries to beat the market; passive investing buys the whole market via an index fund and accepts the market return. In 1991 William Sharpe pointed out that the contest is settled by arithmetic, not opinion. Every share is held by someone, so passive investors and active investors together hold the market portfolio. Passive investors earn the market return by construction. Therefore active investors, in aggregate, also earn exactly the market return — before costs. After their higher fees and trading costs, the average actively managed dollar must underperform the average passive dollar. No model, no assumptions about efficiency — just accounting.

The data cooperates. S&P's SPIVA scorecards (spglobal.com/spiva) find that roughly 90% of US large-cap funds underperformed the S&P 500 over the trailing 15 years, with similar figures across most categories and countries. Skilled managers exist, but identifying them in advance is the hard problem: past outperformance barely persists, and much of what looks like alpha is factor exposure you could buy for a few basis points.

Worked example: what a 1% fee really costs

Invest $100,000 for 30 years. At 7% you end with 100,000 × 1.0730 ≈ $761,000. Pay a 1% annual expense ratio and compound at 6% instead: ≈ $574,000. The fee looked like 1% but consumed 25% of your final wealth, because — as Section 1 warned — costs compound exactly like returns. The fee-drag widget in Section 19 lets you feel this interactively.

Real world

This arithmetic reshaped an industry: index funds and passive ETFs went from a curiosity in 1976 (Vanguard's first index fund was mocked as "Bogle's folly") to holding roughly half of US fund assets today, and fee competition has pushed broad-market index funds below 0.05% per year. For most portfolios, the highest-confidence "alpha" available is simply refusing to pay unnecessary fees.

Deep dive: the n-asset case is one line of linear algebra

Everything above generalizes instantly. Stack the weights into a vector w and the pairwise covariances into the covariance matrix Σ: portfolio variance is σp2 = wTΣw, and expected return is wTμ. The efficient frontier is the solution set of "minimize wTΣw subject to wTμ = target" — a quadratic program with a closed form when short sales are allowed.

The catch is estimation, not optimization. For n = 500 assets, Σ has n(n+1)/2 = 125,250 free parameters, estimated from noisy, non-stationary returns — and the optimizer greedily exploits every estimation error, loading up on whatever spurious correlations your sample contains ("error maximization"). Practitioners fight back with regularization you already know: shrinkage estimators (Ledoit–Wolf pulls the sample covariance toward a structured target), factor models that reduce Σ to a low-rank-plus-diagonal form, and constraints on weights. Naive mean-variance optimization on raw sample estimates is the finance equivalent of fitting a 125,000-parameter model without regularization and shipping it to production.

Check your understanding

1. You hold a 50/50 mix of two assets and their correlation falls from +0.8 to +0.1 (volatilities unchanged). What happens?

Expected return is a weighted average of the assets' returns, so correlation doesn't touch it. But ρ multiplies the cross term 2w(1−w)ρσAσB in the variance, so lower ρ means lower portfolio risk at the same return — the free lunch.

2. Which kind of risk does holding 30 stocks instead of 1 essentially eliminate?

Company-specific surprises average out across ~20–30 holdings (the 1/N term in portfolio variance). Market-wide shocks hit every holding at once — that systematic floor is why the decay curve in Figure 15.1 flattens near 15% instead of falling to zero.

3. Risk-free rate 3%, equity risk premium 6%, stock beta 1.5. What expected return does CAPM assign?

E[r] = rf + β × ERP = 3% + 1.5 × 6% = 12%. Note that only systematic exposure (β) enters — the stock's total volatility is irrelevant under CAPM.

4. Portfolio A: E[r] = 9%, σ = 20%. Portfolio B: E[r] = 6%, σ = 8%. Risk-free rate 2%. Which has the better risk-adjusted performance, and why can a leveraged investor prefer it even while wanting more risk?

Sharpe(A) = (9−2)/20 = 0.35; Sharpe(B) = (6−2)/8 = 0.50. Borrowing to hold 2.5× portfolio B gives σ = 20% with E[r] = 2% + 2.5 × 4% = 12% — same risk as A, three points more return. The slope of the line is what matters, not the point you start at.

5. Why must the average actively managed dollar underperform the average passively managed dollar after costs?

This is Sharpe's arithmetic: it needs no assumption about market efficiency or skill. Aggregate active holdings equal the market minus passive holdings, so gross returns match; net returns then differ only by costs, which are higher for active.

Key takeaways

  • Risk and return are summarized as mean and standard deviation of returns, annualized with the √time rule — a normal-distribution convenience that understates tail risk (Section 16).
  • Diversification is covariance math: portfolio variance is w²σA² + (1−w)²σB² + 2w(1−w)ρσAσB, so lower correlation cuts risk at zero cost in expected return.
  • Roughly 20–30 holdings eliminate most idiosyncratic risk; the systematic (market) component is a floor nothing diversifies away.
  • Rational investors hold portfolios on the efficient frontier; adding a risk-free asset collapses the choice to one tangency portfolio plus a lending/borrowing dial along the capital market line, whose slope is the Sharpe ratio.
  • CAPM prices only systematic risk: E[r] = rf + β × ERP, with β the OLS slope against the market and alpha the regression intercept. It powers cost-of-equity estimates (Section 13) even though the empirical beta-return line is flatter than it predicts.
  • Factors — value, size, momentum, quality, low-vol — are engineered features with historically positive premia, wide error bars, and a serious overfitting problem; judge any manager's alpha against a multi-factor regression.
  • Sharpe's arithmetic guarantees the average active dollar loses to passive after fees; about 90% of US large-cap funds trail their index over 15 years, and a 1% fee compounds into ~25% of final wealth over 30 years.

Part IV · Investing & Risk

16. Risk Management: Measuring and Taming Uncertainty

Every model in this book so far quietly assumed the future behaves. Risk management is the discipline that assumes it won't — it names the ways money vanishes, puts numbers on the danger, and buys insurance against the numbers being wrong. This is also the most realistic first job in finance for a data person, so you will meet the role by name before the section ends.

The risk taxonomy: six ways money disappears

"Risk" is not one thing. On a trading floor it is a filing cabinet of distinct failure modes, each with its own desk, its own metrics, and its own war stories. Learn the six that matter, each with one vivid example, because half of risk management is correctly diagnosing which risk just walked in the door.

Market risk is the one you already feel: prices move against you. In the COVID crash of March 2020 the S&P 500 fell 34% in 33 days — a portfolio does nothing wrong and still loses a third of its value. Credit risk is the risk a borrower doesn't pay you back; when Lehman Brothers failed in 2008, its senior bonds eventually paid holders roughly 8 cents on the dollar. Liquidity risk is subtler: you can own an asset "worth" $100 and discover that the only buyer today bids $70 — the asset is fine, the exit is broken (see Section 6 on why markets seize up).

Operational risk has nothing to do with markets and everything to do with humans and systems failing: in 1995 a single rogue trader hid £827 million of losses and destroyed Barings, a bank that had financed the Napoleonic Wars. Counterparty risk is credit risk's cousin, specific to contracts like derivatives: in 2008 AIG had written insurance on mortgage bonds and simply could not pay the claims, which is why the government stepped in. And model risk — the one this section keeps circling back to — is the risk your measurement itself is broken: your VaR model says the loss you just took was a once-in-a-billion-years event, and it says that every few years.

These categories leak into each other — that is the point. A 2008-style crisis is market risk (prices fall) triggering liquidity risk (no buyers) exposing counterparty risk (AIG can't pay) revealing model risk (the models said none of this could co-occur). The rest of this section builds the tools to measure the biggest of them, then honestly catalogs the times those tools failed.

Volatility vs drawdown: what actually breaks you

Section 15 measured risk as volatility — the standard deviation of returns — because it makes the math clean. But volatility is symmetric: it counts a lucky +30% year as exactly as "risky" as a −30% year. No investor has ever been wiped out by an upside surprise. The number that actually ends careers and firms is maximum drawdown: the deepest peak-to-trough fall you ever endure.

Drawdown matters more than volatility for two brutally practical reasons. First, the recovery math is asymmetric and merciless. A loss of L takes a gain of L ÷ (1 − L) just to get back to even — a bigger number than L, and it explodes as L grows. Second, humans and institutions don't get to wait for the average to reassert itself: a fund that draws down 40% sees redemptions, a bank that draws down 40% sees a run, and both can be forced to sell at the bottom, converting a paper loss into a permanent one.

gain needed to recover = L1 − L
L = the drawdown as a fraction · a −50% loss needs +100%; a −90% loss needs +900%

Worked example: the hole gets deeper faster than you fell

Lose 10% and you need +11.1% back — barely worse than symmetric. Lose 20%, you need +25%. Lose 50%, you need +100% — the gain must be twice the loss. Lose 90% (crypto tokens do this routinely), and you need +900% just to break even. This is why capital preservation is not timidity: avoiding the deep hole is mathematically worth more than catching the big upside, because you compound off the survivors.

Analogy: this is just geometric vs arithmetic mean

You already know why: multiplicative processes are governed by the geometric mean, and variance drags the geometric mean below the arithmetic one by roughly σ²/2. A +50%/−50% pair averages to 0% arithmetically but leaves you at 0.75 of where you started (a −25% compound outcome). Volatility isn't just uncertainty around a fixed return — it actively eats the return. Big drawdowns are the extreme case of that same log-space penalty.

Value at Risk: the number every risk desk quotes

Executives don't want a distribution; they want one number that answers "how bad can a bad day be?" That number is Value at Risk (VaR). Read it as a full sentence with three parts. "1-day 99% VaR = $1M" means: over one day, with 99% probability, losses will not exceed $1 million. Equivalently, on the worst 1 day in 100 you expect to lose at least $1M — VaR is the door, not the size of the room behind it.

That last clause is the whole subtlety. VaR is a quantile of the loss distribution: the 99th percentile loss. It tells you the threshold you breach 1% of the time; it says nothing about how catastrophic the breach is when it happens. There are three standard ways to compute it, and honest practitioners know each one's failure mode.

Method How it works Pro Con
Historical Take the last N days of actual P&L, sort them, read off the 1st percentile. No distribution assumed; captures the real fat tails you actually lived through. The future can only contain crashes your window already saw — a calm window hides the danger.
Parametric Assume returns are normal; VaR ≈ z × σ√t × V − μtV. Just plug in mean and vol. Fast, closed-form, needs only two numbers per asset. The normal assumption denies fat tails — it systematically understates extreme risk.
Monte Carlo Simulate thousands of random future paths from a chosen model, sort the outcomes. Handles options, path-dependence, any distribution you can code. Only as good as the model and correlations you feed it — garbage in, confident garbage out.

The deeper fix for VaR's blind spot is to report the size of the room, not just the door. Conditional VaR (CVaR, also called expected shortfall) answers "given that we breach VaR, how much do we lose on average?" It is the mean of the tail beyond the cutoff, so it is always at least as large as VaR and it actually feels the shape of the disaster. Regulators shifted the bank trading book from VaR to expected shortfall precisely for this reason — it is the better tail measure, and the deep dive below explains the mathematical sense in which it is coherent where VaR is not.

Real world: the risk analyst — your most realistic first job

This is you. A market-risk analyst spends the day computing exactly these numbers: pulling positions each morning, running the VaR and expected-shortfall engine, checking limits ("desk X is $2M over its VaR limit — why?"), investigating breaches, and running the backtest that compares yesterday's VaR against yesterday's real P&L. It is Python, pandas, SQL, and distributions all day — your exact stack — plus enough finance to know what the numbers mean. Model validation (stress-testing the risk models themselves) is the adjacent quant-heavy track. Section 20 maps the full path.

VaR simulator: 2,000 Monte Carlo draws

Set your portfolio, drift, and volatility, then pick a horizon and confidence. The histogram is 2,000 simulated P&L outcomes; the black line is VaR, the red bars are the CVaR tail. Watch the simulated VaR track the parametric formula — and hit Re-run to feel the sampling noise.

VaR (simulated)—
CVaR / expected shortfall—
Parametric VaR (check)—

—

Why VaR broke in 2008: the map is not the territory

In the summer of 2007, David Viniar, CFO of Goldman Sachs, told the Financial Times his firm had seen "25-standard-deviation moves, several days in a row." Take that literally and it is absurd — under a normal distribution a 25-sigma day should not occur once in the entire age of the universe, let alone several times in a week. He was not describing the market. He was describing his model. This is model risk in one sentence.

VaR failed in 2008 for three compounding reasons every risk analyst should be able to recite. First, fat tails: real returns have far more extreme days than the normal curve allows, so parametric VaR systematically under-counts the disasters. Second, correlation breakdown: the diversification from Section 15 assumed correlations stay put, but in a crisis everything falls together and correlations that were 0.3 snap to nearly 1, so risks meant to offset instead stack. Third, VaR is silent on the tail's shape: a 99% VaR says nothing about the 1% of days that actually bankrupt you.

Common pitfall: trusting the number because it has a decimal point

A VaR figure is a claim about a distribution you estimated from the recent past, using assumptions (normality, stable correlations, sufficient liquidity to exit) that are exactly the assumptions a crisis violates. It is a genuinely useful management tool in normal times and a dangerously precise-looking fiction in the tail. The correct posture is the one Alan Greenspan learned the hard way: your model of the world is not the world. Treat every risk number as a hypothesis, and reserve capital for the case where it's wrong.

The intellectually honest response is not to throw VaR away — it's to surround it. Use expected shortfall to see the tail's weight, stress-test with correlations forced to 1, and hold a capital buffer sized for the model being wrong. That buffer is what the next subsection is about, and the reason it exists is everything you just read.

Stress testing, scenarios, and why banks hold buffers

If VaR asks "what's a normal bad day?", stress testing asks "what happens in a specific catastrophe?" — and pointedly refuses to assign it a probability, because the whole failure of VaR was mis-estimating tail probabilities. A stress test picks a scenario (rates +300 basis points, equities −40%, the dollar spikes, credit spreads blow out) and simply revalues the entire book under it. Scenario analysis generalizes this to a handful of coherent, named worlds ("2008 replay," "1970s stagflation," "sovereign default").

Banks do this for real, and regulators make them. Since 2009 the US Federal Reserve has run an annual CCAR / DFAST exercise: it hands the biggest banks a hypothetical severe recession — unemployment to 10%, a housing bust, a market crash — and each bank must prove it would still have enough capital left to keep lending. Fail, and you're barred from dividends or buybacks until you raise more. The European and Bank of England tests follow the same template — the regulator forcing the industry to imagine the tail on purpose.

Which brings us to regulatory capital and the Basel Accords. A bank is a leveraged institution: it funds long-term loans with short-term deposits and holds only a thin sliver of its own equity underneath. That equity is the shock absorber that eats losses before depositors do.
